api-dev.title: Internet & How the Web Works
api-dev.description: Before writing a single line of backend code, understand the infrastructure you're building on. Learn how data travels across the internet, what happens when you hit an API endpoint, and the protocols that power the web.
topics: How the internet works — packets, routers, ISPs
DNS — domain resolution, A records, TTL
TCP vs UDP — connections, handshakes, reliability
REST architecture — resources, statelessness, uniform interface
Browsers vs API clients — curl, Postman, Insomnia
api-dev.challenge: Use curl to make GET, POST, and DELETE requests against a public API (e.g. JSONPlaceholder). Inspect headers and status codes for each.

children.title: TCP/IP & the Packet Model
children.description: Every HTTP request your API serves is a stream of TCP segments reassembled by the OS. Without understanding TCP's three-way handshake and how packets are routed across networks, you will misdiagnose connection timeouts, misread Wireshark traces, and have no mental model for why keep-alive matters for API performance.

children.title: DNS — Domain Resolution & TTL
children.description: DNS translates human-readable hostnames into IP addresses through a chain of recursive resolvers, authoritative servers, and TTL-governed caches. Misunderstanding TTL causes blue-green deployment surprises where old IPs are still cached after you rotate traffic, and skipping DNS knowledge leaves you unable to debug connectivity failures in production.

children.title: HTTP Methods, Headers & Status Codes
children.description: HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH) carry semantic contracts that browsers, proxies, and CDNs use to decide whether to cache or retry. Returning a 200 on a failed mutation or using GET for a state-changing operation breaks idempotency assumptions, causes double-submission bugs, and defeats CDN caching strategies.

children.title: HTTPS & TLS Handshake
children.description: TLS encrypts data in transit and authenticates the server via certificates, but the handshake adds a round trip that affects API latency. Not understanding TLS session resumption and HTTP/2 multiplexing leads to over-optimizing the wrong layer and shipping APIs that are either insecure or unnecessarily slow.

children.title: REST Constraints & Statelessness
children.description: REST's statelessness constraint means each request must carry all the context the server needs; storing session state on the server violates REST and breaks horizontal scaling behind a load balancer. Understanding these constraints prevents you from building so-called REST APIs that are really stateful RPC endpoints in disguise.

children.title: API Clients — curl, Postman & Insomnia
children.description: Being able to fire raw HTTP requests from the terminal with curl or from a GUI like Postman is the first debugging superpower for any API developer. Without it you are forced to write frontend code just to test your own endpoints, which slows every feedback loop and obscures whether a bug lives in the client or the server.

children.title: Browser DevTools — Network Tab
children.description: The Network tab reveals the exact headers, payloads, timings, and status codes your browser exchanges with APIs. Skipping it forces you to guess what is being sent and received, leading to hours of debugging problems that a 5-second inspection would have revealed instantly.

children.title: HTTP Caching Basics — Cache-Control & ETags
children.description: Cache-Control directives and ETags allow clients and CDNs to avoid redundant network requests. Without this knowledge your API responses will be fetched on every request even when the data hasn't changed, wasting bandwidth and origin server capacity under load.

api-dev.title: OS, Terminal & Linux Basics
api-dev.description: APIs run on servers. You need to be comfortable with the command line, file system navigation, process management, and basic Linux administration to work effectively on any backend.
topics: File system navigation — ls, cd, mkdir, rm, chmod
Text processing — grep, awk, sed, cat, pipe
Process management — ps, kill, top, background jobs
SSH — key generation, remote login, scp
Shell scripting basics — variables, loops, conditionals

resources.label: Missing Semester of CS Education — MIT

api-dev.challenge: Write a bash script that reads a log file, extracts all lines with ERROR, counts them, and outputs a summary with a timestamp.

children.title: File System Hierarchy & Permissions
children.description: Linux's filesystem hierarchy (/, /etc, /var, /tmp) determines where configuration files, logs, and runtime data live on any server your API will run on. Misunderstanding file permissions (chmod, chown) leads to misconfigured processes running as root or API servers that cannot read their own config files in production.

children.title: Pipes, Redirection & Text Processing
children.description: Shell pipes connect Unix tools into data pipelines without writing temporary files; redirection controls stdin, stdout, and stderr separately. Without these primitives you cannot efficiently parse logs, transform API response fixtures, or chain CI commands — skills you will use daily as a backend developer.

children.title: Processes, PIDs & Signals
children.description: Every Node.js server is an OS process identified by a PID and controllable via signals (SIGTERM, SIGINT, SIGHUP). Not handling SIGTERM gracefully means your API drops in-flight requests during a rolling deployment; not understanding fork/exec means you cannot reason about how your process manager spawns workers.

children.title: Environment Variables & .env Patterns
children.description: Environment variables are the standard mechanism for injecting configuration (database URLs, API keys) into processes without hard-coding secrets into source. Committing a .env file to version control is one of the most common security incidents; understanding how env vars propagate through shells, Docker, and process managers prevents it.

children.title: SSH Key Pairs & Remote Login
children.description: SSH public-key authentication replaces password-based logins with cryptographic proof of identity, making brute-force attacks impractical. Misunderstanding key pairs leads developers to copy private keys between machines or use password auth on production servers — both are serious security vulnerabilities that are completely avoidable.

children.title: tmux & Persistent Terminal Sessions
children.description: When you SSH into a server and your connection drops, any running process in that terminal is killed. tmux multiplexes a single SSH connection into persistent, detachable sessions so long-running tasks (migrations, builds) survive network interruptions — not knowing this leads to interrupted deployments and corrupted databases.

children.title: Bash Variables, Conditionals & Loops
children.description: Bash scripts automate repetitive server tasks — seeding databases, rotating logs, running health checks on deploy. Without knowing how variables, conditionals, and exit codes work you will write fragile scripts that fail silently, leaving servers in a broken half-configured state after an interrupted deployment.

children.title: cron & Scheduled Jobs
children.description: cron is the standard Unix scheduler for running periodic jobs like log rotation, cache warming, and database backups. Misconfiguring cron syntax causes jobs to run at the wrong interval or not at all, leading to missed cleanups and data drift that only surfaces when storage fills up at 3am.

api-dev.title: Node.js Fundamentals
api-dev.description: Node.js is the runtime your API will live in. Learn how it works under the hood — the event loop, async I/O, module systems — and build confidence writing server-side JavaScript.
topics: Node.js architecture — event loop, libuv, non-blocking I/O
ESM vs CommonJS — import/require, module resolution
Built-in modules — fs, path, http, events, stream
Error handling patterns — try/catch, Promise rejection
Debugging Node.js — inspector, VS Code debugger
api-dev.challenge: Build a raw HTTP server (no Express) that serves a JSON API with two endpoints — one that reads a file asynchronously and one that returns server stats.

children.title: The Event Loop & Non-blocking I/O
children.description: Node.js handles thousands of concurrent connections with a single thread by delegating I/O to libuv and processing callbacks through the event loop's phases. Writing CPU-intensive synchronous code blocks the loop and stalls every other concurrent request — understanding the loop is the difference between a scalable API and one that degrades under load.

children.title: Promises, async/await & Error Propagation
children.description: Unhandled Promise rejections crash Node.js processes in newer versions and produce silent failures in older ones. Understanding how Promise chains propagate errors and how async/await interacts with try/catch is essential — mixing callback-style and Promise-style error handling in the same codebase is the most common source of swallowed errors in Node APIs.

children.title: ESM vs CommonJS — Module Resolution
children.description: Node.js supports two module systems (CommonJS require and ESM import) that cannot be freely mixed in the same project. Misunderstanding the difference causes 'require is not defined in ESM scope' errors and interop nightmares where third-party packages ship only one format but your project uses the other.

children.title: Built-in Modules — fs, path, http, stream
children.description: Node's built-in modules handle file I/O, path manipulation, HTTP server creation, and streaming data without any dependencies. Reaching for third-party packages for tasks the standard library handles makes bundles heavier and introduces supply-chain risk; knowing what is built-in also means fewer cold-start dependencies in serverless deployments.

children.title: package.json, Semantic Versioning & Lock Files
children.description: Semantic versioning (major.minor.patch) defines a compatibility contract, but range operators in package.json (^ and ~) mean 'npm install' can pull different versions on different machines without a lock file. Committing package-lock.json or yarn.lock pins the entire dependency graph, preventing 'works on my machine' failures caused by transitive dependency upgrades.

children.title: npm Scripts & Workspaces
children.description: npm scripts are the task runner baked into every Node project — they invoke local bin executables from node_modules without global installs. Without them, every developer needs to know the exact paths to linters, test runners, and build tools; with them, a single 'npm run build' works identically in every environment including CI.

children.title: Node.js Inspector & VS Code Debugger
children.description: The Node.js inspector protocol exposes breakpoints, call stacks, and variable inspection through Chrome DevTools and VS Code. Relying solely on console.log for debugging means you cannot inspect object state at a breakpoint or step through async code, turning 10-minute debugging sessions into hours of log-spraying.

children.title: Memory Profiling & Heap Snapshots
children.description: Node.js applications can develop memory leaks where heap usage grows unboundedly until the process is killed by the OS. Without knowing how to capture heap snapshots and use the V8 memory profiler, you cannot identify whether leaks come from retained event listeners, closed-over variables, or leaked database connections.

api-dev.title: Express & REST API Design
api-dev.description: Build well-structured, maintainable REST APIs with Express. Learn routing patterns, middleware chains, request validation, and how to structure a production-grade API codebase.
topics: Express routing — router.get/post/put/delete, params, query strings
Middleware — logging (morgan), body parsing, error handling
Controller pattern — separating route logic from business logic
Input validation with Zod or Joi
REST conventions — resource naming, status codes, HATEOAS basics
api-dev.challenge: Build a Books REST API — CRUD endpoints, input validation with Zod, a custom error-handling middleware, and a request logger.

children.title: The Middleware Pipeline
children.description: Express processes requests through a sequential chain of middleware functions, each having the option to respond, modify the request/response, or call next(). Forgetting to call next() silently hangs requests; placing error-handling middleware before routes means errors are never caught — the order of middleware is the most consequential and least obvious aspect of Express architecture.

children.title: Router — Route Parameters & Query Strings
children.description: Route parameters (:id) and query strings (?page=2) are the two mechanisms for passing data through URLs, and confusing them leads to poorly designed API contracts. Misusing route params for optional filters (which belong in query strings) produces brittle URL patterns that break when filter logic changes.

children.title: Resource Naming & HTTP Verbs
children.description: Well-designed REST APIs use plural nouns for resources (/books, /users/:id/orders) and HTTP verbs to express intent, making them self-documenting and consistent. Using verbs in URLs (/getBooks, /createUser) is a REST anti-pattern that makes APIs harder to learn, document, and cache correctly.

children.title: Input Validation with Zod or Joi
children.description: Unvalidated user input is the root cause of injection attacks, type errors, and corrupted database records. Schema validation with Zod or Joi at the route boundary catches malformed requests before they reach business logic, generating descriptive error messages that clients can act on instead of cryptic 500 stack traces.

children.title: Centralized Error-Handling Middleware
children.description: Express's four-argument error handler (err, req, res, next) is the single place where all thrown errors and rejected promises should converge. Without it, unhandled errors produce inconsistent response shapes — some endpoints return HTML stack traces, others JSON, and none return useful status codes for the client to branch on.

children.title: Request Logging with morgan
children.description: Access logs record every incoming request's method, URL, status code, and response time — the minimum observability needed to diagnose production issues. Without structured access logs you cannot answer basic questions like 'which endpoint is returning 500s?' or 'what is the p99 latency for /checkout?' without replaying traffic.

children.title: Controller Pattern — Separating Routes from Logic
children.description: Placing business logic directly in route callbacks makes it untestable in isolation and leads to fat route files. The controller pattern separates route definition from logic, making individual operations unit-testable without spinning up an HTTP server and making route files readable at a glance.

children.title: API Versioning Strategies
children.description: API versioning (/v1/users vs header-based Accept: application/vnd.api.v2+json) determines how you ship breaking changes without forcing immediate client upgrades. Ignoring versioning from day one means the first breaking change forces all consumers to update simultaneously, which is impossible in third-party integrations.

api-dev.title: Databases — SQL & NoSQL
api-dev.description: Choose the right database for the job. Understand relational vs document models, write efficient queries, design schemas, and connect databases to your Node.js API.
topics: Relational model — tables, primary/foreign keys, normalization
SQL — SELECT, JOIN, GROUP BY, transactions, EXPLAIN
MongoDB with the native driver or Mongoose
Indexing strategies — B-tree, compound, partial indexes
Database design patterns — 1-N, N-M, embedding vs referencing
api-dev.challenge: Add PostgreSQL to your Books API — design a normalized schema with authors and books, write raw SQL queries, and add indexes for frequently-filtered columns.

children.title: SQL — JOINs, Transactions & EXPLAIN
children.description: JOINs are how relational databases combine data across normalized tables, but an implicit cartesian product from a missing JOIN condition returns every combination of rows — producing silently incorrect results at any scale. EXPLAIN ANALYZE reveals whether the query planner uses an index or performs a full sequential scan, which is the first thing to check before adding any index.

children.title: Schema Design — Normalization & Foreign Keys
children.description: Normalization removes data duplication to prevent update anomalies where the same fact is stored in two places and updated in only one, creating inconsistent data. Foreign key constraints enforce referential integrity at the database level; without them, orphaned child records accumulate silently and corrupt business reports.

children.title: Connection Pooling with node-postgres (pg)
children.description: Opening a new TCP connection to PostgreSQL for every query costs 20-100ms per request, which is catastrophic at any meaningful traffic level. Connection pooling reuses a fixed set of connections, but misconfiguring pool size causes either connection exhaustion (too small) or database server overload (too large).

children.title: Parameterized Queries & SQL Injection Prevention
children.description: Parameterized queries separate SQL code from user-supplied data at the protocol level, making SQL injection structurally impossible rather than relying on escaping. String-interpolating user input into SQL produces the #1 vulnerability in web applications — a single unescaped apostrophe can drop your entire database.

children.title: Document Model — Embedding vs Referencing
children.description: The most consequential MongoDB schema decision is whether to embed related data in the same document or reference it by ID across collections. Embedding optimizes for read performance but makes cross-document updates inconsistent; referencing keeps data normalized but requires multiple queries. Getting this wrong early is expensive to reverse once collections grow.

children.title: Indexes & the Aggregation Pipeline
children.description: MongoDB's aggregation pipeline ($match, $group, $lookup, $project) is a powerful multi-stage query language that replaces many SQL queries, but stages that appear before $match do not benefit from indexes — pipeline stage order directly determines whether a query scans millions of documents or a small index.

children.title: B-tree Indexes & Composite Index Column Order
children.description: A composite index on (status, created_at) is not the same as (created_at, status) — the leading column determines which queries can use the index. Putting a low-cardinality column (status with 3 values) first makes the index nearly useless for filtering by created_at alone, causing the query planner to fall back to a full table scan.

resources.label: Use the Index, Luke — Composite Indexes

children.title: Partial & Covering Indexes
children.description: A partial index includes only rows matching a condition (e.g. WHERE deleted_at IS NULL), making it far smaller and faster than a full-column index for queries that always include that filter. A covering index includes all columns a query needs in the index itself, eliminating the heap fetch entirely — both are only useful if you know your query patterns.

api-dev.title: Authentication & Security
api-dev.description: Secure your API against real-world threats. Implement auth flows, hash passwords properly, understand OAuth, and apply OWASP principles to harden your endpoints.
topics: Password hashing — bcrypt, argon2, why salts matter
JWT — structure, signing algorithms (HS256 / RS256), expiry
OAuth 2.0 flows — authorization code, client credentials
OWASP API Top 10 — injection, broken auth, rate limiting

resources.label: Web Dev Simplified — JWT Auth Tutorial

api-dev.challenge: Add full auth to your Books API — register/login with hashed passwords, JWT access + refresh tokens, protected routes, and rate limiting on the login endpoint.

children.title: bcrypt & Adaptive Hashing
children.description: bcrypt deliberately adds a configurable cost factor that makes it orders of magnitude slower than SHA-256, which is the entire point — slowing down offline brute-force attacks after a database breach. Using fast hashes (MD5, SHA-1) for passwords is a critical vulnerability; setting bcrypt's cost factor too low negates its protection as hardware gets faster.

children.title: Salt — Why Random Salts Matter
children.description: Salts make each password hash unique even when two users choose the same password, defeating precomputed rainbow table attacks. bcrypt generates a random salt automatically, but using a static or predictable salt is equivalent to not salting at all — understanding this prevents the temptation to simplify hashing code in ways that silently remove the protection.

children.title: JWT Structure, Signing & the 'alg:none' Pitfall
children.description: A JWT consists of a base64-encoded header, payload, and signature — the signature is the only part that provides security. Libraries that accept alg:none allow attackers to craft valid tokens without a secret; always explicitly specify the allowed algorithm (HS256 or RS256) rather than trusting the header the attacker controls.

children.title: Refresh Token Rotation & Token Revocation
children.description: Short-lived access tokens expire quickly, but refresh tokens are long-lived and must be rotated and invalidated on logout. Without rotation, a stolen refresh token grants indefinite access; without a revocation store (Redis denylist), you cannot invalidate tokens before expiry — meaning a compromised credential cannot be remediated without rotating the signing secret for all users.

children.title: OAuth 2.0 Flows — Authorization Code vs Client Credentials
children.description: OAuth 2.0's authorization code flow is for delegated user authorization (sign in with Google) while client credentials is for machine-to-machine API access. Using client credentials in a browser app exposes the secret to any user who opens DevTools; confusing the flows ships the wrong security model to production.

children.title: PKCE — Securing the Authorization Code Flow
children.description: PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) prevents authorization code interception attacks in public clients (mobile apps, SPAs) that cannot store a client secret. Without PKCE a malicious app registered to the same URL scheme can exchange an intercepted code for tokens — a vulnerability that became the recommended baseline for all OAuth clients in RFC 9700.

children.title: CORS — Cross-Origin Resource Sharing
children.description: CORS is enforced by browsers to prevent a malicious website from making authenticated requests to your API using a visitor's existing cookies or tokens. Using a wildcard origin (*) with credentials enabled is a security misconfiguration that allows any site to impersonate your users — understanding the preflight handshake prevents this.

children.title: helmet.js & Security Headers
children.description: HTTP security headers (X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, HSTS) tell browsers how to handle your API's responses defensively. Express by default sets none of these; without helmet.js, clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and protocol downgrade attacks remain open against any frontend consuming your API.

api-dev.title: Caching & Performance
api-dev.description: Make your API fast and resilient. Learn caching at the application and HTTP layers, use Redis for session storage and rate limiting, and optimize response times.
topics: Redis data structures — strings, hashes, sorted sets, TTL
HTTP cache headers — Cache-Control, ETag, Last-Modified
Rate limiting strategies — fixed window, sliding window, token bucket
api-dev.challenge: Add Redis caching to your Books API — cache expensive queries with a 5-minute TTL, invalidate on mutations, and implement rate limiting per IP.

children.title: Strings, Hashes & TTL Expiry
children.description: Redis strings store single values (cache entries, counters) while hashes store field-value maps (session objects, user profiles) — using strings for structured data forces serialize/deserialize on every read. TTL-based expiry is the primary mechanism for cache invalidation; omitting TTLs on cached data creates unbounded memory growth that eventually evicts everything via Redis's LRU policy.

children.title: Sorted Sets for Rate Limiting & Leaderboards
children.description: Sorted sets store members with a floating-point score, enabling O(log N) rank queries — perfect for leaderboards and sliding-window rate limiting using timestamps as scores. Implementing a rate limiter with a plain string counter and a reset key creates a race condition that allows bursts well above the configured limit during the reset window.

children.title: Cache-Control Directives & CDN Interaction
children.description: Cache-Control: max-age tells browsers how long to cache a response, while s-maxage and no-store control CDN behavior independently. Returning Cache-Control: no-cache when you mean no-store allows the browser to cache the response and revalidate it on every request — a subtle distinction that causes stale auth responses to appear after logout.

children.title: ETag & Conditional Requests
children.description: ETags are opaque identifiers for a specific version of a resource; clients send them back in If-None-Match headers and receive 304 Not Modified if the resource is unchanged. Without ETags, every poll-based client downloads the full response body even when nothing has changed, multiplying bandwidth and origin load unnecessarily.

children.title: Cache-Aside Pattern & Invalidation
children.description: Cache-aside (lazy loading) checks Redis first and falls back to the database on a miss, writing the result back to Redis. The hardest part is invalidation — a cached object must be evicted or updated whenever the underlying data changes; incorrect invalidation logic causes stale reads that are indistinguishable from fresh ones, eroding user trust.

children.title: N+1 Query Problem & the DataLoader Pattern
children.description: The N+1 problem occurs when fetching a list of N items each triggers an additional database query (e.g. fetching 100 books then querying the author for each), producing 101 queries instead of 2. DataLoader solves this by batching per-tick requests into a single query and caching results within a request, but it only helps if you understand why the N+1 happens in the first place.

children.title: gzip & Brotli Compression
children.description: Compression middleware reduces JSON response bodies by 70-90%, dramatically cutting bandwidth costs and improving response times for clients on slow connections. Not checking the Accept-Encoding header before compressing causes compressed responses to be sent to clients that cannot decompress them, producing garbled data.

children.title: Pagination — Offset vs Cursor
children.description: Offset pagination (LIMIT 20 OFFSET 200) forces the database to scan and discard the first 200 rows on every page request, making deep pages progressively slower as a table grows. Cursor-based pagination uses a WHERE id > last_seen_id index scan, which is O(1) regardless of page depth — a critical difference that only manifests at scale.

api-dev.title: Testing & API Documentation
api-dev.description: Ensure your API works correctly and is understandable to consumers. Write integration tests, automate test runs, and generate interactive documentation with OpenAPI/Swagger.
topics: Unit testing with Jest — describe, it, expect
Integration testing with Supertest — test real HTTP endpoints
Test database setup — in-memory SQLite or test MongoDB
Mocking external services with Jest spies
api-dev.challenge: Write full integration tests for your Books API — cover all CRUD endpoints, auth flows, and edge cases. Add Swagger UI with documented schemas.

children.title: Unit Testing — describe, it, expect & Mocks
children.description: Unit tests verify a single function in isolation; any external dependency (database, HTTP call) must be mocked or stubbed. Writing unit tests against real databases makes tests slow, order-dependent, and prone to failures from unrelated data — misunderstanding the boundary between unit and integration tests leads to test suites that are either too slow to run in CI or too shallow to catch real bugs.

children.title: Integration Testing with Supertest
children.description: Supertest fires real HTTP requests against your Express app in-process without binding to a port, testing the entire middleware stack, routing, and handler logic together. Skipping integration tests means a middleware ordering bug or route conflict will only be discovered in production; they are the highest-value tests for any REST API.

children.title: Test Database Setup & Teardown
children.description: Integration tests that share a database must isolate their state — either by wrapping each test in a transaction and rolling back, or by spinning up a dedicated test database per run. Failing to reset state between tests produces order-dependent test suites that pass individually but fail together, hiding real bugs and creating false confidence.

children.title: Code Coverage & What It Doesn't Guarantee
children.description: Coverage metrics (line, branch, function) measure which code was executed during tests, not whether behavior was verified. Achieving 100% coverage by asserting only that no error is thrown produces a green badge while completely untested business logic ships to production — coverage is a floor, not a ceiling.

children.title: OpenAPI 3.0 Specification — Paths, Schemas & Components
children.description: The OpenAPI spec is a machine-readable contract for your API that generates interactive documentation, client SDKs, and server stubs. Maintaining a hand-edited YAML file that drifts from the actual implementation is worse than no documentation — clients rely on it for integration and silent drift causes integration failures that are hard to trace back to the spec.

children.title: swagger-jsdoc & Swagger UI Integration
children.description: swagger-jsdoc generates an OpenAPI spec from JSDoc comments co-located with your route handlers, keeping documentation close to the code it describes and reducing the chance of drift. Without automated generation, documentation must be manually updated on every endpoint change — a process that is almost always skipped under deadline pressure.

children.title: Mocking External Services with Jest Spies
children.description: Real HTTP calls to third-party services (payment processors, email providers) in tests are slow, flaky, and can trigger real side effects. Jest's jest.spyOn and jest.mock replace module implementations with controlled doubles that return predictable responses, making tests deterministic and runnable without internet access.

children.title: Testing Auth Flows — Protected Route Coverage
children.description: Auth middleware must be tested with valid tokens, expired tokens, malformed tokens, and missing Authorization headers — each path has different expected behavior. Testing only the happy path produces an auth layer that blocks legitimate users on edge cases (clock skew, mobile apps that hold stale tokens) that were never exercised.

api-dev.title: Message Queues & Background Jobs
api-dev.description: Not every operation needs to be synchronous. Learn how to offload heavy tasks to background workers, use message queues, and implement webhooks for event-driven integrations.
topics: Webhooks — sending & receiving with signature verification
api-dev.challenge: Add an email welcome queue to your Books API — when a user registers, enqueue a job that sends a welcome email with retry logic and a dead-letter fallback.

children.title: Why Queues — Decoupling, Retries & Backpressure
children.description: Queues decouple producers from consumers, allowing them to scale independently and survive each other's outages. Without a queue, a synchronous call from your API to a slow email service stalls the HTTP response for every user registration; a queue absorbs the spike, retries on transient failures, and lets the API respond immediately.

children.title: BullMQ — Queues, Jobs & Workers
children.description: BullMQ separates the job producer (adds jobs to the queue), the worker (processes them), and the queue itself (backed by Redis). Confusing producer and worker concerns in a single process means you cannot scale them independently — the part of your system that produces jobs may not be the bottleneck, and scaling it wastes resources.

children.title: Retries, Exponential Backoff & Dead-Letter Queues
children.description: Automatic retries are essential for handling transient failures (temporary network errors, API rate limits), but retrying immediately on every failure hammers a struggling downstream service. Exponential backoff with jitter spreads retries over time; dead-letter queues capture jobs that exhaust all retries so they can be inspected and replayed rather than silently discarded.

children.title: Job Idempotency
children.description: A worker that can be safely re-executed without side effects (idempotent) can be retried on failure without risk. Non-idempotent jobs (e.g. sending an email without checking if it was already sent) produce duplicate side effects on retry, which is why designing jobs as idempotent operations from the start prevents duplicate emails, payments, and records.

children.title: Sending Webhooks — Delivery Guarantees & Retries
children.description: A webhook is an HTTP POST your server sends to a subscriber's endpoint when an event occurs. Delivery is not guaranteed — the subscriber's server may be down, so you must queue webhook dispatches with retry logic and store delivery history. Fire-and-forget webhook implementations result in missed events that subscribers never know about.

children.title: Receiving Webhooks — HMAC Signature Verification
children.description: Webhook endpoints that accept any POST request can be spoofed by an attacker who knows the endpoint URL. HMAC signature verification (computing HMAC-SHA256 of the raw body with a shared secret and comparing it to the X-Signature header) proves the payload came from the genuine sender and was not tampered with in transit.

children.title: Delayed Jobs & Cron Scheduling with BullMQ
children.description: BullMQ supports delayed jobs (run this in 30 minutes) and repeatable cron jobs (run every day at 9am) backed by Redis sorted sets. Using setInterval or setTimeout for recurring tasks in a multi-process deployment means each process independently runs the task, resulting in N executions instead of one — only a distributed lock or a queue avoids this.

children.title: Monitoring Queues — BullBoard
children.description: Queue health metrics (waiting, active, completed, failed job counts) are essential for detecting backlogs before they become user-visible outages. Without a queue dashboard you are blind to whether your workers are keeping up with producers, and you discover failures only when a downstream system reports missing data.

api-dev.title: Deployment & Production Readiness
api-dev.description: Ship your API to production with confidence. Containerize with Docker, automate deployments with CI/CD, and set up logging and monitoring so you can observe your running service.
topics: Docker Compose — local multi-service dev (app + db + redis)
Railway / Render / Fly.io — zero-config cloud deployment
GitHub Actions CI — lint, test, build on every push
Structured logging with Pino or Winston
APM basics — error tracking with Sentry

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana — Docker for Beginners

api-dev.challenge: Dockerize your Books API with a multi-stage Dockerfile, write a docker-compose.yml for local dev (app + postgres + redis), and deploy to Railway with a GitHub Actions CI pipeline.

children.title: Dockerfile — Layers, Multi-stage Builds & .dockerignore
children.description: Each RUN, COPY, and ADD instruction creates an immutable layer; layer order determines cache efficiency. Placing npm install before COPY . means dependency installation is only re-run when package.json changes. Multi-stage builds keep the final image small by discarding build tools; including node_modules in the image via a missing .dockerignore can double image size and expose dev dependencies in production.

children.title: Docker Compose — Local Multi-service Development
children.description: Docker Compose orchestrates multiple containers (API, Postgres, Redis) with a single command and a declarative YAML file, matching the topology of your production environment. Without it, developers manually start services in the right order and forget to stop them — leading to port conflicts, stale data, and subtle 'works on my machine' bugs.

children.title: GitHub Actions — Workflow Syntax & Triggers
children.description: A GitHub Actions workflow is a YAML file that runs jobs on GitHub-hosted runners triggered by push, pull_request, or schedule events. Misunderstanding workflow triggers leads to CI that only runs on main (missing PR feedback), or that runs on every push to every branch (wasting minutes and money on in-progress commits).

children.title: CI Pipeline — Lint, Test & Build Gates
children.description: A CI pipeline that runs lint, tests, and a Docker build on every PR makes the main branch a green baseline that can always be deployed. Merging without CI gates allows broken code to accumulate, making every hotfix more expensive because the codebase is always partially broken and you cannot distinguish new failures from existing ones.

children.title: Structured Logging with Pino or Winston
children.description: Structured logs emit JSON objects with consistent fields (level, timestamp, requestId, message) instead of free-form strings. Log aggregation platforms (Datadog, Loki, CloudWatch) can index and query JSON fields; querying free-text logs for all errors from a specific userId in a time range is nearly impossible without structure.

children.title: Health Check Endpoints & Graceful Shutdown
children.description: A /health endpoint lets load balancers and container orchestrators verify the application is ready to serve traffic before routing requests to it. A graceful shutdown handler (listening to SIGTERM) stops accepting new connections, waits for in-flight requests to complete, then closes database pools — without it, rolling deployments drop requests that were mid-processing when the container was killed.

children.title: Railway & Render — Zero-config Cloud Deployment
children.description: Railway and Render detect Dockerfiles or package.json build scripts and deploy to managed infrastructure with automatic TLS, environment variable management, and private networking. Understanding what these platforms abstract away (process supervision, health checks, zero-downtime deploys) prevents you from being helpless when something breaks that the platform usually handles.

children.title: Error Tracking with Sentry
children.description: Sentry captures unhandled exceptions with full stack traces, breadcrumbs, and request context, and groups them by root cause across all instances of your app. Without error tracking, production exceptions are invisible until a user reports them; by then the context (request payload, user ID, line number) is gone from ephemeral container logs.

db-engineer.title: Database Fundamentals
db-engineer.description: Start with what a database is and why it matters. Learn the core concepts shared by every relational system before diving into a specific engine.
db-engineer.challenge: Design a normalized schema for a small e-commerce store on paper, then create the tables.

children.title: Tables, Keys & Relationships
children.description: The relational model is not just a storage format — it is a mathematical contract. Misunderstanding primary keys leads to duplicate rows that violate entity integrity, and confusing foreign keys with application-level checks means referential integrity is never enforced at the database layer, producing orphaned rows that silently corrupt reports.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke — The Relational Model

children.title: Data Types & Schema Contracts
children.description: Choosing the wrong data type (e.g., storing dates as VARCHAR) bypasses all database-level validation and forces every query to cast values, making indexes useless. Correctly typed columns let the engine enforce domain constraints for free and enable operators and functions that only work on native types.

children.title: ACID Properties Explained
children.description: ACID is the promise your database makes about what happens when things go wrong. Misunderstanding atomicity leads to half-written multi-step operations that leave data in an inconsistent state; misunderstanding isolation leads to race conditions where two concurrent transactions read stale data and both think they are the winner.

children.title: SQL vs NoSQL — When Each Fits
children.description: Choosing the wrong storage paradigm early means either fighting the database's data model for years or doing a costly migration. Relational databases enforce schema and relationships at the storage layer; document databases trade consistency guarantees for schema flexibility and horizontal scale — picking based on hype rather than access patterns causes real pain.

resources.label: MongoDB Docs — SQL to MongoDB Mapping
CMU Intro to Database Systems — Lecture 1

children.title: How Databases Execute Queries
children.description: A query goes through parsing, planning, and execution before returning rows. Not knowing this means you blame the network when the query planner picks a bad plan, or you add indexes in the wrong place because you do not understand which stage is the bottleneck. The EXPLAIN command only makes sense with this mental model in place.

resources.label: PostgreSQL Docs — Overview of Query Processing
Use The Index, Luke — Anatomy of an Index

children.title: Connection Management
children.description: Database connections are expensive OS-level resources — each one holds memory, an open socket, and backend process state. Applications that open a new connection per query exhaust the connection limit under load, causing all queries to fail. Connection pooling (PgBouncer, ProxySQL) is not optional in production; it is the baseline.

children.title: Installing & Connecting to PostgreSQL
children.description: Understanding the psql client and pg_hba.conf authentication file is critical because misconfiguring host-based authentication is the most common reason developers cannot connect and waste hours debugging. Knowing what pg_hba.conf controls lets you distinguish an authentication error from a network error immediately.

children.title: MySQL Workbench & Basic Administration
children.description: A GUI tool like MySQL Workbench makes schema visualization and query profiling accessible during learning, but relying on it without understanding the SQL it generates means you cannot work when only a terminal is available. Learn the underlying SQL first; use the GUI as a lens, not a crutch.

db-engineer.title: SQL Mastery
db-engineer.description: Become fluent in SQL — the language you'll use every day. Move from basic selects to complex analytical queries.
db-engineer.challenge: Answer 10 analytical business questions about your store using window functions and CTEs.

children.title: JOINs — How They Actually Work
children.description: JOINs are not just syntax — each type (INNER, LEFT, CROSS) corresponds to a different set operation with different row-multiplying behavior. Confusing LEFT and INNER JOIN when NULLs are involved silently drops rows from result sets, producing reports that look correct but are missing data. Understanding the Cartesian product that underlies all joins prevents accidental row explosions.

children.title: Aggregate Functions & GROUP BY
children.description: GROUP BY collapses rows, and every column in the SELECT that is not inside an aggregate must appear in the GROUP BY — violating this rule either errors out or (in MySQL's lenient mode) silently picks an arbitrary value from the group, a major source of incorrect analytics. Understanding this makes the HAVING clause logical rather than magical.

children.title: CTEs & Subqueries
children.description: A CTE (WITH clause) gives a subquery a name, making complex queries readable and debuggable — but in PostgreSQL prior to v12, CTEs were optimization fences that prevented the planner from pushing predicates into them, causing severe performance regressions. Knowing this historical behavior prevents mysterious slowdowns when deploying on older engines.

children.title: Window Functions
children.description: Window functions compute a value across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing them — unlike GROUP BY. Misunderstanding the PARTITION BY and ORDER BY clauses inside OVER() produces rolling totals that reset at the wrong boundaries or ranks that are computed over the entire table instead of per-category, yielding silently wrong analytics.

children.title: NULL Handling & Three-Valued Logic
children.description: SQL uses three-valued logic — TRUE, FALSE, and UNKNOWN — because NULL represents the absence of a value. Writing col != 'x' does not match rows where col IS NULL, which silently excludes data. Every WHERE clause that can encounter NULLs must use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL, and misunderstanding this is one of the top sources of incorrect query results.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke — Null in the Index

children.title: Set Operations — UNION, INTERSECT, EXCEPT
children.description: UNION deduplicates rows while UNION ALL does not — using UNION when you mean UNION ALL causes a hidden sort/hash operation on large result sets that can be orders of magnitude slower. Understanding which set operation to use and when deduplication is needed prevents both correctness bugs and unexpected performance problems.

children.title: Transactions & Savepoints in SQL
children.description: Wrapping multi-step writes in an explicit transaction is the only way to guarantee that either all changes commit or none do. Forgetting to commit leaves locks held until the connection closes, blocking other queries. Savepoints enable partial rollbacks within a transaction, a pattern critical for complex data pipelines that need fine-grained error recovery.

children.title: CASE Expressions & Conditional Logic
children.description: CASE is the SQL equivalent of if/else and lets you pivot, categorize, and transform data inside a single query without application-side post-processing. Not knowing CASE forces developers to pull full result sets into application memory just to add a derived column, which destroys performance for large datasets that the database could filter first.

db-engineer.title: Relational Modeling & Normalization
db-engineer.description: Design schemas that scale and stay consistent. Master normalization, relationships and constraints.
db-engineer.challenge: Normalize a messy spreadsheet of orders into a clean relational schema with proper constraints.

children.title: 1NF Through 3NF — What They Actually Prevent
children.description: Normal forms are not bureaucratic rules — each form eliminates a specific class of update anomaly. A table not in 2NF has partial dependencies that cause the same fact to be stored in multiple rows, so updating one copy without the others creates contradictory data. Understanding what anomaly each normal form prevents lets you normalize deliberately rather than by rote.

children.title: BCNF & When to Stop Normalizing
children.description: Boyce-Codd Normal Form eliminates every remaining functional dependency anomaly, but a fully normalized schema for a high-read workload can require so many JOINs that query performance is unacceptable. Knowing when to denormalize — and documenting the trade-off — is a design skill; doing it without understanding the anomalies you are reintroducing is how data corruption starts.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke — Clustering & Covers

children.title: Entity-Relationship Modeling
children.description: An ER diagram is the contract between business requirements and the physical schema. Skipping ER modeling and going straight to tables means cardinalities are guessed rather than verified — a many-to-many modeled as one-to-many silently loses data from day one. The diagram forces you to resolve ambiguity before writing DDL.

children.title: CHECK, UNIQUE & NOT NULL Constraints
children.description: Constraints are database-enforced rules that no application code can bypass — not even direct psql access or a migration script. Relying solely on application validation means a buggy deploy, a manual data fix, or a second service writing to the same table can silently violate invariants. Constraints are the last line of data integrity defense.

children.title: Surrogate vs Natural Keys
children.description: A natural key (email, SSN) encodes a real-world fact that can change — when it does, every foreign key referencing it must cascade, causing wide table updates and cache invalidations. Surrogate keys (auto-increment IDs, UUIDs) are stable by design but require a separate UNIQUE constraint on the natural key to prevent duplicate business entities.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke — Primary Keys

children.title: Cascading Actions & Referential Integrity
children.description: ON DELETE CASCADE and ON DELETE SET NULL have very different consequences — CASCADE silently deletes child rows across the entire tree, which can be correct behavior or catastrophic data loss depending on the relationship semantics. Not specifying cascade behavior defaults to RESTRICT, which is safe but blocks deletes until application code removes children first.

children.title: Versioned Migrations with Tools
children.description: Manually running SQL scripts against a shared database means no team member knows which scripts have been applied and in what order — drift between environments causes bugs that reproduce only in production. Migration tools (Flyway, Liquibase, Alembic) give every schema change a version, a checksum, and an idempotent application record that eliminates this drift.

children.title: Zero-Downtime Schema Changes
children.description: Adding a NOT NULL column with a DEFAULT takes an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock in older PostgreSQL versions, blocking all reads and writes for the duration of the table rewrite. On large production tables this causes visible downtime. Knowing which DDL operations acquire which lock types — and the expand/contract pattern for migrations — is essential for shipping schema changes safely.

db-engineer.title: PostgreSQL Deep Dive
db-engineer.description: Master the world's most advanced open-source database. Explore the features that make PostgreSQL a power tool.
db-engineer.challenge: Build a search feature over your store's products using full-text search and JSONB attributes.

children.title: JSONB — Semi-Structured Data in Postgres
children.description: JSONB stores JSON in a binary decomposed format that supports indexing via GIN, letting you query nested attributes without a schema change. Using the text-based json type instead means no operator indexing and repeated parsing on every access. Misunderstanding the difference between json and jsonb — or when to use a proper column instead of either — leads to schemas that are neither relational nor document-oriented and get the worst of both worlds.

children.title: Views & Materialized Views
children.description: A regular view is just a stored query — it re-executes on every reference, offering no performance benefit. A materialized view caches the result and must be refreshed explicitly or on a schedule; forgetting to refresh it means the application reads stale data silently. Understanding when to use each prevents both performance problems and correctness bugs in reporting pipelines.

children.title: Stored Procedures & Functions in PL/pgSQL
children.description: PL/pgSQL runs inside the database server, eliminating round-trips for complex multi-step logic. However, untested stored procedures become black boxes that are hard to version-control and even harder to debug — a function with a bug that silently swallows errors can corrupt data for months. Understanding PL/pgSQL's exception handling and transaction semantics is non-negotiable before writing production logic there.

children.title: Triggers — Power & Pitfalls
children.description: Triggers fire automatically on data changes and are ideal for audit logs and derived-column maintenance, but they make data behavior non-obvious — a developer inserting a row has no idea a trigger is modifying three other tables unless they know to look. Over-relying on triggers creates invisible coupling that makes debugging nightmarish and testing almost impossible.

children.title: PostgreSQL Extensions — PostGIS, pg_trgm
children.description: Extensions turn PostgreSQL into a specialized engine for geospatial data (PostGIS), fuzzy text search (pg_trgm), or time-series workloads (TimescaleDB). Using LIKE '%search%' for fuzzy search instead of pg_trgm performs a sequential scan of every row — pg_trgm with a GIN index makes the same query orders of magnitude faster with a two-line schema change.

children.title: Full-Text Search with tsvector & tsquery
children.description: PostgreSQL's native full-text search uses tsvector (a normalized token list) and tsquery (a search expression) to enable relevance-ranked, stemmed keyword search without Elasticsearch. Storing a pre-computed tsvector column updated by a trigger is crucial — computing it at query time on millions of rows makes full-text search 100× slower than it needs to be.

children.title: psql Meta-Commands & Productivity
children.description: psql's backslash meta-commands (\d, \dt, \di, \x, \copy) are not SQL — they are client-side shortcuts that avoid writing repetitive catalog queries. Not knowing them means wasting time writing information_schema queries for tasks that take one keystroke, and it means you cannot navigate a production database efficiently under pressure.

children.title: VACUUM, ANALYZE & Autovacuum
children.description: PostgreSQL uses MVCC for concurrency — deleted and updated rows are not immediately removed but marked as dead tuples. Without VACUUM, dead tuples accumulate, bloating tables and slowing sequential scans. ANALYZE updates planner statistics; outdated statistics cause the query planner to choose catastrophically bad execution plans for queries on tables that have grown significantly since the last analysis.

db-engineer.title: MySQL & Operational Databases
db-engineer.description: Learn MySQL, the backbone of countless production apps. Understand its storage engines and operational behavior.
db-engineer.challenge: Migrate a schema to MySQL and demonstrate transaction isolation behavior with concurrent sessions.

children.title: InnoDB Architecture — Buffer Pool & Redo Log
children.description: InnoDB's buffer pool caches data and index pages in memory — sizing it too small means every query hits disk; too large starves the OS page cache. The redo log provides crash recovery, but setting innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit=2 trades durability for performance in a way that loses up to one second of committed transactions on a crash. Understanding these two knobs is the foundation of MySQL performance tuning.

children.title: Transaction Isolation Levels in MySQL
children.description: MySQL's default isolation level is REPEATABLE READ, which prevents non-repeatable reads but allows phantom reads in some engines. InnoDB uses gap locks to prevent phantoms in REPEATABLE READ, which is stronger than the SQL standard requires and can cause unexpected deadlocks for range queries that are not obvious from the SQL alone. Switching to READ COMMITTED eliminates gap locks at the cost of stronger phantom exposure.

children.title: Key Behavioral Differences
children.description: MySQL in its default configuration silently truncates strings that exceed column length and accepts invalid dates like '2024-02-30', while PostgreSQL rejects them with an error. Running MySQL with strict mode off on development and on in production — or migrating queries between the two engines — exposes these silent corruption behaviors in the worst possible moment.

children.title: Deadlocks & How to Detect Them
children.description: A deadlock occurs when two transactions each hold a lock the other needs, causing both to wait forever. InnoDB detects deadlocks automatically and kills the transaction with the least work done, but the application must handle the rollback and retry. Not instrumenting for deadlocks means they appear as random application errors with no hint of the root cause.

children.title: my.cnf — Key Configuration Variables
children.description: MySQL ships with conservative defaults designed to run on small VMs — innodb_buffer_pool_size defaults to 128MB and max_connections to 151. Running a production workload on defaults means the database is bottlenecked by configuration, not hardware. Knowing which variables to tune first (buffer pool, redo log size, query cache disable) has an immediate performance impact.

children.title: Slow Query Log & Performance Schema
children.description: The slow query log records every query exceeding long_query_time and is the fastest way to find the queries worth optimizing. Performance Schema provides nanosecond-resolution instrumentation of mutex waits, I/O, and memory allocation — but enabling all instruments has measurable overhead, so knowing which instruments are cheap enough to leave on in production is necessary.

children.title: MySQL Stored Procedures
children.description: MySQL stored procedures run inside the server and can encapsulate complex multi-statement business logic with IN/OUT parameters, but they execute in a single-threaded interpreter with limited debugging support. Putting critical business logic in stored procedures that are not version-controlled creates a shadow codebase that diverges from the application repository and is invisible to code review.

children.title: MySQL Event Scheduler
children.description: The MySQL Event Scheduler runs SQL on a schedule directly in the database server, eliminating the need for an external cron job for simple periodic tasks like data archiving or statistics aggregation. However, events have no retry logic and no observable execution log by default — a failed event is silent unless ERROR logging is enabled and monitored.

db-engineer.title: NoSQL with MongoDB
db-engineer.description: Add document databases to your toolkit. Learn when NoSQL fits and how to model data in MongoDB.
db-engineer.challenge: Model a content platform in MongoDB and write an aggregation pipeline for a trending feed.

children.title: BSON, Documents & Collections
children.description: MongoDB stores documents in BSON, a binary superset of JSON that adds types like Date, ObjectId, and Decimal128 that plain JSON cannot represent. Using strings for dates and IDs in BSON documents bypasses these native types and breaks date range queries, sort order, and referential patterns — all silently, with no schema enforcement to catch the mistake.

children.title: Embedding vs Referencing
children.description: The central MongoDB data modeling decision is whether to embed related data inside one document or store it in a separate collection with a reference. Embedding makes reads fast and atomic but leads to unbounded document growth when the embedded array can grow indefinitely — MongoDB has a 16MB document size limit. Referencing keeps documents small but requires application-side joins ($lookup) that cannot use the same indexes as a relational JOIN.

children.title: Aggregation Pipeline Stages
children.description: The aggregation pipeline processes documents through ordered stages — $match, $group, $project, $sort, $limit. Putting $match after $group instead of before it means the full collection is scanned and grouped before filtering, which is catastrophically slower. Stage order is not arbitrary; it determines whether indexes can be used and how much data flows through each stage.

children.title: $lookup — Joins in MongoDB
children.description: $lookup performs a left outer join between two collections and is the closest MongoDB equivalent to a SQL JOIN. Unlike relational JOINs, $lookup always loads both collections' matching documents into memory and cannot leverage a compound index that spans both collections. Overusing $lookup on hot paths is a common performance anti-pattern in MongoDB applications that started as relational databases.

children.title: MongoDB Indexes — Types & Tradeoffs
children.description: MongoDB supports single-field, compound, multikey (array), text, geospatial, and wildcard indexes. A multikey index on an array field creates one index entry per array element — indexing a large array field can cause index bloat that is many times the size of the data itself. Not understanding multikey index behavior leads to surprising disk usage and slow write performance on array-heavy documents.

children.title: Schema Design Patterns (Bucket, Outlier)
children.description: MongoDB schema design patterns like Bucket (grouping time-series data into documents) and Outlier (handling documents that would otherwise violate the normal embedding rule) solve specific performance problems that a naive document model creates at scale. Applying the wrong pattern — or none at all — results in either unbounded document growth or a reference-heavy design that loses MongoDB's read locality advantage.

children.title: Atlas Search & Vector Search
children.description: MongoDB Atlas Search is built on Lucene and provides full-text, fuzzy, autocomplete, and vector search without needing a separate Elasticsearch cluster. Not knowing that Atlas Search indexes are asynchronously synced — not transactionally consistent with write operations — can cause recently inserted documents to be invisible to search queries for seconds to minutes.

children.title: Write Concern & Read Preference
children.description: Write concern controls how many replica set members must acknowledge a write before the driver returns success — w:1 acknowledges from the primary only, w:majority waits for a quorum. Using w:0 (fire-and-forget) in a financial context silently loses writes on primary failure. Read preference controls which replica services reads; using secondaryPreferred without understanding replication lag means reads can return stale data.

db-engineer.title: Caching & Redis
db-engineer.description: Speed up systems with in-memory data. Use Redis for caching, sessions, queues and real-time features.
db-engineer.challenge: Add a Redis cache-aside layer and a leaderboard to your app, with sensible TTLs and invalidation.

children.title: Strings, Hashes & Sorted Sets
children.description: Redis is not a key-value store for strings only — choosing the right data structure determines both performance and memory usage by an order of magnitude. Storing a user session as a JSON string means every partial update requires fetching, deserializing, modifying, and re-serializing the entire blob; storing it as a Hash lets HSET update a single field atomically with O(1) complexity.

children.title: TTL, Expiration & Eviction Policies
children.description: Redis TTL is set per-key with EXPIRE or EXPIREAT and the key is deleted lazily on next access plus a background sweep. Using PERSIST instead of re-setting a TTL on cache refresh means keys accumulate forever, filling memory and triggering the eviction policy. Understanding the difference between volatile-lru (evict only keys with TTL), allkeys-lru, and noeviction (reject writes) prevents cache stampedes and OOM errors.

children.title: Cache-Aside, Write-Through & Write-Behind
children.description: In cache-aside the application checks Redis before the database and populates the cache on miss — a cache miss under high concurrency causes a thundering herd where dozens of requests all query the database simultaneously. Write-through keeps cache and database in sync on every write but doubles write latency. Write-behind (write-back) batches database writes but risks data loss if Redis crashes before the flush.

children.title: Cache Invalidation Strategies
children.description: Cache invalidation — knowing when to delete or update a cached value — is the hardest problem in caching. Time-based expiry (TTL) is simple but serves stale data up to the TTL window. Event-driven invalidation (deleting the key on write) is more correct but requires every write path to know which cache keys to purge, creating hidden coupling. Getting this wrong produces data inconsistencies that are reported as bugs but are actually architectural issues.

children.title: Pub/Sub — Real-Time Messaging
children.description: Redis Pub/Sub delivers messages to all currently subscribed clients with at-most-once semantics — messages published when no subscriber is connected are lost permanently. If your use case requires at-least-once delivery or the ability for consumers to replay missed messages, Redis Streams (XADD/XREAD) is the correct primitive, not Pub/Sub. Using Pub/Sub for durable messaging silently drops messages during deployments.

children.title: Redis Streams — Durable Message Queues
children.description: Redis Streams support consumer groups where multiple consumers share a stream and each message is delivered to exactly one consumer in the group, with explicit acknowledgment (XACK) required before the message leaves the pending-entries list. Not acknowledging processed messages causes the pending list to grow unboundedly, increasing memory usage and causing XAUTOCLAIM overhead on recovery.

children.title: RDB Snapshots vs AOF Persistence
children.description: RDB saves a point-in-time snapshot periodically — if Redis crashes between snapshots, all writes since the last snapshot are lost. AOF logs every write command and can be configured for fsync-per-second (risk losing one second of data) or fsync-always (durable but slow). Using Redis as a primary data store without understanding these persistence modes results in data loss that surprises teams who thought Redis was as durable as PostgreSQL.

children.title: Rate Limiting with Redis
children.description: Redis is the standard backend for distributed rate limiting because its atomic operations (INCR + EXPIRE, or the Lua-script-based sliding window) work correctly across multiple application servers where an in-process counter would not. A fixed-window counter implemented without atomicity allows double the allowed requests at the window boundary — a well-known race condition that only Redis's single-threaded model prevents cleanly.

db-engineer.title: Indexing & Query Optimization
db-engineer.description: Make databases fast. Understand indexes deeply and learn to diagnose and fix slow queries.
db-engineer.challenge: Profile a slow query, add the right index, and cut its execution time using EXPLAIN ANALYZE.

children.title: B-Tree Index Structure
children.description: PostgreSQL's default index is a B-tree — a balanced tree where leaf nodes hold sorted index entries pointing to heap rows. The sort order is what makes range queries (>, <, BETWEEN) and ORDER BY fast; without understanding that B-trees are sorted, you will add an index to a column used only in equality checks and miss that a composite index with a different column order would serve both equality and range queries.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke — Anatomy of an Index

children.title: GIN, BRIN & Hash Indexes
children.description: GIN (Generalized Inverted Index) indexes every element inside an array or JSONB document and is the only index type that makes array and full-text containment queries fast. BRIN (Block Range Index) stores min/max values per block and is tiny and fast for naturally ordered data like timestamps, but completely useless for random-access lookups. Applying B-tree to array columns or GIN to sequential timestamp ranges wastes resources and misses the speedup.

children.title: Composite Indexes & Column Order
children.description: A composite index on (a, b) supports queries on a alone and on (a, b) together, but not on b alone — the leftmost prefix rule. Reversing the column order because b has higher cardinality is a common mistake; the query planner cares about which columns appear in the WHERE clause first, not cardinality. A poorly ordered composite index is never used and wastes write overhead.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke — Concatenated Indexes

children.title: Partial Indexes
children.description: A partial index only indexes rows that satisfy a WHERE predicate, making it smaller and faster than a full-table index. Indexing all rows of a status column where 99% are 'completed' wastes index space and slows writes; a partial index on WHERE status = 'pending' covers the active 1% that queries actually filter on. Not knowing about partial indexes means leaving obvious wins on the table.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke — Partial Indexes

children.title: Reading EXPLAIN ANALYZE Output
children.description: EXPLAIN ANALYZE shows the actual query plan with real row counts and timing at each node. The gap between the planner's estimated rows and actual rows is the single most diagnostic number — when actual rows are 1000× the estimate, planner statistics are stale and the chosen plan is probably wrong. Not being able to read this output means you optimize by guessing instead of by evidence.

children.title: Seq Scan vs Index Scan vs Index-Only Scan
children.description: The planner chooses a sequential scan even when an index exists if it estimates that the query returns more than ~5-10% of the table — reading the full table in one pass is cheaper than random index lookups for large fractions. An index-only scan is fastest because it reads the index without touching heap pages, but it only works when all selected columns are in the index. Understanding these three scan types tells you when an index will or will not be used.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke — Index-Only Scans

children.title: The N+1 Query Problem
children.description: An N+1 query happens when code fetches a list of N records and then issues one additional query per record to load a related field — what looks like two lines of code becomes 1001 database round-trips for a 1000-row list. The fix is a JOIN or a second query with an IN clause that batches all IDs. Not recognizing N+1 in ORM-generated SQL is why applications that work fine in development collapse in production under real data volumes.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke — Select N+1 Problem

children.title: PgBouncer & Connection Pooling
children.description: PostgreSQL spawns a backend process per connection — at 500 connections the server is spending more time scheduling processes than executing queries. PgBouncer operates in transaction-pooling mode where a physical connection is only held for the duration of a single transaction and returned to the pool, allowing thousands of application connections to share tens of database connections. Not using a pooler is the most common cause of PostgreSQL connection exhaustion.

db-engineer.title: Replication, Sharding & HA
db-engineer.description: Scale databases beyond one machine. Learn replication, partitioning and high-availability patterns.
db-engineer.challenge: Set up streaming replication with a read replica and route read queries to it.

children.title: Streaming Replication in PostgreSQL
children.description: PostgreSQL streaming replication sends WAL (Write-Ahead Log) records from the primary to standbys in real time, keeping them a few milliseconds behind. Not monitoring replication lag means a standby promoted during a failover might be seconds behind, causing data loss. Not understanding the distinction between synchronous and asynchronous replication means choosing the wrong trade-off between performance and durability.

children.title: MongoDB Replica Sets
children.description: A MongoDB replica set is a group of mongod instances that maintain the same dataset — one primary and one or more secondaries. Writes go to the primary; secondaries replicate asynchronously via the oplog. Connecting directly to a standalone instance instead of the replica set URI means your driver never retries on failover, so a primary election causes application errors instead of a brief pause.

children.title: Sharding Strategy — Range vs Hash
children.description: A range shard key (e.g., timestamp) lets you prune shards for time-range queries but creates hot shards as all recent writes go to the latest range shard. A hash shard key distributes writes evenly but makes range queries scatter across all shards. Choosing the shard key is irreversible without resharding the entire collection — getting it wrong at launch causes years of imbalanced load.

children.title: PostgreSQL Table Partitioning
children.description: Table partitioning splits a logical table into physical child tables by a partition key (range, list, or hash). The query planner can prune partitions — a query with WHERE created_at > '2024-01-01' only scans relevant monthly partitions instead of a billion-row table. Not specifying the partition key in queries defeats pruning entirely, making partitioning add overhead without the benefit.

children.title: CAP Theorem & Consistency Models
children.description: CAP theorem states that a distributed system can guarantee at most two of Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance. Most database failures are network partitions — understanding that PostgreSQL synchronous replication sacrifices availability (primary blocks on replica ACK) while asynchronous replication sacrifices consistency (replica might be behind) lets you make an informed trade-off rather than accepting defaults blindly.

children.title: Patroni & Automatic Failover for PostgreSQL
children.description: Patroni is the de-facto standard for PostgreSQL HA — it uses a distributed configuration store (etcd/Consul/ZooKeeper) as a quorum system to elect a new primary and reconfigure standbys automatically when the primary fails. Without a tool like Patroni, a primary failure requires manual promotion and DNS changes, causing minutes of downtime. Understanding how Patroni handles split-brain (two nodes think they are primary) prevents the worst possible failure mode.

children.title: Redis Sentinel vs Redis Cluster
children.description: Redis Sentinel monitors a primary and promotes a replica on failure — it provides HA but all data lives on one shard. Redis Cluster shards data across 16384 hash slots spread across multiple nodes, providing both HA and horizontal write scale. Using Sentinel when you need to exceed one node's memory means a hard migration to Cluster later; using Cluster when you only need HA adds operational complexity for no benefit.

children.title: Read Replicas & Load Balancing
children.description: Read replicas serve SELECT queries from a copy of the data, reducing load on the primary. The catch is replication lag — a read immediately after a write may return stale data from a replica that has not yet applied the change. Not tracking replication lag and not routing reads that require fresh data back to the primary produces race conditions that are reported as random bugs.

db-engineer.title: Backup, Security & Administration
db-engineer.description: Operate databases responsibly. Master backups, security hardening and monitoring for production.
db-engineer.challenge: Build a backup + restore runbook and apply least-privilege roles to your production schema.

children.title: pg_dump, pg_basebackup & Point-in-Time Recovery
children.description: pg_dump takes a logical snapshot of a database that can be restored to any PostgreSQL version, but it does not capture the state between dumps — if a database is corrupted at 2:47 PM and the last dump was at midnight, you lose nearly 15 hours of data. pg_basebackup plus continuous WAL archiving enables point-in-time recovery (PITR) to any second within the archive window, which is the only backup strategy acceptable for production databases with significant write volume.

children.title: Testing Restores — The Missing Step
children.description: An untested backup is not a backup — it is a hope. Backup files can be corrupt, incomplete, or target a different PostgreSQL major version whose pg_restore rejects them. Regularly restoring to a staging environment and verifying row counts is the only way to know your recovery procedure works before you need it under pressure. Most teams discover their backups are broken during an actual disaster.

children.title: PostgreSQL Roles & Least Privilege
children.description: PostgreSQL uses roles as the unified concept for both users and groups — a role can log in (user) or be granted to another role (group). Connecting every application with a superuser role means a SQL injection vulnerability or a runaway migration can DROP any database. Least-privilege means the application role has SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on only the tables it needs, and no DDL rights at all.

children.title: Row-Level Security (RLS)
children.description: Row-Level Security attaches a policy to a table that the database evaluates invisibly on every query — a SELECT from a multi-tenant table automatically returns only the current user's rows without any WHERE clause in the application. Not using RLS in a multi-tenant schema means a single missing WHERE tenant_id = $1 in any query exposes all tenants' data, and code review is the only guard against that mistake.

children.title: Encryption at Rest & in Transit
children.description: SSL/TLS in transit prevents credentials and query results from being readable on the network — pg_hba.conf's hostssl forces TLS for all TCP connections. Encryption at rest protects database files on disk if storage is stolen or a cloud snapshot is misconfigured as public, but it does not protect against a compromised database user — access control and encryption are complementary, not substitutes.

children.title: pgAudit — Audit Logging
children.description: pgAudit extends PostgreSQL's logging to record every statement executed by every role, which is required for SOC 2, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA compliance. Without it, the only record of a data breach or a destructive query is whatever the application logs captured — which is typically nothing for direct database access. Enabling pgAudit on a busy database with verbose settings can fill disk in minutes, so selective session/object-level auditing must be configured carefully.

resources.label: PostgreSQL Docs — Error Reporting and Logging

children.title: pg_stat_* Views — Built-in Monitoring
children.description: PostgreSQL ships with a statistics collector that exposes pg_stat_activity (current connections and queries), pg_stat_user_tables (sequential vs index scan counts, dead tuple counts), and pg_stat_bgwriter (checkpoint frequency). Not querying these views means you are flying blind — a table with a ballooning dead tuple count and zero autovacuum runs is invisible until queries start timing out.
