children.title: Production Migrations — Zero-Downtime Patterns
children.description: Running a migration that holds an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on a busy table blocks all reads and writes for its duration — even a few seconds is user-visible. The expand/contract pattern (add nullable column, backfill in batches, add constraint, remove old column) keeps the application running throughout. Not understanding lock levels for common DDL operations means deploying schema changes during maintenance windows unnecessarily or causing unplanned outages.

systems-dev.title: Systems Programming Foundations
systems-dev.description: Systems programming starts close to the metal. Understand how programs are built, how memory is laid out, and how code becomes machine instructions.

resources.label: Ben Eater — How a CPU works

systems-dev.challenge: Write a C program that manipulates raw bytes and explain its memory layout with a diagram.

children.title: The Compilation Pipeline
children.description: Source code passes through preprocessing, compilation, assembly and linking before becoming an executable. Misunderstanding this pipeline means you cannot diagnose linker errors, strip dead code, or reason about symbol visibility — all critical when building shared libraries or embedded firmware.

children.title: Stack vs Heap Memory Layout
children.description: The stack is automatically managed and holds local variables and return addresses; the heap requires manual management and holds dynamic allocations. Confusing the two leads to dangling pointers (reading freed heap memory) or stack overflows (unbounded recursion), both of which cause silent data corruption or crashes in production.

children.title: Binary, Hex & Two's Complement
children.description: All data in memory is a sequence of bits. Two's complement determines how signed integers overflow, and getting it wrong produces subtle arithmetic bugs that only surface on edge-case inputs. Hex is the universal language for addresses, masks and protocol fields — fluency is non-negotiable in systems work.

children.title: Linkers, Loaders & Executables
children.description: The linker resolves symbol references and produces an ELF or PE binary; the loader maps segments into virtual memory at runtime. Without this knowledge you cannot explain why a static library bloats a binary, how shared library versioning works, or why position-independent code is required for ASLR.

children.title: How the CPU Executes Code
children.description: Modern CPUs fetch, decode and execute instructions through a pipeline, using branch predictors and out-of-order execution to maximize throughput. Ignoring this means your 'optimized' loop is actually stalling the pipeline on every iteration, and you will not be able to explain performance counter data from tools like perf.

resources.label: Ben Eater — How a CPU works

children.title: Pointers & Addresses
children.description: A pointer is an integer holding a virtual memory address. Pointer arithmetic steps by the size of the pointed-to type, so dereferencing the wrong offset corrupts adjacent memory silently. Every systems bug involving buffer overflows, use-after-free or type confusion traces back to misunderstanding pointers.

children.title: Object Files & Symbol Tables
children.description: Object files produced by the compiler contain sections (.text, .data, .bss) and a symbol table mapping names to addresses. Not understanding sections causes confusion about why global variables cost BSS space even when zero, and why inlining a function can unexpectedly change binary size.

children.title: Undefined Behavior in C
children.description: The C standard grants compilers freedom to assume UB never occurs, meaning the optimizer legally deletes null-pointer checks and reorders operations in ways that look insane. Programs with UB appear to work in debug builds and crash or produce wrong results in optimized builds, making UB the single most dangerous concept in C programming.

resources.label: LLVM — What Every C Programmer Should Know About UB

systems-dev.title: C & the Memory Model
systems-dev.description: C is the lingua franca of systems. Master manual memory management, pointers and the tools that keep them safe.
systems-dev.challenge: Implement a dynamic array and a linked list in C with zero memory leaks (verified by valgrind).

children.title: malloc, free & the Heap Allocator
children.description: malloc() requests a block from the heap allocator which maintains free-lists and metadata adjacent to your allocation. Writing past the end of a malloc'd buffer corrupts allocator metadata, causing a crash or silent data corruption far from the original bug site — a nightmare to diagnose without Valgrind.

children.title: Pointer Arithmetic & Array Decay
children.description: Arrays in C decay to pointers to their first element, losing size information. Pointer arithmetic in wrong units (stepping by bytes when you meant elements) reads garbage data or escapes object bounds, triggering undefined behavior that sanitizers will catch but plain builds may silently tolerate.

resources.label: Beej's Guide to C — Pointers II

children.title: Null-Terminated Strings & Buffer Overflows
children.description: C strings are arrays of chars terminated by '\0'. Functions like strcpy and sprintf do not bounds-check, so writing a longer string into a fixed buffer smashes adjacent stack memory including the saved return address — the classic stack overflow exploit vector. Using strncat, snprintf and explicit length tracking prevents entire vulnerability classes.

children.title: Struct Padding & Alignment
children.description: The compiler inserts padding bytes between struct fields so each field meets its alignment requirement. Assuming sizeof(struct) equals the sum of field sizes causes wrong offsets when serializing to disk or sending over a network, producing data corruption that only manifests on specific architectures.

resources.label: The Lost Art of Structure Packing

children.title: GDB Fundamentals
children.description: GDB lets you halt execution, inspect registers and memory, and step through machine instructions. Without it you are left adding printf statements to race conditions and heisenbugs that disappear under observation. Core dumps analysed with gdb are also the primary post-mortem technique for production crashes.

children.title: Valgrind Memcheck
children.description: Valgrind instruments every memory access and reports reads of uninitialised memory, use-after-free, and heap leaks with full stack traces. Memory bugs that appear harmless in small tests cascade into data corruption and security vulnerabilities at scale, and Valgrind is the fastest way to catch them before code review.

children.title: File I/O & errno
children.description: POSIX file I/O functions return -1 on error and set the global errno. Not checking return values and errno means your program silently loses data when a disk is full or a file descriptor limit is hit. errno is also overwritten by the next failing call, so it must be read immediately after the error.

children.title: Format String Vulnerabilities
children.description: Passing user input as the format string to printf (e.g. printf(buf)) lets attackers read and write arbitrary memory using %x and %n format specifiers. This is a well-documented critical severity class; the fix is always printf("%s", buf). Not knowing this pattern means introducing a critical CVE in any C program that formats user data.

systems-dev.title: Modern C++
systems-dev.description: Build on C with modern C++. Use RAII, smart pointers and the STL to write fast, safer systems code.
systems-dev.challenge: Rewrite your C data structures in modern C++ using RAII and smart pointers.

children.title: RAII — Resource Acquisition Is Initialization
children.description: RAII ties resource lifetimes to object lifetimes: a destructor always runs when a local object goes out of scope, even on exception. Code that skips RAII and manages resources manually leaks them on any early-return or thrown exception path, producing connection and file-descriptor leaks that only appear under error conditions.

children.title: Smart Pointers — unique_ptr & shared_ptr
children.description: unique_ptr expresses sole ownership and deletes the object when it goes out of scope; shared_ptr uses reference counting for shared ownership. Mixing raw delete with shared_ptr, or creating two shared_ptrs from the same raw pointer, causes double-free crashes. Understanding ownership semantics prevents the entire class of manual delete errors.

children.title: Move Semantics & Rvalue References
children.description: Move semantics transfer ownership of resources (like heap buffers) without copying them, cutting allocation overhead in performance-critical code. Misunderstanding moves — using an object after it has been moved from, or forgetting to mark constructors noexcept — causes subtle bugs and prevents the compiler from applying the return-value optimization.

children.title: The Rule of Five
children.description: If a class manages a resource, you must define or delete the destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor and move assignment — the Rule of Five. Omitting any one leads to double-frees, shallow copies that corrupt shared state, or silent resource leaks when objects are moved into containers.

children.title: STL Containers & Iterator Invalidation
children.description: Operations like push_back on a vector may reallocate the underlying array, invalidating all existing iterators and pointers into that vector. Continuing to use an invalidated iterator is undefined behavior that produces crashes or silent corruption. Knowing each container's invalidation rules is required to write correct loops that mutate containers.

children.title: Templates & Compilation Cost
children.description: Templates are instantiated at compile time for each set of type arguments, and errors deep in a template produce notoriously long diagnostic messages. Unbounded template instantiation also inflates build times and binary sizes. Concepts (C++20) constrain templates early and dramatically improve error messages and compile times.

children.title: const-correctness & References
children.description: Marking parameters and member functions const communicates immutability to the compiler, enabling optimizations and catching logic errors at compile time. Without const-correctness, a library cannot guarantee thread-safe reads, and callers cannot pass temporaries or const objects to your functions.

children.title: Undefined Behavior in C++
children.description: C++ inherits all of C's UB and adds more: signed integer overflow, out-of-bounds array access and strict aliasing violations are all UB. Compilers exploit UB for optimization, meaning a program can pass all tests under -O0 and silently compute wrong results under -O2. Enabling -fsanitize=undefined is mandatory during development.

systems-dev.title: Rust for Systems
systems-dev.description: Get memory safety without a garbage collector. Learn how Rust's ownership model prevents whole classes of bugs.
systems-dev.challenge: Port one of your C++ data structures to Rust and call it from C via FFI.

children.title: Rust Ownership Model
children.description: Every value has a single owner; when ownership is transferred (moved), the source binding becomes invalid and the compiler rejects further use. This statically eliminates use-after-free and double-free at zero runtime cost. Misunderstanding ownership means fighting the borrow checker instead of leveraging it to prove your code correct at compile time.

children.title: Borrowing & Lifetimes
children.description: A borrow is a reference with a lifetime — the span of code during which the reference is valid. The borrow checker enforces that no mutable reference coexists with any other reference and that references never outlive the data they point to. Lifetime annotations become necessary whenever reference lifetimes cannot be inferred, such as in structs that store references.

resources.label: The Rust Book — References and Borrowing

children.title: Traits & Generic Bounds
children.description: Traits define shared interfaces; generic functions bounded by traits are monomorphized at compile time, producing zero-cost abstractions with no vtable dispatch. Choosing between trait objects (dyn Trait, runtime dispatch) and generics (compile-time dispatch) affects binary size, performance and object-safety — getting this wrong creates unnecessary overhead or unsatisfied trait constraints.

children.title: Error Handling with Result & the ? Operator
children.description: Rust uses Result<T, E> instead of exceptions; the ? operator propagates errors up the call stack automatically. Ignoring errors by unwrapping (unwrap(), expect()) panics in production on unexpected inputs. Designing proper error types and using ? consistently makes error paths as readable as the happy path.

children.title: Zero-Cost Abstractions
children.description: Rust's iterators, closures and smart pointers compile to the same machine code as hand-written loops and raw pointers because there is no runtime overhead from boxing or garbage collection. If your Rust code is slower than equivalent C, you are almost certainly paying for unnecessary heap allocations or dynamic dispatch that type annotations will reveal.

children.title: unsafe Rust & When to Use It
children.description: The unsafe keyword opts out of the borrow checker's guarantees for raw pointer dereference, FFI calls and mutable statics. Unsafe code must manually uphold all invariants the compiler normally enforces; a single violated invariant produces undefined behavior indistinguishable from a C bug. unsafe should be confined to the smallest possible scope with a safety comment.

children.title: Cargo — Build System & Package Manager
children.description: Cargo handles dependency resolution, compilation, testing and publishing in one tool. Feature flags let you conditionally compile optional functionality without bloating every binary. Not understanding Cargo's lock-file semantics leads to reproducibility failures where CI builds succeed but production binaries include a different transitive dependency version.

children.title: C Interop via FFI
children.description: Rust can call C functions and export Rust functions to C via the Foreign Function Interface. Crossing the FFI boundary exits Rust's safety guarantees: the called C function can write to arbitrary memory, and panicking across an FFI boundary is undefined behavior. Every FFI call site needs explicit invariant documentation and must be wrapped in a safe abstraction.

systems-dev.title: Linux & the OS Interface
systems-dev.description: Systems software talks to the kernel. Learn the Linux process model, system calls and the file system interface.
systems-dev.challenge: Write a mini-shell in C that forks processes, handles pipes and reaps children.

children.title: System Calls & the Kernel/User Boundary
children.description: Every interaction with hardware, files or other processes crosses from user space to kernel space via a system call, incurring a context-switch cost. Unnecessary syscalls (e.g. calling write() per byte) are a common performance bottleneck, and failed syscalls silently return -1 unless you check errno after every call.

children.title: Processes, fork & exec
children.description: fork() creates a copy-on-write clone of the calling process; exec() replaces that process image with a new program. Not reaping child processes with wait() leaks zombie entries in the process table. Understanding the fork-exec idiom is the foundation of every shell, daemon and containerised workload on Linux.

children.title: File Descriptors & the Open File Table
children.description: A file descriptor is a small integer indexing the per-process file descriptor table, which references kernel open-file objects. Leaking file descriptors exhausts the per-process limit (ulimit -n), causing open() to return EMFILE and crashing network servers that never close accepted sockets.

children.title: Signals & Async-Signal Safety
children.description: Signals interrupt normal execution and invoke a signal handler asynchronously. Only async-signal-safe functions may be called from a handler; calling malloc or printf from a signal handler causes deadlocks because the process may already hold the allocator lock. The standard pattern is to write a byte to a self-pipe and handle the event in the main loop.

children.title: Virtual Memory & Page Tables
children.description: Each process sees a private virtual address space; the MMU translates virtual addresses to physical addresses through page tables. A segfault is the CPU raising a page fault when a translation is missing or a permission is violated. Without understanding virtual memory you cannot reason about mmap performance, huge pages or why accessing a freshly allocated page is slow.

children.title: IPC — Pipes, Shared Memory & Message Queues
children.description: Pipes provide a unidirectional byte stream between processes; shared memory is the fastest IPC mechanism but requires explicit synchronization to avoid data races. Choosing the wrong IPC primitive — e.g. shared memory without a mutex — produces race conditions across process boundaries that are harder to debug than thread races.

children.title: Pthreads & Thread Lifecycle
children.description: POSIX threads share the process address space; creating a thread with pthread_create and failing to join or detach it leaks its stack. Thread-local storage (TLS) is the safe way to give each thread private state without locks, but TLS variables are invisible across thread boundaries which surprises developers expecting shared globals.

children.title: Scheduling — Preemption & Priority
children.description: The Linux CFS scheduler preempts threads based on virtual runtime, not nanosecond sleeps. Setting SCHED_FIFO with a high priority can starve lower-priority threads, including the kernel softirq handler, causing network packet drops. Understanding scheduling prevents latency spikes in real-time and high-throughput systems.

systems-dev.title: Concurrency & Parallelism
systems-dev.description: Make systems use all the cores. Master threads, synchronization and lock-free techniques.
topics: Data races & how to avoid them
systems-dev.challenge: Build a thread-safe work-stealing task queue and benchmark it against a mutex-guarded queue.

children.title: Data Races & the Memory Model
children.description: A data race occurs when two threads access the same memory location concurrently and at least one write is unsynchronized. Races produce undefined behavior in both C++ and Rust (outside unsafe): the compiler and CPU may reorder operations in ways that make the race outcome arbitrary. ThreadSanitizer detects races at runtime, but preventing them requires understanding happens-before relationships.

children.title: Mutexes, Deadlock & Priority Inversion
children.description: A mutex serializes access to a shared resource; acquiring two mutexes in inconsistent order across threads creates a deadlock where neither thread can proceed. Priority inversion — a high-priority thread blocked on a mutex held by a low-priority thread — can lock up real-time systems for unbounded time without priority inheritance.

children.title: Atomic Operations & Memory Ordering
children.description: Atomic operations appear indivisible to other threads, but memory ordering (relaxed, acquire, release, seq_cst) controls which other writes become visible around the atomic. Using relaxed ordering on a flag that guards non-atomic data allows the CPU to speculatively load stale values, silently breaking the invariant the flag was meant to protect.

resources.label: Rust Atomics and Locks — Mara Bos

children.title: Lock-Free Data Structures
children.description: Lock-free algorithms use compare-and-swap (CAS) loops to modify shared state without mutexes, eliminating contention under high parallelism. The ABA problem — a pointer changing from A to B and back to A between a load and a CAS — invalidates many naive lock-free designs and requires tagged pointers or hazard pointers to solve correctly.

children.title: Async Runtimes & the Event Loop
children.description: Async runtimes like Tokio schedule thousands of tasks on a small thread pool using cooperative multitasking. A single blocking syscall on an async task stalls the entire thread, starving all other tasks on that thread. Every I/O operation in an async context must use async-aware equivalents; mixing blocking and async code is the most common Tokio performance pitfall.

children.title: C++ Coroutines (C++20)
children.description: C++20 coroutines are stackless: suspension saves only local variables, not a full stack frame, making them far cheaper than OS threads. Because the coroutine frame is heap-allocated, mismanaging its lifetime — e.g. destroying an awaitable before the coroutine resumes — is undefined behavior that produces hard-to-diagnose crashes.

children.title: Condition Variables & Spurious Wakeups
children.description: Condition variables allow a thread to sleep until a predicate becomes true. Spurious wakeups — where a thread wakes without being signalled — are permitted by POSIX; code that checks the condition once instead of in a while loop will proceed on a spurious wakeup with an unsatisfied predicate, causing hard-to-reproduce bugs under load.

children.title: Thread Pool Design & Work Stealing
children.description: A fixed-size thread pool amortizes thread creation cost; each worker dequeues tasks from a shared queue. False sharing — multiple threads writing to adjacent cache lines — can make a thread pool slower than a single-threaded solution by causing cache-line bouncing between cores. Aligning each worker's state to a cache line (64 bytes) eliminates this overhead.

systems-dev.title: Networking & Sockets
systems-dev.description: Connect systems over the network. Learn the socket API and how to build high-throughput servers.
systems-dev.challenge: Build a non-blocking TCP echo server with epoll that handles thousands of connections.

children.title: Berkeley Sockets — socket, bind, connect
children.description: The POSIX socket API exposes TCP/IP through file descriptors: socket() creates a descriptor, bind() assigns a local address, listen() and accept() handle incoming connections. Not setting SO_REUSEADDR before bind() causes 'Address already in use' errors for 60 seconds after a server restart due to TIME_WAIT sockets lingering in the kernel.

children.title: TCP — Three-Way Handshake & Flow Control
children.description: TCP's three-way handshake establishes sequence numbers; flow control (receive window) and congestion control (CWND) limit throughput. Sending large messages without understanding Nagle's algorithm and delayed ACKs adds up to 40ms of artificial latency per round trip in interactive protocols — disabling TCP_NODELAY is the standard fix.

children.title: Blocking vs Non-Blocking Sockets
children.description: A blocking recv() suspends the calling thread until data arrives; a non-blocking socket returns EAGAIN immediately if no data is available. Using blocking sockets in a single-threaded server serializes all clients — one slow client blocks all others. Setting O_NONBLOCK with fcntl() switches to non-blocking mode and enables event-loop architectures.

children.title: I/O Multiplexing — epoll
children.description: epoll_wait() returns only the file descriptors that are ready for I/O, allowing a single thread to manage tens of thousands of connections. Edge-triggered mode (EPOLLET) only notifies on state transitions, so failing to read all available data in one call causes the fd to go silent permanently until new data arrives — a subtle bug that manifests as stuck connections.

children.title: Protocol Framing & Message Boundaries
children.description: TCP is a byte stream with no inherent message boundaries; a single send() call does not guarantee a single recv() call receives the same amount of data. Assuming otherwise produces parsers that work in tests (small messages, local loopback) and silently corrupt data in production (large messages, high-latency WAN links) due to partial reads.

children.title: UDP & Unreliable Datagrams
children.description: UDP delivers packets individually with no ordering or reliability guarantees. Applications that require ordered or reliable delivery on UDP must implement their own sequence numbers and retransmission — the basis of QUIC and most game networking protocols. Using UDP with the expectation of delivery causes silent data loss that only surfaces under network stress.

children.title: Event-Driven Server Design
children.description: An event-driven server uses a single thread and a readiness notification mechanism (epoll/kqueue) to multiplex I/O across all connections, avoiding the per-thread stack overhead of the thread-per-connection model. The critical constraint is that no handler may block; a blocking call inside the event loop blocks all other connections for its entire duration.

children.title: Socket Options & Tuning
children.description: Kernel socket buffers (SO_SNDBUF, SO_RCVBUF) control how much data the kernel will buffer per socket before applying backpressure. Setting these too small saturates the buffer and causes send() to block or return EAGAIN under burst traffic; setting them too large wastes RAM and increases latency by allowing more data to queue before the sender is informed of congestion.

systems-dev.title: Performance & Profiling
systems-dev.description: Make code fast and prove it. Understand the memory hierarchy and use profilers to find real bottlenecks.
systems-dev.challenge: Profile a hot loop, improve data locality, and document the speedup with flamegraphs.

children.title: CPU Caches & Data Locality
children.description: L1/L2/L3 caches serve data in 64-byte cache lines. Accessing a struct field brings the entire line into cache; iterating over an array of structs that are larger than a cache line wastes bandwidth loading unused fields. Reorganizing hot data into a struct-of-arrays (SoA) layout can yield 5–10x speedups purely from cache efficiency.

resources.label: Ulrich Drepper — What Every Programmer Should Know About Memory
Agner Fog — Optimizing Software in C++

children.title: Branch Prediction & Pipelines
children.description: Modern CPUs speculatively execute past branches using a branch predictor. A misprediction flushes the pipeline (15–20 cycle penalty). Sorting data before processing it makes branches predictable; replacing unpredictable branches with branchless arithmetic using conditional moves (cmov) eliminates the penalty entirely for simple cases.

children.title: perf & Hardware Performance Counters
children.description: perf uses hardware PMU counters to measure events like cache misses, branch mispredictions and retired instructions with near-zero overhead. perf stat gives a per-run summary; perf record + perf report identifies which functions are hot. Without counter data you are guessing at bottlenecks; with it you can distinguish memory-bound from compute-bound code precisely.

children.title: Flamegraphs
children.description: A flamegraph visualises sampled call stacks: the width of each frame is proportional to the time spent in that function. Flamegraphs reveal not just which function is hot but the call path that reaches it, enabling targeted optimization without wading through per-function tables. They are generated from perf script output via Brendan Gregg's flamegraph.pl.

children.title: Benchmarking Methodology
children.description: A benchmark is only meaningful if it measures the right thing: CPU frequency scaling (turbo boost), OS scheduling jitter and JIT warm-up all add noise. Pinning the CPU governor to 'performance', using clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC) and running enough iterations to amortise noise are minimum requirements; publishing a single run time is not a benchmark.

children.title: Allocation & Memory Bandwidth
children.description: Memory bandwidth is the rate at which data can be transferred between DRAM and the CPU; it is a hard ceiling for memory-bound algorithms. Every heap allocation through malloc involves acquiring an allocator lock (in the common case) and touching new cache lines; replacing many small allocations with a custom arena allocator can eliminate allocation from the hot path entirely.

children.title: SIMD — Single Instruction Multiple Data
children.description: SIMD instructions (SSE2, AVX2, AVX-512) operate on 128–512 bit registers, processing 4–16 floats in a single instruction. Auto-vectorization by the compiler requires data alignment and no loop-carried dependencies; adding __attribute__((aligned(32))) and breaking aliasing with restrict hints enables the compiler to emit SIMD without manual intrinsics.

children.title: Compiler Optimizations & Flags
children.description: -O2 enables most safe optimizations; -O3 adds vectorization and more aggressive inlining but can increase code size. Link-time optimization (-flto) allows the compiler to inline across translation unit boundaries, critical for small utility functions in headers. Profile-guided optimization (PGO) uses runtime profiles to optimize the most-executed paths, typically yielding 10–20% improvement.

systems-dev.title: Building Systems Tools in Go
systems-dev.description: Go is ideal for fast, portable systems tooling. Build CLIs and network tools that ship as a single binary.
systems-dev.challenge: Build a concurrent port scanner CLI in Go that cross-compiles to Linux, macOS and Windows.

children.title: Goroutines & the Go Scheduler
children.description: Goroutines are multiplexed onto OS threads by the Go runtime scheduler using M:N threading. Each goroutine starts with a 2–8 KB stack that grows dynamically, making it practical to spawn millions of goroutines. Blocking a goroutine on a channel or I/O yields to the scheduler transparently; blocking via cgo or an OS syscall can exhaust the thread pool and stall the runtime.

resources.label: Go Blog — Concurrency is not Parallelism

children.title: Channels & the CSP Model
children.description: Channels implement Communicating Sequential Processes: goroutines pass ownership of data through channels rather than sharing memory under locks. Sending on a closed channel panics; receiving from a closed channel returns the zero value immediately. Select-with-default enables non-blocking channel operations, which is essential for building cancellation and timeout patterns correctly.

children.title: Building CLIs with cobra
children.description: cobra structures CLI applications into commands, subcommands and flags with automatic help generation. Not separating command business logic from cobra's Run function makes commands untestable in isolation. Cobra's PersistentPreRunE hook is the correct place to validate global flags and initialise configuration before any subcommand runs.

children.title: Cross-Compilation & Static Binaries
children.description: Go compiles to a statically linked binary by default, but CGO (Go-C interop) re-introduces dynamic linking against libc. Setting CGO_ENABLED=0 produces a truly static binary that runs on any Linux system regardless of glibc version. GOOS and GOARCH environment variables cross-compile to any target without a toolchain change — the primary reason Go dominates DevOps tooling.

resources.label: Go Blog — Building Go Programs for Multiple Platforms

children.title: os & syscall Packages
children.description: Go's os package wraps POSIX file and process operations with idiomatic error handling using the error interface. The lower-level syscall and golang.org/x/sys packages expose raw system calls for OS-specific functionality. Not checking errors from os.File.Close() can silently discard buffered writes if the underlying write syscall fails during close.

children.title: context.Context & Cancellation
children.description: context.Context propagates deadlines, cancellation signals and request-scoped values through call chains. A goroutine that ignores context cancellation leaks until the process exits, consuming memory and potentially holding file descriptors or connections open. Every blocking operation — I/O, sleep, channel receive — must select on ctx.Done() to be cancellable.

children.title: pprof — CPU & Memory Profiling
children.description: Go's pprof package samples goroutine stacks at 100 Hz for CPU profiles and records every heap allocation for memory profiles. Embedding net/http/pprof in a server exposes live profiles over HTTP. The heap profile distinguishes in-use allocations from cumulative allocations, making it possible to identify goroutine leaks and allocation-heavy hot paths in running services.

children.title: Go Data Race Detector
children.description: Building with -race instruments every memory access and reports concurrent accesses at runtime. The race detector adds ~5–10x slowdown and 5–10x memory overhead, so it is used in tests and staging rather than production. A race report is deterministic proof of a data race — the detector never reports false positives, so every report demands a fix.

resources.label: Go Blog — Introducing the Race Detector

systems-dev.title: Debugging, Testing & Deployment
systems-dev.description: Ship reliable systems software. Master debuggers, sanitizers and build pipelines for native code.
systems-dev.challenge: Add a CI pipeline that builds your project with sanitizers and runs a fuzz target.

children.title: GDB — Breakpoints, Watchpoints & Core Dumps
children.description: Watchpoints halt execution when a specific memory address is written, catching the exact instruction that corrupts a variable — impossible to locate with printf. Core dumps capture the full process state at crash time; loading one with gdb reveals the exact stack frame, register values and corrupted memory, turning a mysterious SIGSEGV into a diagnosed bug.

resources.label: GDB User Manual — Core Dump Files

children.title: Reverse Debugging with rr
children.description: Mozilla's rr records a deterministic execution trace that can be replayed and stepped backwards. This eliminates the need to reproduce non-deterministic bugs — races, signal-timing issues and memory corruption that only occur after thousands of operations. rr is indispensable for any bug that disappears when you add a printf or rebuild with debug symbols.

children.title: AddressSanitizer (ASan)
children.description: ASan instruments every memory access and detects heap overflows, stack overflows and use-after-free with precise stack traces at roughly 2x runtime overhead. It works by poisoning shadow memory around allocations and checking it on every access. Running ASan in CI prevents the class of memory safety bugs that only manifest in production under specific workloads.

children.title: UBSan & ThreadSanitizer
children.description: UBSan inserts checks for signed overflow, null dereference and invalid casts, reporting undefined behavior at the exact source location. ThreadSanitizer detects data races with ~5–15x overhead by tracking every memory access's 'happened-before' relationship. Both should be permanent fixtures in the test suite — not just tools you reach for after a bug is reported.

children.title: Coverage-Guided Fuzzing with libFuzzer
children.description: libFuzzer mutates input bytes guided by code coverage feedback, automatically discovering inputs that reach new code paths and crash the target. Fuzz testing finds bugs that unit tests miss because humans cannot enumerate the input space of a parser or protocol implementation. Integrating a fuzz target into CI with a corpus catches regressions before release.

children.title: Rust Fuzzing with cargo-fuzz
children.description: cargo-fuzz wraps libFuzzer for Rust, automatically enabling ASan and UBSan alongside coverage feedback. Because Rust prevents most memory safety bugs, cargo-fuzz is most valuable for finding logic errors, panics on unexpected inputs and integer overflows in parsers. The cargo fuzz corpus stores minimised crashing inputs for regression prevention.

children.title: CMake — Modern Target-Based Builds
children.description: Modern CMake (3.x+) uses targets and properties instead of global variables: target_link_libraries with PUBLIC/PRIVATE/INTERFACE correctly propagates include paths and compile options to dependents. Using add_compile_options() globally contaminates all targets including third-party code, causing hard-to-diagnose flag conflicts in large projects.

children.title: CI for Native Code — Sanitizer & Fuzz Pipelines
children.description: A CI pipeline for native code must build with at least ASan+UBSan and run the full test suite under those flags on every PR. A separate nightly job runs the fuzz corpus for N seconds to catch regressions. Without mandatory sanitizer builds in CI, memory bugs are found in production rather than during development, where fixes cost an order of magnitude more.

security-dev.title: Security Foundations
security-dev.description: Build a mental model of security. Learn the core principles and the threat landscape every developer must understand.
security-dev.challenge: Threat-model a simple login form and list its assets, threats and mitigations.

children.title: CIA Triad
children.description: Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability are the three pillars every security decision must balance. Violating any one — e.g., leaking credentials, tampering with data, or denying service — constitutes a security breach with real business and legal consequences.

children.title: Threat Landscape & Attack Vectors
children.description: Understanding how attackers think — phishing, supply chain attacks, insider threats — lets you design defenses before incidents occur. Without this mental model, security controls are reactive patches rather than coherent architecture.

children.title: TCP/IP & DNS Security
children.description: Most attacks traverse TCP/IP, and DNS is frequently abused for exfiltration or redirection. Knowing how packets flow, how DNS resolves, and where each step can be hijacked is essential for both building and hardening networked services.

children.title: Firewalls, VPNs & Segmentation
children.description: Network segmentation limits blast radius when a host is compromised; firewalls enforce policy at boundaries. Misconfigured rules — such as overly permissive egress — are a primary path attackers use to exfiltrate data after initial access.

children.title: OWASP Top 10 Overview
children.description: The OWASP Top 10 catalogs the most critical web-application risks, ranked by prevalence and exploitability. Ignoring any item on this list means shipping software with known, documented vulnerabilities that automated scanners will find within hours of deployment.

children.title: Security Mindset & Secure Design
children.description: Defense-in-depth, least privilege, and fail-safe defaults are design patterns that prevent single points of failure in security. Applying them from the start is far cheaper than retrofitting controls after a breach.

children.title: Wireshark for Traffic Analysis
children.description: Wireshark lets you capture and dissect live network traffic to identify plaintext credentials, unusual protocols, or exfiltration attempts. Developers who understand packet captures can verify that their apps are not leaking sensitive data in transit.

children.title: Nmap for Network Scanning
children.description: Nmap reveals open ports, running services, and OS fingerprints — information attackers gather before exploiting a target. Running Nmap against your own infrastructure regularly helps catch misconfigurations and unintended exposure before attackers do.

security-dev.title: Web Security & OWASP Top 10
security-dev.description: Learn the most common web vulnerabilities by exploiting and then fixing them. The OWASP Top 10 is your map.
security-dev.challenge: Find and fix an XSS and a SQL injection vulnerability in OWASP Juice Shop.

children.title: Injection Attacks (SQLi, XSS, Command)
children.description: Injection flaws occur when untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query. SQL injection alone can expose entire databases; command injection can yield full server compromise — both are trivially prevented by parameterized queries and output encoding.

children.title: Broken Access Control
children.description: Broken access control, ranked #1 in OWASP 2021, lets unauthorized users read or modify others' data simply by manipulating IDs or URLs. Proper server-side enforcement — never trusting client-supplied identity — is the only reliable mitigation.

children.title: Content Security Policy (CSP)
children.description: CSP instructs browsers which sources may load scripts, styles, and media, making XSS attacks significantly harder to exploit. A missing or permissive CSP is one of the most common findings in web security audits and allows injected scripts to run unchallenged.

children.title: HSTS, X-Frame-Options & CORS
children.description: HSTS forces HTTPS connections preventing SSL-strip attacks; X-Frame-Options prevents clickjacking; CORS misconfiguration can expose APIs to unauthorized origins. These headers are low-effort, high-impact controls that every web app should set by default.

children.title: Intercepting & Modifying Requests
children.description: Burp Suite's proxy lets you intercept, inspect, and replay HTTP requests to test how the server handles unexpected or malicious input. Every backend developer should use it to verify their app rejects manipulated parameters and enforces server-side validation.

children.title: Active Scanning & Repeater
children.description: Burp's Scanner automates detection of common vulnerabilities; Repeater lets you craft custom payloads iteratively. Together they form the core workflow for manual and semi-automated web application penetration testing.

children.title: CVE & NVD Databases
children.description: CVE identifiers uniquely track publicly known vulnerabilities; NVD provides CVSS severity scores and patch status. Regularly checking dependencies against NVD catches known vulnerabilities before attackers exploit them in your running software.

children.title: OWASP WebGoat – Hands-on Practice
children.description: WebGoat is a deliberately insecure application for practicing web security exploitation in a safe environment. Hands-on practice with real attack patterns builds the intuition needed to spot and fix the same issues in production code.

security-dev.title: Authentication
security-dev.description: Get authentication right. Implement secure login flows with OAuth2, OpenID Connect and JSON Web Tokens.
security-dev.challenge: Implement an OAuth2 + OIDC login with secure JWT handling and refresh-token rotation.

children.title: JWT Structure & Signing
children.description: JWTs consist of a header, payload, and signature; the signature's integrity depends entirely on using a strong secret or asymmetric key pair. Accepting tokens signed with the 'none' algorithm or weak secrets allows attackers to forge identities without knowing any credentials.

children.title: JWT Security Pitfalls
children.description: Common JWT vulnerabilities include algorithm confusion attacks, missing expiry validation, and storing sensitive data in the unencrypted payload. Mitigating these requires strict algorithm whitelisting, short expiry windows, and never trusting payload claims without signature verification.

children.title: bcrypt & Password Hashing
children.description: Bcrypt applies a computationally expensive work factor to password hashing, making brute-force attacks orders of magnitude slower than MD5 or SHA. Storing passwords with fast hash algorithms means a stolen database can be fully cracked in hours on commodity hardware.

children.title: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
children.description: MFA requires a second factor beyond a password, dramatically reducing account takeover risk even after credential leaks. TOTP-based MFA (e.g., Google Authenticator) is resistant to phishing when combined with app binding, unlike SMS-based codes vulnerable to SIM-swapping.

children.title: OAuth 2.0 Flows
children.description: OAuth 2.0 delegates authorization without sharing passwords; choosing the wrong flow (e.g., Implicit flow in SPAs) exposes tokens in URLs and browser history. The Authorization Code flow with PKCE is the current best practice for all client-side applications.

children.title: OpenID Connect & Identity Tokens
children.description: OIDC layers identity on top of OAuth 2.0, issuing ID tokens that prove who the user is rather than just what they can access. Confusing the access token with the ID token leads to privilege escalation or broken user identification across services.

children.title: Secure Session Handling
children.description: Sessions must use cryptographically random IDs, short expiry, and HttpOnly/Secure cookies to prevent hijacking via XSS or network sniffing. Reusing session IDs after login enables session fixation attacks where an attacker pre-sets a known session value.

children.title: Token Revocation & Logout
children.description: Stateless JWTs cannot be revoked mid-lifetime without a blocklist; failing to implement one means a stolen token remains valid until expiry. Proper logout must invalidate server-side sessions and clear all client-side token storage.

security-dev.title: Authorization & Access Control
security-dev.description: Control who can do what. Design robust authorization models and avoid the #1 web risk: broken access control.
security-dev.challenge: Add object-level RBAC to an API and write tests proving users can't access others' resources.

children.title: RBAC Design & Enforcement
children.description: RBAC assigns permissions to roles rather than individuals, simplifying administration and reducing privilege creep over time. Authorization must always be enforced on the server; trusting client-side role checks alone is a critical vulnerability that any user can bypass with browser dev tools.

children.title: Principle of Least Privilege
children.description: Every user, service, and process should have only the minimum permissions needed to perform their function. Over-privileged accounts dramatically amplify the damage of any compromise — a read-only service account cannot drop a database even if its credentials are stolen.

children.title: ABAC & Policy-Based Authorization
children.description: ABAC evaluates attributes of users, resources, and environment at request time to make fine-grained decisions that RBAC alone cannot express. Without ABAC, multi-tenant systems often resort to complex role proliferation that becomes unmaintainable and error-prone.

children.title: Open Policy Agent (OPA)
children.description: OPA decouples policy decisions from application code, letting teams update authorization rules without redeploying services. Hardcoding authorization logic in application code makes it difficult to audit, test, or change consistently across microservices.

children.title: Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR)
children.description: IDOR occurs when an application exposes internal implementation objects (IDs, filenames) without verifying the requester's right to access them. A single missing authorization check on a record lookup can expose every user's private data to any authenticated attacker.

children.title: Vertical vs. Horizontal Privilege Escalation
children.description: Vertical escalation gains higher roles (e.g., user becomes admin); horizontal escalation accesses peers' resources without elevation. Both are authorization failures and are commonly discovered through parameter tampering or by replaying requests with different user contexts.

resources.label: OWASP Top 10 A01 – Broken Access Control

children.title: OAuth Scopes & Resource Servers
children.description: OAuth scopes limit what an access token can do; resource servers must validate both the token signature and the required scope on every request. A token issued for read access that the server never checks can be abused to write, delete, or administer resources.

children.title: Row-Level & Column-Level Security
children.description: Database-level row security ensures that even if authorization logic in application code is bypassed, the database refuses to return unauthorized rows. This defense-in-depth layer prevents mass data leakage from SQL injection or logic bugs that slip through code review.

security-dev.title: Cryptography Essentials
security-dev.description: Use cryptography correctly. Learn the primitives, when to use each, and the dangers of rolling your own.
security-dev.challenge: Encrypt sensitive fields at rest with AES-GCM and verify integrity with HMAC.

children.title: AES Encryption
children.description: AES is the standard symmetric cipher for bulk data encryption; using it in ECB mode leaks data patterns while GCM mode provides both encryption and authentication integrity. Misuse — hardcoded keys, reused IVs, or ECB mode — can render encrypted data trivially recoverable.

children.title: RSA & Elliptic Curve Cryptography
children.description: RSA and ECC are asymmetric algorithms used for key exchange, digital signatures, and certificate issuance; small key sizes or padding oracle vulnerabilities make them exploitable. RSA-2048 is the minimum acceptable today; ECC P-256 achieves equivalent security with smaller keys and faster operations.

children.title: TLS 1.3 & Certificate Validation
children.description: TLS 1.3 eliminates legacy cipher suites and reduces the handshake to one round-trip, removing multiple attack surfaces present in TLS 1.0/1.1. Improper certificate validation — skipping hostname checks or accepting self-signed certs in production — enables trivial man-in-the-middle attacks.

children.title: Certificate Pinning & HPKP
children.description: Certificate pinning restricts which certificates a client will trust for a given domain, preventing attackers with a rogue CA certificate from impersonating your service. Misconfigured pinning without a backup pin leads to application lock-out when certificates rotate.

resources.label: OWASP – Certificate and Public Key Pinning

children.title: Cryptographic Hash Functions
children.description: Hash functions like SHA-256 produce fixed-length digests used for data integrity verification, digital signatures, and HMAC authentication. Using MD5 or SHA-1 for security-sensitive operations is dangerous as both have known collision attacks that allow forged signatures.

children.title: PBKDF2, Argon2 & Key Derivation
children.description: Key derivation functions stretch low-entropy secrets (passwords) into cryptographic keys using deliberate computational cost and salt. Using a fast hash like SHA-256 directly on a password allows GPU-accelerated cracking that can test billions of candidates per second.

children.title: Environment Variables & Secret Vaults
children.description: Secrets must never be committed to source control; environment variables are a minimum, while dedicated vaults (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) provide auditing, rotation, and fine-grained access control. Leaked secrets in public repositories are harvested by automated scanners within minutes of exposure.

children.title: Key Rotation & Lifecycle Management
children.description: Cryptographic keys must be rotated on a schedule and immediately upon suspected compromise; systems that cannot rotate keys without downtime accumulate long-lived keys that become attractive targets. Implementing automated rotation before a breach forces the practice without the pressure of an incident.

security-dev.title: Secure Coding Practices
security-dev.description: Write code that resists attack. Apply input validation, output encoding and safe defaults throughout your app.
security-dev.challenge: Harden a vulnerable API endpoint with validation, parameterized queries and proper secret handling.

children.title: Input Validation Strategies
children.description: Validating input on the server — not just the client — using allowlists rather than blocklists prevents malformed data from reaching business logic or databases. Relying solely on client-side validation is trivially bypassed by any attacker who intercepts the HTTP request.

children.title: Parameterized Queries & ORM Safety
children.description: Parameterized queries and prepared statements ensure user input is always treated as data, never as SQL syntax. Even ORM-based applications can introduce injection if raw query methods (e.g., `.query()`, `.raw()`) are used without proper escaping.

resources.label: OWASP – SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet

children.title: SAST Tools & Integration
children.description: Static Application Security Testing analyzes source code for security vulnerabilities before runtime, catching issues like hardcoded credentials, insecure deserialization, and path traversal at the earliest possible stage. Integrating SAST into the CI pipeline ensures that security regressions are caught before code is merged.

children.title: Dependency Scanning
children.description: Third-party dependencies routinely introduce vulnerabilities that developers inherit but did not write; tools like npm audit, Snyk, and Dependabot surface these with CVE severity scores. A single unpatched transitive dependency was responsible for the Log4Shell exploit affecting millions of applications.

children.title: Security-Focused Code Review
children.description: Security code review specifically looks for dangerous function calls, missing authorization checks, and improper error handling that functional review often misses. Establishing a security checklist tied to OWASP Top 10 ensures reviewers systematically address the highest-probability vulnerability classes.

resources.label: OWASP – Secure Code Review Cheat Sheet

children.title: Error Handling & Information Disclosure
children.description: Verbose error messages that include stack traces, database queries, or file paths give attackers a roadmap of the application's internals. Proper error handling logs details server-side while returning generic, non-revealing messages to clients.

children.title: Insecure Deserialization
children.description: Deserializing untrusted data can lead to remote code execution when an attacker crafts a payload that exploits gadget chains in the application's dependencies. Frameworks like Java's native serialization, PHP's `unserialize()`, and Python's `pickle` are particularly dangerous with untrusted input.

children.title: Secure File Upload Handling
children.description: File uploads must validate MIME type server-side, restrict extensions, scan for malware, and store files outside the webroot to prevent direct execution. Unrestricted uploads are a direct path to webshell deployment and full server compromise.

security-dev.title: API & Network Security
security-dev.description: Protect data in transit and APIs at the edge. Configure TLS, CORS and defenses against abuse.
security-dev.challenge: Configure TLS, strict CORS, a CSP and rate limiting on an API, then verify with a header scanner.

children.title: Enforcing HTTPS End-to-End
children.description: HTTPS encrypts traffic between client and server, preventing eavesdropping and tampering on untrusted networks; HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) prevents browsers from ever downgrading to HTTP. Mixed content — loading HTTP resources on an HTTPS page — partially negates these protections and is flagged by browsers.

children.title: CORS Configuration
children.description: CORS controls which origins can make cross-origin requests to your API; an overly permissive policy (`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` with credentials) allows malicious sites to make authenticated requests on behalf of your users. CORS must be configured server-side — browser enforcement can be bypassed by non-browser clients.

children.title: API Rate Limiting
children.description: Rate limiting throttles the number of requests a client can make in a time window, preventing brute-force attacks on authentication endpoints and resource exhaustion from abuse. Without it, an attacker can systematically enumerate valid credentials or overwhelm backend services at negligible cost.

resources.label: OWASP – API Security Top 10 – Unrestricted Resource Consumption

children.title: DDoS Mitigation
children.description: Distributed Denial of Service attacks overwhelm services with traffic from thousands of compromised hosts; mitigations include CDN-based traffic scrubbing, anycast routing, and challenge-based bot detection. Availability is part of the CIA triad — unmitigated DDoS causes the same business harm as a data breach.

children.title: OWASP API Security Top 10
children.description: The OWASP API Security Top 10 documents API-specific risks including broken object-level authorization, excessive data exposure, and mass assignment — vulnerabilities that differ from traditional web app risks. REST APIs are the primary attack surface for modern applications and deserve a dedicated threat model.

children.title: Input Sanitization at API Boundaries
children.description: Every API endpoint is a potential injection point; validating schema, types, and value ranges on every incoming payload prevents malformed data from propagating into downstream services or databases. Mass assignment — blindly binding request bodies to database models — is a common API vulnerability that exposes admin fields to user control.

resources.label: OWASP API Security – Excessive Data Exposure

children.title: WAF Deployment & Rules
children.description: A Web Application Firewall inspects and filters HTTP traffic based on rules that detect common attacks like SQLi, XSS, and path traversal at the network layer. WAFs are not a substitute for secure code — a skilled attacker can often bypass WAF rules — but they add a meaningful layer of defense against automated attacks.

children.title: API Gateway Security
children.description: API gateways centralize authentication, rate limiting, logging, and schema validation for all API traffic, reducing per-service duplication. Bypassing the gateway — e.g., calling internal service URLs directly — allows attackers to circumvent all gateway-level controls.

security-dev.title: Threat Modeling & Pen Testing
security-dev.description: Think like an attacker. Model threats systematically and learn the basics of ethical penetration testing.
security-dev.challenge: Produce a STRIDE threat model and a short pen-test report for a demo web app.

children.title: STRIDE Framework
children.description: STRIDE categorizes threats into Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, and Elevation of Privilege — providing a structured checklist for identifying what can go wrong in a system design. Running STRIDE during design is dramatically cheaper than discovering those threats in production.

children.title: Data Flow Diagrams & Trust Boundaries
children.description: Threat modeling begins with a data flow diagram that shows where data moves between components and where trust boundaries exist; threats are generated at every crossing of a trust boundary. Missing a trust boundary in the diagram means missing the threats that cross it.

children.title: Pen Test Phases (Recon, Exploit, Report)
children.description: A structured pen test follows phases: reconnaissance, scanning, exploitation, post-exploitation, and reporting — mirroring real attacker methodology to find what a motivated adversary would. Skipping reconnaissance produces incomplete results because many vulnerabilities are only visible with full context about the target.

children.title: Scope, Rules of Engagement & Legal
children.description: Unauthorized penetration testing is illegal regardless of intent; written scope and rules of engagement protect both testers and organizations by defining exactly what systems can be tested and how. Exceeding scope — even to demonstrate a critical vulnerability — can result in criminal liability.

resources.label: EC-Council – Pen Test Rules of Engagement

children.title: Metasploit Framework
children.description: Metasploit provides a library of exploitation modules and payloads that automate the process of exploiting known vulnerabilities — making it both an essential pen testing tool and a reminder of how quickly known CVEs can be weaponized. Defenders use Metasploit to verify that patches and controls actually prevent exploitation.

children.title: OWASP ZAP for Automated Scanning
children.description: OWASP ZAP is a free, open-source DAST tool that actively probes running applications for vulnerabilities including XSS, injection, and misconfigured security headers. It can be integrated into CI/CD pipelines to continuously test applications as they change.

children.title: Responsible Disclosure Programs
children.description: Responsible disclosure gives vendors time to patch before vulnerabilities are made public; bug bounty programs incentivize external researchers to find and report vulnerabilities rather than sell or exploit them. Organizations without a clear disclosure policy risk receiving no advance warning before public exploit publication.

children.title: Bug Bounty Platforms
children.description: Platforms like HackerOne and Bugcrowd connect organizations with vetted security researchers under legal agreements that define scope and payment. Participating in bug bounties as a researcher builds practical skills faster than most training courses by providing real targets with real impact.

security-dev.title: Security Testing & DevSecOps
security-dev.description: Automate security in the pipeline. Add static, dynamic and dependency scanning so issues are caught early.
security-dev.challenge: Add SAST, dependency scanning and secret scanning as blocking checks to a CI pipeline.

children.title: DAST Tools & CI Integration
children.description: DAST tools test running applications by sending attack payloads and analyzing responses, catching runtime vulnerabilities that static analysis cannot detect such as authentication bypasses and session issues. Integrating DAST into staging environments provides continuous security feedback on every deployment.

children.title: Snyk for Dependency & Container Scanning
children.description: Snyk scans dependencies, container images, and infrastructure-as-code for known vulnerabilities and misconfigurations, providing fix advice with each finding. Container images frequently include vulnerable OS packages that developers don't consider part of their application but attackers absolutely target.

children.title: Shifting Security Left
children.description: Shifting left means integrating security activities — threat modeling, SAST, dependency scanning — into the earliest stages of development rather than gating on a pre-release pen test. Security findings discovered during design cost approximately 30x less to fix than those found after deployment.

children.title: Security Gates in CI/CD Pipelines
children.description: Security gates in CI/CD pipelines fail builds when vulnerabilities above a severity threshold are detected, preventing insecure code from reaching production. Without automated gates, security findings are advisory and routinely deprioritized in release pressure.

children.title: IaC Scanning (Terraform, CloudFormation)
children.description: Infrastructure-as-code files define cloud resources and their security configurations; scanning them with tools like Checkov or tfsec catches public S3 buckets, open security groups, and disabled encryption before provisioning. IaC misconfigurations are responsible for a significant proportion of cloud data breaches.

children.title: Container & Kubernetes Security
children.description: Containers running as root, with host network access, or from unscanned base images introduce vulnerabilities that persist across deployments. Kubernetes RBAC, Pod Security Admission, and network policies provide the controls to enforce least privilege at the orchestration layer.

children.title: Centralized Security Logging
children.description: Centralized logging aggregates security events from all services into a searchable store, enabling detection of attack patterns that span multiple systems. Applications that log insufficiently — missing authentication failures, access denials, or privilege changes — leave investigators blind during incident response.

resources.label: OWASP Top 10 A09 – Security Logging Failures

children.title: Alerting & Anomaly Detection
children.description: Automated alerting on anomalies — unusual login locations, spiking error rates, unexpected data volumes — reduces dwell time, which is the period between breach and detection that averages over 200 days without active monitoring. Alerts must be tuned to balance signal-to-noise; alert fatigue from too many false positives causes responders to ignore real incidents.

security-dev.title: Incident Response & Compliance
security-dev.description: Prepare for when things go wrong. Build logging, an incident response plan, and awareness of compliance regimes.
security-dev.challenge: Add security logging to an app and write an incident response runbook for a credential leak.

children.title: SIEM Architecture & Use Cases
children.description: Security Information and Event Management systems correlate logs from firewalls, endpoints, and applications to detect attack patterns in real time using rules and machine-learning models. Without a SIEM, security teams lack the visibility to detect multi-stage attacks that touch many systems over days or weeks.

children.title: Incident Response Playbooks
children.description: Playbooks define step-by-step response procedures for specific incident types — ransomware, credential stuffing, data exfiltration — so teams respond consistently under pressure rather than improvising. Untested playbooks fail when needed most; tabletop exercises and regular drills validate them before real incidents occur.

children.title: Digital Forensics Fundamentals
children.description: Digital forensics preserves and analyzes evidence from compromised systems to determine what happened, how it happened, and what data was accessed or exfiltrated. Improper evidence collection — modifying timestamps or overwriting memory — can taint investigations and make root cause analysis impossible.

children.title: Business Continuity & Disaster Recovery
children.description: BC/DR plans define recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) to restore services after security incidents, system failures, or natural disasters. Organizations without tested recovery procedures find that backups are corrupt, offsite copies are missing, or restoration procedures take far longer than expected.

children.title: GDPR Data Protection Requirements
children.description: GDPR requires lawful basis for processing personal data, breach notification within 72 hours, data minimization, and the right to erasure — violations can result in fines up to 4% of global annual revenue. Developers directly affect GDPR compliance through logging practices, data retention policies, and encryption decisions.

children.title: ISO 27001 & SOC 2 Frameworks
children.description: ISO 27001 provides a framework for establishing an Information Security Management System with controls covering risk assessment, access management, and incident response; SOC 2 audits service organizations against trust service criteria. Achieving these certifications requires systematic evidence collection that forces organizations to actually implement the controls they claim to have.

children.title: PCI DSS for Payment Card Security
children.description: PCI DSS mandates specific controls for organizations that store, process, or transmit cardholder data, including encryption at rest and in transit, access logging, and regular penetration testing. Non-compliance results in fines from card brands and potential loss of the ability to process card payments — consequences that dwarf the cost of compliance.

children.title: HIPAA Security Rule for Healthcare Data
children.description: HIPAA's Security Rule requires administrative, physical, and technical safeguards for electronic Protected Health Information (ePHI), including access controls, audit logging, and encryption. Healthcare data breaches carry the highest per-record cost of any industry and trigger mandatory HHS notification.

graphql-dev.title: Backend & API Foundations
graphql-dev.description: Before GraphQL, you need a solid backend base. Understand HTTP, build a basic Node service, and know what REST does well so you can see what GraphQL improves.
graphql-dev.challenge: Build a tiny REST API, then list every place it over-fetches or needs multiple round trips.

children.title: HTTP Methods & Status Codes
children.description: Every GraphQL request travels over HTTP, so misunderstanding methods and status codes leads to silent failures — a 200 that hides a resolver error is a classic GraphQL gotcha. Knowing what the transport layer is doing lets you debug at the right layer instead of blaming the wrong tool.

children.title: Node.js Event Loop & Module System
children.description: GraphQL resolvers are async by design — every field resolution can return a Promise. Without understanding the Node.js event loop you will create accidental blocking code that stalls the entire request, and without the module system you cannot structure a scalable resolver tree.

children.title: Over-fetching & Under-fetching
children.description: REST endpoints return fixed shapes; clients that need less get too much (over-fetch) and clients that need more must call multiple endpoints (under-fetch). Understanding these two failure modes is the core motivation for GraphQL — without this mental model you will design GraphQL APIs that replicate the same REST anti-patterns.

children.title: When REST Still Wins
children.description: GraphQL is not a universal replacement for REST. File uploads, HTTP caching, and simple public APIs often work better as REST. Misapplying GraphQL to every use case adds complexity without benefit, so knowing the trade-offs prevents architectural mistakes before they get expensive.

children.title: Environment Variables & Config
children.description: Secrets hard-coded into source are a security vulnerability that leaks into version control and CI logs. Proper environment config separation (development, staging, production) prevents database credential leaks and lets the same image run in any environment without code changes.

children.title: npm Scripts & Project Structure
children.description: A well-organized project structure determines whether a GraphQL codebase stays maintainable as schema, resolvers, and data sources grow. Mixing schema definitions, business logic and database calls in single files causes circular dependencies and untestable code.

children.title: The GraphQL Specification
children.description: GraphQL is first a language specification, not a library. Understanding the spec means your mental model is not tied to any particular server implementation — when Apollo, Yoga, or Mercurius differ in behavior you can trace the discrepancy back to spec compliance rather than guessing.

children.title: Declarative Data Fetching
children.description: The key GraphQL insight is that clients declare exactly the data shape they need and the server fulfills it. If you misunderstand this shift from imperative REST calls to declarative queries, you end up wrapping REST endpoints in GraphQL resolvers one-to-one, which negates the whole benefit.

graphql-dev.title: GraphQL Fundamentals
graphql-dev.description: Learn the GraphQL query language and type system from the client's perspective before building a server.
graphql-dev.challenge: Use a public GraphQL API to write queries with variables, fragments and nested fields.

children.title: Fields, Arguments & Variables
children.description: Arguments make queries dynamic, but inlining raw values instead of using variables prevents query plan caching and opens the door to injection. Every production query should use typed variables — treating them as optional syntactic sugar leads to security vulnerabilities and poor server performance.

children.title: Fragments & Directives
children.description: Fragments eliminate duplication in complex queries and are the foundation for client-side query composition in frameworks like Relay. Misunderstanding them leads to repeated field selections across queries that get out of sync; misunderstanding directives like @skip and @include leads to over-fetching on the client.

children.title: Scalars & Nullability
children.description: In GraphQL every field is nullable by default — the opposite of TypeScript and most ORM conventions. A single null field in a non-null chain can bubble up and null out entire parent objects, causing cascading UI failures. Defining nullability intentionally is one of the most consequential schema decisions you will make.

children.title: Introspection & Tooling
children.description: Introspection is how GraphiQL, Apollo Sandbox, and code generators discover your schema at runtime. Disabling it in production is a security best practice, but you must understand what it exposes — full type information, descriptions, deprecations — before you can make an informed decision about when to restrict it.

children.title: Aliases & Operation Names
children.description: Aliases let you request the same field multiple times with different arguments in a single query — without them you cannot compare two users' profiles in one round trip. Operation names are mandatory in production for logging and tracing; anonymous operations make debugging nearly impossible.

children.title: Mutations vs Queries — Execution Order
children.description: Unlike queries, mutations execute serially in the order they appear in a document. Misunderstanding this means you might rely on serial execution for queries (which run in parallel) and introduce subtle race conditions, or conversely use queries when you need guaranteed ordering for write operations.

children.title: GraphQL Playground & GraphiQL
children.description: GraphiQL and Apollo Sandbox are the interactive consoles where you validate schema understanding before writing server code. Skipping hands-on exploration means you build resolvers against a mental model of the query language rather than its actual behavior, causing field-not-found and type-mismatch errors at runtime.

children.title: Understanding Errors in GraphQL Responses
children.description: GraphQL always returns HTTP 200 even when resolvers fail — errors live in the top-level errors array alongside a partial data object. Treating any 200 as success is the most common GraphQL client bug; you must always check the errors array to know whether the response is truly usable.

graphql-dev.title: Schema Design with SDL
graphql-dev.description: A GraphQL API is only as good as its schema. Design expressive, evolvable schemas with the Schema Definition Language.
graphql-dev.challenge: Design the schema for a blog with users, posts, comments and cursor-based pagination.

children.title: Object Types, Interfaces & Unions
children.description: Interfaces enforce a contract across multiple types and enable polymorphic queries — without them you duplicate fields across types and lose the ability to query heterogeneous lists uniformly. Unions let you return one of several unrelated types from a single field; confusing unions with interfaces leads to schemas where clients cannot distinguish return types without brittle hacks.

children.title: Input Types
children.description: Input types are separate from output types — you cannot reuse an object type as a mutation argument. This distinction exists because output types can have resolver functions and circular references while input types must be pure data. Mixing them up causes SDL parse errors that are confusing until you understand the reasoning.

children.title: Versionless API Design & Deprecation
children.description: GraphQL's promise of no versioning relies on additive changes and the @deprecated directive instead of v2 endpoints. Breaking this contract by removing or renaming fields without deprecation first breaks all existing clients silently; understanding the evolution strategy before you design fields prevents painful rework.

children.title: Schema-first vs Code-first
children.description: Schema-first keeps the SDL as the source of truth and generates types; code-first generates SDL from annotated code. Choosing wrong for your team creates friction — schema-first is harder to refactor in large TypeScript codebases, while code-first can drift from documentation if generation is skipped in CI.

children.title: Cursor-based Pagination (Relay Connections)
children.description: Offset pagination breaks when items are inserted or deleted between pages, causing duplicates or skipped rows. Cursor-based pagination using the Relay Connection spec is stable under concurrent mutations and is the de-facto standard for production GraphQL APIs — not knowing it forces a costly schema rewrite once data volumes grow.

children.title: Nullability in Pagination
children.description: A non-null list of non-null edges will propagate a null all the way up to the query root if a single edge resolver throws. Designing pagination fields with careful nullability — nullable edges, non-null pageInfo — means partial failures return usable data instead of a blank page.

children.title: Custom Scalars (Date, JSON, UUID)
children.description: Built-in scalars cover String, Int, Float, Boolean, and ID. Without custom scalars you serialize dates as untyped strings that any value can satisfy, bypassing type safety entirely. Custom scalars add runtime validation and serialization logic so bad data fails at the schema boundary instead of deep in business logic.

children.title: Enums — Type Safety vs Flexibility
children.description: Enums guarantee that only declared values are accepted, catching invalid strings at the schema layer. However, adding a new enum value is a breaking change for clients using exhaustive switch statements. Understanding this tension prevents you from over-using enums for things that will expand, and under-using them where data is truly categorical.

graphql-dev.title: Resolvers & Context
graphql-dev.description: Resolvers are where a schema meets your data. Learn the resolver chain, context, and how arguments flow through.
topics: Resolver signature — parent, args, context, info
graphql-dev.challenge: Implement resolvers for your blog schema against in-memory data, including nested post→author resolution.

children.title: The Four Resolver Arguments (parent, args, context, info)
children.description: Every resolver receives the same four arguments, and misusing any one of them is a common source of bugs. Ignoring parent means re-fetching data already loaded by the parent resolver; ignoring info means you cannot build query-aware data loaders that select only requested database columns.

children.title: Default Resolvers & Trivial Fields
children.description: When no resolver is defined for a field, GraphQL falls back to a default resolver that reads the property of the same name from the parent object.
