Goroutines are cheap (~2 KB stack) but not free; spawning millions without backpressure exhausts memory. The scheduler preempts at function call boundaries and I/O, so tight CPU loops with no calls can starve other goroutines.

children.title: Goroutine Leaks
children.description: A goroutine that blocks forever on a channel receive or send with no sender/receiver is a leak — it consumes memory and stack space silently for the lifetime of the process. The most common cause is launching goroutines that wait on a channel that is never closed or sent to. Always ensure goroutines have a defined exit path, typically via context cancellation.

children.title: Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels
children.description: An unbuffered channel synchronizes sender and receiver — send blocks until a receiver is ready and vice versa. A buffered channel allows sends up to its capacity without blocking. Choosing the wrong buffer size causes either deadlocks (buffer too small) or silent memory growth (unbounded work queues). Always size buffers based on a real throughput analysis.

children.title: Channel Direction & Closing
children.description: Channels can be typed as send-only (chan<-) or receive-only (<-chan) to enforce ownership at compile time. Only the sender should close a channel; closing from the receiver side or closing a closed channel panics. A closed channel returns the zero value immediately on receive, which is how fan-out pipelines signal completion to multiple workers.

children.title: sync.Mutex & Race Conditions
children.description: A Mutex protects shared state from concurrent access; reading or writing a variable from multiple goroutines without synchronization is a data race and produces undefined behavior. Go's -race flag instruments the binary to detect races at runtime but cannot catch all paths. Every exported method on a type that contains shared state must document its concurrency safety guarantees.

resources.label: Go Blog — Introducing the Go Race Detector

children.title: sync.WaitGroup
children.description: WaitGroup tracks how many goroutines are outstanding; Add must be called before the goroutine starts, not inside it, to avoid a race where Wait returns before the goroutine has been counted. Passing a WaitGroup by value (copying it) breaks it silently — always pass by pointer. Missing a Done call (e.g., on an early return) causes Wait to block forever.

children.title: context.Context Propagation
children.description: Context carries deadlines, cancellation signals, and request-scoped values through a call chain. It must be the first parameter by convention and must be propagated to every blocking call (HTTP, DB, channel). Ignoring the context means your service cannot respect client timeouts, leading to goroutine pileups and memory exhaustion under slow upstream dependencies.

resources.label: Go Blog — Go Concurrency Patterns: Context

children.title: select & Timeouts
children.description: select multiplexes channel operations; the first ready case runs. Combining select with time.After or context.Done implements timeouts and cancellation for any blocking operation. A select with only a default case is a non-blocking probe; a select with no cases blocks forever — useful only in rare sentinel goroutines.

go-dev.title: Building HTTP Services
go-dev.description: Build web services with Go's standard library, then add a router like Gin or Chi for ergonomics.
go-dev.challenge: Build a JSON REST API for a bookmarks service with routing, middleware and graceful shutdown.

children.title: Handler & HandlerFunc
children.description: Go's net/http is built on the http.Handler interface — a single ServeHTTP(ResponseWriter, *Request) method. http.HandlerFunc is a function adapter that lets plain functions satisfy the interface. Misunderstanding that ResponseWriter buffers output means writing the status code after writing the body silently fails; WriteHeader must be called before any Write.

children.title: Graceful Shutdown
children.description: http.Server.Shutdown drains active connections before closing; calling Close immediately kills in-flight requests. Without graceful shutdown, a rolling deploy drops requests that were mid-flight when the old pod was terminated. Shutdown must be paired with listening for SIGTERM/SIGINT via os/signal and a timeout context to avoid hanging indefinitely.

children.title: Chi Router Middleware Chain
children.description: Chi composes middleware as a stack of http.Handler wrappers called in order; each middleware calls next.ServeHTTP to continue the chain. Middleware that does not call next silently short-circuits the entire chain, causing subsequent handlers to never run. Understanding the middleware execution order is critical for getting auth, logging, and recovery in the right sequence.

children.title: Go 1.22 Enhanced ServeMux
children.description: Go 1.22 added method-based routing and path wildcards directly to net/http's ServeMux (e.g., GET /posts/{id}), reducing the need for third-party routers. Pattern precedence follows specificity rules — more specific patterns win over more general ones. Using an older router without knowing about ServeMux improvements adds unnecessary dependencies.

children.title: JSON Encoding & Decoding
children.description: encoding/json uses reflection to map struct fields to JSON keys via struct tags. json.Decoder.DisallowUnknownFields rejects payloads with extra keys, useful for strict API validation. Forgetting to close the request body (defer r.Body.Close()) causes connection-pool exhaustion under load because the underlying TCP connection cannot be reused.

children.title: HTTP Client & Transport
children.description: The default http.Client has no timeout — a slow server blocks a goroutine forever. Always set Timeout on the client. The underlying http.Transport manages a connection pool; creating a new http.Client per request bypasses the pool and spawns a new TCP connection each time, severely limiting throughput and causing port exhaustion.

children.title: Dependency Injection via Constructors
children.description: Go favors explicit constructor functions (NewServer, NewHandler) that accept dependencies as parameters rather than using a global registry or init-time side effects. This makes dependencies visible, testable, and swappable. Services built on package-level globals are impossible to test in parallel and fragile when initialization order matters.

children.title: Configuration & Environment Variables
children.description: Twelve-factor apps read configuration from environment variables, not hardcoded values or config files committed to version control. In Go, os.Getenv or libraries like godotenv load vars at startup; missing required vars should cause the program to fail fast with a clear message rather than silently using zero values that cause subtle runtime failures.

go-dev.title: Working with Databases
go-dev.description: Persist data from Go. Use database/sql and pgx for PostgreSQL, and generate type-safe queries with sqlc.
go-dev.challenge: Back your bookmarks API with PostgreSQL using sqlc-generated queries and migration files.

children.title: database/sql Connection Pool
children.description: database/sql maintains a pool of reusable database connections controlled by SetMaxOpenConns, SetMaxIdleConns, and SetConnMaxLifetime. Leaving these at defaults (unlimited open connections) allows connection storms under load that exhaust PostgreSQL's max_connections limit. Every query must also respect context cancellation or the pool drains under slow queries.

children.title: pgx vs database/sql
children.description: pgx is a PostgreSQL-specific driver that exposes native features (COPY protocol, listen/notify, pgtype) unavailable through the database/sql interface. Using pgx in database/sql compatibility mode gives you the driver but loses the PostgreSQL-specific API. Knowing when to use pgx's native pool (pgxpool) vs database/sql's pool matters for high-throughput applications.

children.title: sqlc Type-Safe Queries
children.description: sqlc generates Go code from annotated SQL files — queries are validated against the schema at generation time, not at runtime. This means a renamed column is a compile error, not a production panic. The tradeoff is that every query change requires regenerating code; teams that skip this step accumulate a mismatch between SQL and generated types.

children.title: SQL Injection & Parameterized Queries
children.description: Building SQL strings with fmt.Sprintf and user input is a SQL injection vulnerability. database/sql's Query and Exec accept placeholder parameters ($1, $2 for PostgreSQL) that the driver sends separately from the query, preventing injection. ORMs that build dynamic WHERE clauses from struct fields may still produce injections if map keys are interpolated unsafely.

children.title: Transactions & Rollback
children.description: A SQL transaction must be either committed or rolled back; a leaked transaction that is never closed holds a database lock until the connection is recycled, causing deadlocks under concurrent load. The idiomatic Go pattern is to defer tx.Rollback() immediately after Begin — Rollback after Commit is a no-op, so this is always safe.

children.title: Schema Migrations with golang-migrate
children.description: golang-migrate applies versioned up/down SQL migration files to evolve the schema safely. Without a migration tool, schema changes are applied manually, producing environment drift between dev, staging, and production. Migrations must be backward-compatible during rolling deploys — a column rename requires an add-then-drop sequence across two deploys.

children.title: Repository Interface Design
children.description: The repository pattern wraps data access behind an interface so HTTP handlers and business logic do not depend on a specific database. This makes unit testing trivial — tests inject a mock repository instead of a real database. Repositories that expose raw SQL or database-specific types (pgx.Rows) leak persistence concerns into the domain layer.

children.title: N+1 Query Problem
children.description: Fetching a list of records and then querying related data one-by-one in a loop produces N+1 database round-trips — one for the list plus one per item. This is invisible in tests with small datasets but causes seconds of latency in production. The fix is a JOIN or a batch IN clause. sqlc's :many query mode makes it easy to write the correct batch query.

go-dev.title: API Design, Middleware & Auth
go-dev.description: Turn a working service into a robust API with consistent design, authentication and request validation.
go-dev.challenge: Add JWT auth, input validation and rate limiting to the bookmarks API with consistent JSON errors.

children.title: JWT Structure & Validation
children.description: A JWT is a Base64url-encoded header.payload.signature triplet. The signature must be verified using the server's secret or public key on every request — accepting the alg:none attack or skipping signature verification allows any client to forge tokens. Always specify the expected algorithm explicitly and reject tokens whose alg header does not match.

children.title: Storing Tokens & Refresh Patterns
children.description: Short-lived access tokens (15 min) paired with longer-lived refresh tokens (7 days) limit the blast radius of a stolen token. Storing JWTs in localStorage exposes them to XSS; HttpOnly cookies are the recommended storage for browser clients. Refresh tokens stored in the database can be revoked immediately, unlike stateless JWTs which remain valid until expiry.

children.title: Middleware Execution Order
children.description: Middleware wraps handlers in a stack; the first registered middleware is the outermost wrapper. Recovery middleware must be outermost to catch panics from all downstream handlers. Auth middleware must run before any handler that requires authentication. Logging middleware placed outside auth records all requests including unauthenticated ones — intentional but often surprising.

children.title: Rate Limiting
children.description: Rate limiting protects services from brute-force and DDoS by capping requests per client over a time window. Token-bucket algorithms (e.g., golang.org/x/time/rate) allow bursting while enforcing an average rate. Rate limiters keyed only on IP are bypassed by distributed attacks; combining IP, user ID, and endpoint provides layered protection.

children.title: Validation with go-playground/validator
children.description: go-playground/validator inspects struct fields via struct tags (validate:"required,min=1,max=100") and returns detailed field-level errors. Validation must happen at the API boundary before business logic runs — validating after partial processing causes inconsistent state. Custom validators allow domain-specific rules (valid UUID, future date) without polluting handler code.

children.title: Structured Error Responses
children.description: Consistent JSON error envelopes (with a code, message, and optional details array) allow API clients to handle errors programmatically. Leaking internal error messages (database errors, stack traces) to clients exposes schema details and aids attackers. The handler should map domain errors to HTTP status codes in one place, not scattered across multiple layers.

children.title: CORS Configuration
children.description: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing headers tell browsers which origins may call your API. A wildcard Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * prevents credentialed requests (cookies, auth headers) and should never be used for authenticated APIs. The preflight OPTIONS request must return correct headers or the browser blocks the real request before it even reaches your handler.

children.title: Secrets Management
children.description: Hardcoding secrets in source code or committing .env files exposes credentials in version history even after removal. Use environment variables injected at deploy time or a secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager). In Go, reading secrets at startup and storing them in a config struct (not package-level globals) keeps secrets out of logs and stack traces.

go-dev.title: Testing & Benchmarking
go-dev.description: Write tests the idiomatic Go way — table-driven tests, mocks, benchmarks and coverage.
topics: Integration tests with a real database
go-dev.challenge: Add table-driven unit tests, httptest handler tests, and a benchmark to your API, running with -race.

children.title: Table-Driven Tests
children.description: Table-driven tests define a slice of test cases with inputs and expected outputs, then iterate with t.Run for named subtests. This pattern eliminates duplicated test setup, makes adding cases trivial, and produces clear failure messages identifying exactly which case failed. Code with large switch statements and conditional branches particularly benefits from exhaustive table coverage.

children.title: httptest for Handler Testing
children.description: net/http/httptest provides httptest.NewRecorder() and httptest.NewServer() to test HTTP handlers without binding a real port. Testing handlers directly through the http.Handler interface means middleware stacks are exercised exactly as they run in production. Using only unit tests for handlers without httptest misses serialization bugs, missing headers, and middleware interaction issues.

children.title: Interface Mocking
children.description: Go mocks are typically hand-rolled structs or generated via tools like mockery that implement the same interface as the real dependency. Because Go interfaces are small and implicit, mocking only requires implementing the methods you actually test. Over-mocking — mocking types you own — leads to tests that pass even when the real integration breaks.

children.title: The Race Detector
children.description: go test -race instruments the binary with ThreadSanitizer to detect concurrent access to shared variables without proper synchronization. Race conditions are non-deterministic — they may not appear in normal test runs. Running the race detector in CI catches races early; a race report identifies the exact goroutines and source lines involved, making them actionable.

children.title: Writing Benchmarks
children.description: Benchmark functions (BenchmarkXxx(b *testing.B)) run the code b.N times where N is determined by the framework to produce a stable measurement. The benchmarked code must use its results (assign to a package-level sink variable) to prevent the compiler from optimizing away the work via dead code elimination. Benchmarks without this precaution report meaningless sub-nanosecond timings.

children.title: Benchmark Comparison with benchstat
children.description: benchstat compares two benchmark runs statistically, reporting whether performance changes are significant or noise. Without statistical comparison, a 5% timing difference between two runs is indistinguishable from measurement variance. Using benchstat in CI on performance-sensitive packages prevents silent regressions from slipping through code review.

children.title: Testing with a Real Database
children.description: Integration tests that hit a real PostgreSQL instance (spun up via Docker or testcontainers-go) verify that SQL queries, transactions, and migrations work correctly together. Tests that only mock the repository miss query errors, constraint violations, and migration drift. Using t.Cleanup to truncate tables between tests keeps them isolated without a full schema rebuild.

children.title: Test Coverage & go tool cover
children.description: go test -coverprofile=coverage.out generates a coverage profile; go tool cover -html=coverage.out renders it as an annotated HTML file. Coverage percentage is a proxy metric — 100% line coverage can coexist with zero assertion coverage. Focus coverage efforts on critical paths (auth, payment, data mutation) rather than chasing a percentage target.

go-dev.title: Observability & Performance
go-dev.description: Make your service observable and fast. Add structured logging, metrics and tracing, and profile hot paths.
go-dev.challenge: Instrument the bookmarks API with slog, expose Prometheus metrics, and profile one endpoint with pprof.

children.title: pprof CPU & Memory Profiles
children.description: Go's pprof package exposes CPU, memory (heap), goroutine, and block profiles via HTTP or file output. CPU profiling uses sampling — it captures the call stack thousands of times per second to identify hot functions. Memory profiles show live allocations and their sites; misreading inuse_space vs alloc_objects leads to chasing allocations that are already collected rather than leaks.

children.title: Escape Analysis & Heap Allocations
children.description: The Go compiler's escape analysis determines whether a variable lives on the stack (cheap, zero GC cost) or escapes to the heap (subject to GC). go build -gcflags='-m' prints escape decisions. Returning a pointer to a local variable, storing in an interface, or passing to a goroutine forces heap allocation. Reducing allocations on hot paths directly reduces GC pause and CPU overhead.

children.title: log/slog (Go 1.21+)
children.description: slog is the standard library's structured logger; it emits key-value pairs in JSON or text format, making logs machine-parseable without a third-party library. Each log record carries a Level, Time, Message, and arbitrary attributes. Failing to propagate the logger via context or function parameters leads to package-level loggers that cannot be disabled in tests or redirected in production.

resources.label: Go Blog — Structured Logging with slog

children.title: Log Levels & Sampling
children.description: Over-logging at DEBUG level in production can consume more CPU than the business logic itself and fill disk faster than the application can serve traffic. Log sampling (log one in every N similar events) reduces volume while preserving signal. Structured logs with a consistent request ID field allow tracing a single request across thousands of concurrent log lines.

children.title: Prometheus Metrics in Go
children.description: The Prometheus Go client provides Counter, Gauge, Histogram, and Summary types. Histograms with pre-defined buckets measure request latency distributions; choosing inappropriate bucket boundaries (too coarse) hides SLA violations. Registering the same metric name twice panics at startup — use sync.Once or MustRegister in init to prevent duplicate registrations.

children.title: OpenTelemetry Tracing
children.description: OpenTelemetry traces a request as a tree of spans across service boundaries by propagating a trace context header (W3C traceparent). Without trace propagation in outgoing HTTP and database calls, spans appear as disconnected roots with no parent, making distributed debugging impossible. The SDK's sampler controls the fraction of traces captured; always-on sampling crushes the collector under production traffic.

children.title: sync.Pool for Allocation Reuse
children.description: sync.Pool recycles objects (byte buffers, encoder instances) across goroutines to reduce heap allocations on hot paths. The GC may clear the pool at any GC cycle, so objects must be re-initialized on Get. Storing objects with finalizers or large external resources in a Pool causes resource leaks when those objects are silently discarded by the GC.

children.title: Load Testing with k6
children.description: Load testing validates that your service meets throughput and latency targets under realistic concurrency. k6 scripts define virtual users and scenarios in JavaScript and report percentile latencies (p95, p99). Running load tests against a production-like environment with real database connections surfaces connection pool exhaustion and mutex contention that unit tests cannot reveal.

go-dev.title: Containers, gRPC & Deployment
go-dev.description: Ship your Go service. Build tiny container images, expose a gRPC interface, and automate deployment.
topics: Multi-stage Docker builds for tiny images
go-dev.challenge: Containerize the API with a multi-stage build, add a gRPC endpoint, and deploy via a GitHub Actions pipeline.

children.title: Multi-Stage Docker Builds
children.description: A multi-stage Dockerfile uses a Go builder image to compile the binary and a minimal base (scratch or distroless) for the final image, producing images under 10 MB vs hundreds of MB for a single-stage build. The final image must not include the Go toolchain, source code, or build secrets. Layer caching — copying go.mod/go.sum before source files — avoids re-downloading modules on every code change.

children.title: CGO & Static Binaries
children.description: Go produces statically linked binaries by default when CGO is disabled (CGO_ENABLED=0), enabling them to run in scratch (empty) containers. Enabling CGO links against the host's glibc and produces a binary that silently fails in Alpine or distroless containers that use musl libc. Always set CGO_ENABLED=0 in Dockerfiles unless a CGO dependency is explicitly required.

children.title: Protocol Buffers & Code Generation
children.description: gRPC uses Protocol Buffers as its IDL and wire format. The protoc compiler with protoc-gen-go and protoc-gen-go-grpc generates strongly typed client and server stubs. Editing generated files by hand causes them to be overwritten on the next generation; all customization belongs in the service implementation, not the generated interface code.

children.title: gRPC Streaming & Error Handling
children.description: gRPC supports unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, and bidirectional-streaming RPCs. Stream errors must be returned via stream.SendMsg / RecvMsg error checks — returning a nil error after partial data confuses clients. gRPC status codes (codes.NotFound, codes.Internal) must be used instead of HTTP status integers so clients across all languages interpret errors consistently.

children.title: GitHub Actions for Go
children.description: A Go CI workflow typically runs go vet, go test -race -coverprofile, golangci-lint, and docker build in sequence. Caching the Go module cache (~/.cache/go/pkg/mod) and the build cache (~/.cache/go/build) reduces pipeline time from minutes to seconds. Missing the -race flag in CI allows data races to ship undetected despite a passing test suite.

children.title: Zero-Downtime Deploys
children.description: Kubernetes rolling updates replace pods gradually; a new pod must pass readiness probes before the old one terminates. A Go service that is not ready to serve immediately after startup (e.g., it runs migrations synchronously at boot) will fail probes and prevent the deploy from completing. Health check endpoints (/healthz, /readyz) must distinguish between liveness and readiness.

children.title: Container Registries & Image Tagging
children.description: Docker images should be tagged with the Git commit SHA in addition to a mutable tag like latest, so every deployed image is traceable to an exact commit. Pushing only latest makes rollbacks ambiguous — you cannot tell which code version is running without inspecting the image layers. GitHub Container Registry (ghcr.io) integrates natively with GitHub Actions for token-free pushes.

children.title: Environment Parity & 12-Factor
children.description: Production bugs that cannot be reproduced locally are almost always caused by environment parity gaps — different OS, Go version, database version, or missing environment variables. Using the same Docker image in development, staging, and production eliminates the 'works on my machine' class of bugs. The 12-factor app methodology codifies environment parity as a first-class requirement.

rust-dev.title: Rust Fundamentals
rust-dev.description: Begin with Rust's syntax and the toolchain. Learn variables, types and control flow while getting comfortable with the compiler.
topics: Control flow — if, loop, while, for
rust-dev.challenge: Build a CLI temperature converter that parses arguments and handles invalid input gracefully.

children.title: rustc & the Compilation Model
children.description: Rust is an ahead-of-time compiled language — rustc turns your source into machine code with no runtime interpreter. Understanding how rustc parses, type-checks, borrow-checks, and then lowers to LLVM IR gives you a mental model for why the compiler catches entire classes of bugs at compile time rather than at runtime, and helps you read the long, structured error messages the compiler produces.

children.title: Cargo — The Build System & Package Manager
children.description: Cargo is the single tool that handles building, testing, running, linting, and publishing Rust projects. Not understanding Cargo profiles (dev vs release), the lock-file, and feature flags leads to shipping debug builds to production (10x slower), dependency hell, and inability to conditionally compile platform-specific code.

children.title: Variables, Mutability & Shadowing
children.description: Rust variables are immutable by default; you must opt into mutation with `mut`. Shadowing lets you re-bind a name with a different type in the same scope without `mut`, which avoids accidental mutation bugs. Confusing shadowing with mutation is a common early mistake that produces subtle logic errors when the original binding is expected to persist.

children.title: Types, Expressions & Control Flow
children.description: In Rust almost everything is an expression — `if`, `match`, and blocks all return values. Treating statements as expressions (missing semicolons) or misreading integer overflow behavior (panic in debug, wrap in release) are early foot-guns that cause unexpected compile errors or silent data corruption until you internalize how the type system and expression model interact.

children.title: Structured Compiler Diagnostics
children.description: Rust's compiler errors include error codes (E0xxx), source spans, and suggested fixes. Learning to read the full diagnostic — not just the first line — is the single fastest way to progress as a Rust beginner. Developers who ignore the `help:` and `note:` lines spend hours on problems the compiler already diagnosed and solved for them.

children.title: Rust Playground — Rapid Prototyping
children.description: The official Rust Playground lets you compile and run snippets in the browser without any local setup. Using it to isolate a compiler error from a larger project is the fastest way to understand whether a problem is a language misunderstanding or a project-specific issue, and sharing playground links is the standard way to ask for help in the Rust community.

children.title: crates.io & docs.rs
children.description: crates.io is Rust's official package registry and docs.rs automatically hosts API documentation for every published crate. Not knowing how to evaluate a crate (download count, recent maintenance, feature flags) leads to taking on unmaintained or unsound dependencies that later break your build or introduce vulnerabilities.

children.title: Clippy & rustfmt
children.description: Clippy is Rust's official linter with hundreds of machine-applicable suggestions that catch non-idiomatic code, potential panics, and performance issues. Running `cargo clippy` from day one and enforcing `rustfmt` formatting in CI prevents an entire category of code-review comments and bakes best practices in while muscle memory is still forming.

rust-dev.title: Ownership, Borrowing & Lifetimes
rust-dev.description: The heart of Rust. Understand ownership, references and lifetimes — the rules that give Rust memory safety without a garbage collector.
rust-dev.challenge: Write a function library that manipulates strings and vectors using only borrows — no unnecessary clones.

children.title: Ownership Rules & the Drop Model
children.description: Every value in Rust has exactly one owner, and when the owner goes out of scope the value is dropped — freeing memory without a garbage collector. Misunderstanding that assignment moves ownership (not copies) leads to `use of moved value` compiler errors that confuse beginners who expect C++ copy-by-default semantics, and not understanding Drop causes resource leaks when wrapping OS handles.

children.title: Copy vs Clone
children.description: Types that implement `Copy` are duplicated bit-for-bit on assignment (integers, booleans, shared references), while non-Copy types are moved. Calling `.clone()` on large heap-allocated types like `String` or `Vec` in a tight loop causes unnecessary allocations that show up as heap pressure in profilers — understanding when a borrow suffices versus when a clone is truly needed is the key to writing allocation-efficient Rust.

resources.label: The Rust Book — Stack vs Heap

children.title: Shared & Mutable References
children.description: Rust enforces that you may have many shared references (`&T`) or exactly one mutable reference (`&mut T`) at any point in time, but never both simultaneously. Violating this rule — even in single-threaded code — produces borrow checker errors, because allowing concurrent mutation and aliasing is the root cause of iterator invalidation, use-after-free, and data-race bugs in other languages.

children.title: Slices — Borrowing Contiguous Sequences
children.description: Slices (`&[T]`, `&str`) are fat pointers — a reference plus a length — that borrow a contiguous region of memory without copying it. Returning a `String` where a `&str` would suffice, or indexing past slice bounds (a runtime panic in debug, undefined behavior behind `get_unchecked`), are the two most common slice mistakes and both have straightforward borrow-based fixes.

children.title: Lifetime Annotations & Elision
children.description: Lifetimes are compile-time labels that prove a reference will not outlive the data it points to. The compiler infers most lifetimes via elision rules, but when a function returns a reference derived from multiple inputs you must annotate explicitly so the borrow checker can track which input the output borrows from. Skipping this understanding leads to baffling `lifetime does not live long enough` errors that cannot be fixed by trial-and-error alone.

children.title: Lifetime Bounds in Structs
children.description: When a struct holds a reference it must declare a lifetime parameter so the compiler can enforce that the struct cannot outlive the data it borrows. Forgetting struct lifetime annotations or writing them incorrectly produces `missing lifetime specifier` errors and can force awkward ownership refactors — understanding them up front lets you design data structures that compose cleanly with the borrow checker.

resources.label: The Rust Book — Lifetime Annotations in Structs

children.title: Box<T> — Heap Allocation
children.description: `Box<T>` is the simplest heap-allocation primitive in Rust — it moves a value onto the heap and gives you a single-owner pointer. It is essential for recursive data structures (which have infinite stack size without indirection) and for trait objects. Not understanding when the compiler requires boxing leads to `recursive type has infinite size` compile errors and misuse of `clone` as a workaround.

children.title: Stack Overflows & Recursive Algorithms
children.description: Rust does not perform tail-call optimization by default, so deeply recursive functions will overflow the stack at runtime — a panic that is hard to diagnose without knowing the default 8 MB stack limit. Understanding when to use iterative algorithms, `stacker`, or explicit heap-allocated state machines prevents production panics on large inputs that pass unit tests with small data.

rust-dev.title: Traits, Generics & Error Handling
rust-dev.description: Write reusable, expressive Rust. Abstract behavior with traits and generics, and handle failure with Result and the ? operator.
rust-dev.challenge: Build a config parser that returns a custom error enum and propagates failures with the ? operator.

children.title: Defining & Implementing Traits
children.description: Traits are Rust's primary abstraction mechanism — they define shared behavior that types must implement, similar to interfaces. Not understanding trait coherence (the orphan rule) leads to `E0117: only traits defined in the current crate can be implemented` errors when you try to implement a foreign trait for a foreign type, which is a wall beginners hit when working with third-party crates.

children.title: Generics & Monomorphization
children.description: Generic functions and structs are monomorphized at compile time — the compiler stamps out a concrete version for every type combination used. This gives zero-cost abstractions (no virtual dispatch overhead) but can increase binary size. Confusing generic bounds with runtime polymorphism leads to writing `Box<dyn Trait>` everywhere when static dispatch would be more efficient.

children.title: Trait Objects — dyn Trait
children.description: Trait objects (`&dyn Trait`, `Box<dyn Trait>`) enable runtime polymorphism through a vtable pointer, at the cost of one level of indirection and the requirement that the trait is object-safe. Using trait objects when generics would suffice incurs unnecessary heap allocation; using generics when you need a heterogeneous collection causes `the size of X cannot be known at compile time` errors.

children.title: Common Standard-Library Traits
children.description: Traits like `Display`, `Debug`, `From`/`Into`, `Iterator`, and `Deref` are the lingua franca of idiomatic Rust. Not implementing `From` for your error types forces callers to use verbose `.map_err()` chains; not deriving `Debug` makes types unprintable in test failures; misusing `Deref` to fake inheritance creates surprising coercion behavior that confuses code reviewers.

children.title: Result<T, E> & the ? Operator
children.description: `Result<T, E>` makes failure an explicit part of a function's type signature, forcing callers to handle errors at compile time. The `?` operator short-circuits on `Err`, automatically converting the error type via `From`. Reaching for `.unwrap()` in non-test code is the most common beginner mistake — it compiles but panics at runtime on any error path, which is equivalent to an unchecked exception crash.

children.title: Custom Error Types with thiserror
children.description: Defining a custom error enum with `thiserror` lets you express all failure modes of a library in one place, derive `Display` and `Error` automatically, and give callers a typed surface to match on. Using `Box<dyn Error>` or `anyhow` in library code instead forces downstream users into untyped error handling; understanding when each approach is appropriate prevents API design mistakes that are painful to fix after publishing.

children.title: match, if let & while let
children.description: Rust's `match` is exhaustive — the compiler rejects non-exhaustive matches, guaranteeing every case is handled. `if let` and `while let` are syntactic sugar for single-pattern matches. Relying on a wildcard `_` arm to silence exhaustiveness warnings hides newly added enum variants from the type checker, causing silent logic errors when an enum grows.

children.title: Enums as Algebraic Data Types
children.description: Rust enums are full algebraic data types — each variant can carry different data, making them the idiomatic way to model state machines, ASTs, and error hierarchies. Using a struct with an enum `kind` field instead of a proper enum-with-data duplicates the type system's expressivity and leads to runtime checks for invalid state combinations that the compiler could have rejected at compile time.

rust-dev.title: Collections, Iterators & Closures
rust-dev.description: Use Rust's powerful standard library. Chain iterators, capture environment with closures, and pick the right collection.
rust-dev.challenge: Build a word-frequency counter that reads a file and prints the top 10 words using iterator chains.

children.title: Vec<T> — Growable Arrays
children.description: `Vec<T>` is a contiguous, heap-allocated, growable array and the most-used collection in Rust. Not pre-allocating with `Vec::with_capacity` when the final size is known causes repeated reallocations (each doubling the buffer and copying elements), which shows up as cache-miss-heavy allocator pressure in profiles. Accessing out-of-bounds with indexing panics; using `.get()` returns an `Option` and is the safe alternative.

children.title: HashMap<K, V> & HashSet<T>
children.description: Rust's `HashMap` uses a cryptographically secure hasher (SipHash) by default to prevent hash-flooding DoS attacks, at a small performance cost. Switching to `FxHashMap` or `AHashMap` for hot paths where input is not attacker-controlled is a common optimization. Not understanding that `HashMap::entry` avoids a double-lookup is a frequent source of verbose, inefficient map-update code.

children.title: The Iterator Trait & Lazy Adapters
children.description: Rust iterators are lazy — calling `.map()`, `.filter()`, or `.chain()` builds a pipeline description without executing anything until a consuming adapter like `.collect()` or `.for_each()` is called. Materializing intermediate collections with `.collect()` inside a chain wastes memory; not understanding laziness leads to writing for-loops where a composable iterator chain would be both shorter and faster.

children.title: IntoIterator & Custom Iterators
children.description: Implementing `Iterator` on your own types — with just a `next()` method — unlocks the entire adapter library for free. `IntoIterator` allows your type to be used in `for` loops directly. Not implementing these traits forces users of your type into clunky index loops or manual element extraction, making your API feel un-Rusty and harder to compose with the rest of the ecosystem.

children.title: Fn, FnMut & FnOnce Bounds
children.description: Closures in Rust capture their environment and are classified by how they do so: `Fn` borrows immutably, `FnMut` borrows mutably, and `FnOnce` takes ownership and can only be called once. Passing a closure that mutates captured state to an API that requires `Fn` (e.g., `Mutex::new_fn`) produces a compiler error; not understanding the distinction leads to fighting the borrow checker instead of choosing the right capture mode.

children.title: move Closures & Thread Safety
children.description: The `move` keyword forces a closure to take ownership of all captured variables rather than borrowing them. This is required when spawning threads or async tasks — the runtime cannot guarantee the captured references stay valid after the spawning scope exits, and the compiler rejects non-`move` closures that capture non-`'static` references in those contexts.

children.title: Rc<T> & RefCell<T>
children.description: `Rc<T>` provides shared ownership via reference counting for single-threaded contexts. Pairing it with `RefCell<T>` gives interior mutability — the ability to mutate through a shared reference, with borrow-rule enforcement moved from compile time to runtime (a `BorrowMutError` panic). Reaching for `Rc<RefCell<T>>` in multi-threaded code causes compile errors because `Rc` is not `Send`; the correct replacement is `Arc<Mutex<T>>`.

resources.label: The Rust Book — Rc and RefCell

children.title: Arc<T> & Mutex<T> for Shared State
children.description: `Arc<T>` is the thread-safe reference-counted pointer, and `Mutex<T>` provides mutual exclusion. Together (`Arc<Mutex<T>>`) they are the standard pattern for shared mutable state across threads in Rust. Holding a `MutexGuard` across an `.await` point in async code causes a deadlock or compile error (`MutexGuard is not Send`) — use `tokio::sync::Mutex` or drop the guard before awaiting.

rust-dev.title: Cargo, Crates & Modules
rust-dev.description: Organize and share real Rust projects. Master the module system, workspaces and the crate ecosystem.
rust-dev.challenge: Split your word-counter into a library + binary crate, add serde and clap, and document the public API.

children.title: mod, pub & use — Visibility & Paths
children.description: Rust's module system controls visibility with `pub`, `pub(crate)`, and `pub(super)`, giving fine-grained access control within a crate. Making everything `pub` to silence compiler errors defeats encapsulation and leaks implementation details into the public API; not understanding `use` aliasing leads to long, verbose paths repeated throughout files.

children.title: File-Based Module Layout
children.description: Rust resolves `mod foo;` to either `foo.rs` or `foo/mod.rs`, and the directory structure directly mirrors the module tree. Placing files in the wrong location or forgetting to declare a `mod` in the parent module produces `file not found for module` errors; understanding the layout rules is essential before a project grows beyond a single file.

resources.label: The Rust Book — Separating Modules into Files

children.title: Workspaces — Multi-Crate Projects
children.description: A Cargo workspace groups multiple crates under one root `Cargo.toml`, sharing a single `Cargo.lock` and `target` directory. Not using workspaces in a monorepo forces duplicate dependency resolution and separate `target` folders that multiply build times. Understanding workspace-level dependency inheritance (introduced in Cargo 1.64) prevents version drift between sibling crates.

children.title: Features & Conditional Compilation
children.description: Cargo features let you gate optional functionality behind compile-time flags, enabling smaller binaries for embedded targets and optional heavy dependencies like TLS. Not understanding `default-features = false` causes accidental transitive dependency bloat; enabling the wrong feature combination can produce incompatible symbols and linker errors that are hard to diagnose without reading `cargo tree`.

children.title: serde — Serialization & Deserialization
children.description: serde is Rust's de-facto serialization framework, supporting JSON, TOML, YAML, MessagePack, and dozens of formats through a single derive macro. Not understanding serde's attribute system (`rename`, `skip`, `flatten`, `default`) leads to brittle hand-written `From` implementations; misusing `#[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]` causes deserialization failures when API partners add new fields.

children.title: tracing — Structured Diagnostics
children.description: The `tracing` crate provides structured, leveled logging with spans that carry contextual metadata through async call stacks — something `println!` and the `log` crate cannot do. Not switching to `tracing` in async code means log lines from concurrent requests interleave with no correlation ID, making production debugging impossible without distributed tracing infrastructure.

children.title: rustdoc & Doc Tests
children.description: rustdoc generates HTML documentation from `///` comments and, uniquely, compiles and runs every code example in the docs as a test. Doc tests prevent documentation from going stale — a code example that no longer compiles breaks the CI test suite. Not knowing how to write doc tests means shipped crates often have broken examples that erode user trust.

children.title: Publishing to crates.io
children.description: Publishing a crate is permanent — versions cannot be deleted, only yanked (which advises against use but does not prevent it). Not understanding semantic versioning leads to breaking changes in patch releases that break dependents silently; not setting `publish = false` on internal workspace crates causes accidental public releases of private code.

rust-dev.title: Async Rust with Tokio
rust-dev.description: Write high-performance async Rust. Understand futures, the async/await model, and the Tokio runtime.
rust-dev.challenge: Build an async TCP echo server with Tokio that handles many concurrent connections.

children.title: Futures & the Async/Await Model
children.description: A Rust `Future` is a state machine that represents a value not yet computed — it does nothing until polled by an executor. The `async`/`await` syntax desugars into these state machines at compile time, meaning there is no heap allocation per await point in the common case. Misunderstanding that async functions are lazy (they don't start running until `.await`ed or spawned) is the most common async beginner mistake and causes subtle bugs where work silently never executes.

children.title: The Tokio Runtime
children.description: Tokio is a multi-threaded async runtime that schedules futures onto a thread pool, provides async I/O via epoll/kqueue/IOCP, and includes timers and channels. Not understanding that blocking synchronous calls (file I/O, `std::thread::sleep`, CPU-heavy work) inside async tasks stall the entire thread they run on — starving other tasks — leads to throughput collapses under load that are invisible in development.

children.title: tokio::spawn, join! & select!
children.description: `tokio::spawn` runs a future concurrently on the runtime's thread pool; `join!` awaits multiple futures concurrently in the same task; `select!` races futures and proceeds with whichever completes first. Confusing concurrent execution with parallelism (spawned tasks run in parallel, `join!` does not) leads to either under-utilizing CPU cores or introducing unnecessary overhead through spawning for every small operation.

children.title: Async Channels — mpsc & broadcast
children.description: Tokio provides async-aware channels (`mpsc`, `oneshot`, `broadcast`, `watch`) that integrate with the scheduler without blocking threads. Using `std::sync::mpsc` inside async code blocks the thread; not back-pressuring a `broadcast` channel causes receivers to miss messages when the buffer overflows, which is a data-loss bug rather than a crash.

children.title: tokio::task::spawn_blocking
children.description: `spawn_blocking` offloads synchronous, blocking work to a dedicated thread pool so it does not stall the async executor. Calling blocking functions like database drivers without async support, synchronous file I/O, or CPU-intensive cryptography directly in async tasks causes the entire thread to be occupied, preventing other tasks from making progress — this manifests as sudden latency spikes correlated with CPU-heavy operations.

children.title: Send Bounds in Async Code
children.description: Tokio's multi-threaded runtime requires spawned futures to be `Send` — meaning all data held across `.await` points must be safe to move between threads. This produces the infamous `future is not Send` compile error when a `Rc`, `RefCell`, or non-Send type is held across an await. Understanding which types are `Send` and how to restructure code to satisfy the bound prevents hours of confusing compiler diagnostics.

children.title: tokio_stream & the Stream Trait
children.description: A `Stream` is the async equivalent of `Iterator` — it yields items asynchronously and integrates with `while let Some(item) = stream.next().await` or adapter chains from `StreamExt`. Not consuming a stream — leaving it unpolled — causes the producer to block indefinitely if the channel is bounded, creating a deadlock that is silent and hard to diagnose without understanding the flow of backpressure.

children.title: Async Timeouts & Cancellation
children.description: Dropping a future in Rust cancels it — the async state machine is simply deallocated. `tokio::time::timeout` wraps a future and resolves to `Err(Elapsed)` if it does not complete in time, triggering cancellation of the inner future. Not understanding that cancellation unwinds at the nearest `.await` point (not at an arbitrary location) leads to partial work being abandoned and resources like database transactions being left in inconsistent states.

rust-dev.title: Web Services with Axum & Actix
rust-dev.description: Build production web services in Rust. Create routes, extractors and middleware with Axum or Actix Web.
rust-dev.challenge: Build a JSON REST API for a link shortener with Axum, including extractors and typed error responses.

children.title: Routing & Handlers
children.description: Axum routes are composable — you build a `Router` by layering `get`, `post`, and nested routers, each resolving to an async handler function. Axum's type-driven design means a handler signature mismatch (wrong extractor types) is a compile error, not a runtime panic. Not understanding how Axum resolves extractors from the request state leads to `FromRequest` trait errors that are resolved by reading the extractor documentation, not by guessing.

children.title: Extractors — Path, Query & Json
children.description: Axum extractors parse request components into typed Rust values: `Path<T>` decodes URL segments, `Query<T>` decodes query strings, and `Json<T>` deserializes the body via serde. Placing extractors in the wrong order (the body extractor must be last because consuming the body is destructive) causes a compile error; using `String` where a typed extractor would catch invalid input pushes validation errors to runtime.

children.title: Tower Layers & Middleware
children.description: Axum is built on Tower's `Service` trait, and middleware is applied as layers wrapping the router. Understanding the `Layer` / `Service` model lets you compose authentication, rate limiting, compression, and tracing as reusable units. Adding middleware in the wrong order (e.g., tracing after authentication) means request context is not available in the outer layer, producing logs that lack correlation IDs.

children.title: Application State & Shared Data
children.description: Axum passes shared state to handlers via the `State<T>` extractor, which requires the state to be `Clone + Send + Sync`. Wrapping mutable state in `Arc<Mutex<T>>` and storing it in app state is the idiomatic pattern. Storing non-`Clone` types directly in state, or holding `std::sync::MutexGuard` across an await (common when porting synchronous code), causes compile errors that require restructuring the state model.

children.title: Actix Web — Actor Model & App Data
children.description: Actix Web uses its own actor-based runtime and registers shared state via `web::Data<T>` (an `Arc` wrapper). Unlike Axum, Actix Web is not built on Tower, so middleware is written with its own `Transform`/`Service` traits. Mixing Actix and Tokio primitives (e.g., using `tokio::sync::Mutex` inside an Actix actor) can cause runtime panics if the Actix executor does not support the Tokio runtime context.

children.title: Error Handling in Web Handlers
children.description: Both Axum and Actix Web require handler errors to implement `IntoResponse` (Axum) or `ResponseError` (Actix). Returning a bare `String` error leaks implementation details to clients; not mapping internal errors to appropriate HTTP status codes produces 500s for recoverable conditions like `NotFound`. Implementing a unified application error type that converts to a JSON error body is the production-grade pattern.

children.title: Typed JSON Request & Response Bodies
children.description: Defining dedicated `#[derive(Deserialize)]` request structs and `#[derive(Serialize)]` response structs for each endpoint creates a compile-time contract between your handler logic and the wire format. Using `serde_json::Value` for request parsing defers validation to runtime and moves type errors from compile time to customer-facing 500s; investing in typed DTOs pays dividends in test coverage and refactoring safety.

children.title: OpenAPI Documentation with utoipa
children.description: `utoipa` generates OpenAPI 3 specifications from Rust types and handler annotations at compile time, eliminating the drift that occurs when hand-written specs fall out of sync with implementation. Without API documentation, integration partners are forced to reverse-engineer your endpoints; outdated specs are often worse than none because they confidently describe the wrong behavior.

rust-dev.title: Databases with SQLx & Diesel
rust-dev.description: Persist data safely. Use SQLx for compile-time-checked queries or Diesel's ORM against PostgreSQL.
rust-dev.challenge: Back your link shortener with PostgreSQL using SQLx, with migrations and a connection pool.

children.title: Compile-Time Query Verification
children.description: SQLx's `sqlx::query!` macro verifies SQL syntax and column types against a live database at compile time, turning SQL typos and schema drift into build failures rather than production errors. Not running `cargo sqlx prepare` to snapshot query metadata for offline builds causes CI failures when the database is not available; skipping type checking by using `query()` instead of `query!()` re-introduces the runtime type-mismatch errors SQLx was designed to eliminate.

children.title: Connection Pooling with PgPool
children.description: SQLx's `PgPool` manages a pool of persistent database connections that are reused across requests, avoiding the 50–100 ms TCP + TLS + PostgreSQL handshake cost per query. Creating a new connection per request (a common beginner pattern) causes connection exhaustion under load and triggers PostgreSQL's `max_connections` limit, resulting in `too many connections` errors that look like a database outage.

children.title: Diesel Schema & DSL
children.description: Diesel generates a Rust DSL from your database schema that catches SQL errors at compile time through the type system — invalid column references and type mismatches are build failures. The `diesel_migrations` crate manages schema migrations embedded in the binary. Not running `diesel migration run` before building causes `table not found` panics at startup; not understanding `schema.rs` regeneration after migrations causes stale column definitions.

children.title: Transactions & Error Recovery
children.description: Both SQLx and Diesel expose transaction APIs that map to BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK. SQLx uses an async closure pattern; Diesel uses a synchronous callback. Not wrapping multi-step writes in a transaction causes partial failure states where some rows are inserted and others are not — a consistency violation that is expensive to detect and repair in production data.

children.title: SQLx Migrations
children.description: SQLx's built-in migration runner applies versioned SQL files from a `migrations/` directory and records applied migrations in a `_sqlx_migrations` table. Running migrations at application startup (via `sqlx::migrate!().run(&pool).await`) ensures the schema is always in sync with the code. Not checking migration checksums — which SQLx validates on every run — lets silently edited migration files create schema drift across environments.

children.title: Row Mapping & FromRow
children.description: SQLx's `#[derive(FromRow)]` maps database rows to Rust structs by column name, with compile-time checking when using `query_as!`. Mismatching column names between the struct fields and the query (e.g., snake_case vs camelCase) causes `column not found` runtime errors; using `Option<T>` for nullable columns is required — mapping a nullable column to a non-Option type panics on `NULL` rows.

children.title: N+1 Queries & Batch Loading
children.description: The N+1 query problem occurs when you fetch N parent records and then issue one query per parent to fetch its children — instead of a single JOIN or `WHERE id = ANY($1)`. In an ORM-free environment like SQLx it is easy to accidentally write N+1 loops because there is no automatic lazy loading; recognizing the pattern and replacing it with a batch query or JOIN typically yields 10x–100x latency improvements on large datasets.

children.title: PostgreSQL EXPLAIN ANALYZE
children.description: `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` shows the actual execution plan PostgreSQL chose for a query, including whether it used an index, the estimated vs actual row counts, and where time was spent. Not using EXPLAIN before declaring a query fast leads to shipping queries that perform sequential scans on million-row tables; understanding the output is the foundational database performance skill regardless of which Rust database library you use.

rust-dev.title: Testing, Benchmarking & Unsafe
rust-dev.description: Ensure correctness and speed. Write tests and benchmarks, and learn when (and when not) to reach for unsafe.
rust-dev.challenge: Add an integration test suite and a criterion benchmark to your API, then profile the hot path.

children.title: Unit & Integration Tests
children.description: Rust has first-class test support: `#[test]` marks unit tests co-located with source code in `#[cfg(test)]` modules, while integration tests live in the `tests/` directory and test only the public API. Not understanding the difference — unit tests have access to private internals, integration tests do not — leads to either over-testing implementation details or missing integration-level regressions.

children.title: Property-Based Testing with proptest
children.description: Property-based testing generates hundreds of random inputs to falsify invariants rather than testing specific cases. `proptest` shrinks failing inputs to the minimal reproducing example automatically. Relying solely on hand-written examples misses edge cases like empty strings, integer overflow boundaries, and Unicode edge cases that property testing finds routinely; it is especially valuable for parsing and serialization code.

children.title: Criterion.rs — Statistical Benchmarking
children.description: Criterion runs each benchmark many times, applies statistical analysis to filter noise, and reports throughput with confidence intervals. Using `cargo bench` with the built-in (unstable) test harness instead gives single-shot timings that vary wildly between runs. Not understanding that Criterion compares against a saved baseline means you can accidentally regress performance and not notice until users complain.

children.title: Profiling with cargo-flamegraph
children.description: `cargo-flamegraph` generates a flame graph from `perf` (Linux) or `DTrace` (macOS) data, showing where CPU time is actually spent. Benchmarking without profiling tells you a function is slow but not why; flame graphs reveal whether the bottleneck is the algorithm, allocator pressure, cache misses, or a surprising third-party crate deep in the call stack.

children.title: The unsafe Keyword & Invariants
children.description: `unsafe` does not disable the borrow checker — it unlocks five specific additional capabilities: dereferencing raw pointers, calling `unsafe` functions, accessing mutable statics, implementing `unsafe` traits, and accessing union fields. Misusing `unsafe` does not produce a compiler error; it produces undefined behavior that manifests as memory corruption, data races, or security vulnerabilities in release builds long after the code was written.

children.title: FFI — Calling C from Rust
children.description: Rust's FFI lets you call C libraries by declaring `extern "C"` function signatures and linking against the library. Every call through FFI is implicitly `unsafe` because the C code does not uphold Rust's safety invariants. Not matching the C function signature exactly (wrong types, wrong calling convention) causes undefined behavior; not handling null pointers returned from C causes panics or memory corruption.

children.title: Miri — Undefined Behavior Detector
children.description: Miri is an interpreter for Rust's mid-level IR that detects undefined behavior in `unsafe` code: invalid memory access, uninitialized reads, and violations of Rust's aliasing rules. Running `cargo miri test` on code that contains `unsafe` blocks catches bugs that compile cleanly and pass normal tests but would corrupt memory in production. Not running Miri on `unsafe` code means UB is discovered only after a production incident.

children.title: AddressSanitizer & ThreadSanitizer
children.description: Rust supports LLVM sanitizers via `RUSTFLAGS=-Zsanitizer=address cargo +nightly test`. AddressSanitizer detects heap-use-after-free and buffer overflows that Miri misses in code that links to C libraries; ThreadSanitizer detects data races in multi-threaded code. Not running sanitizers in CI for crates with `unsafe` or FFI code means race conditions and memory errors survive code review and reach production.

rust-dev.title: WebAssembly & Production Deployment
rust-dev.description: Take Rust to the browser and to production. Compile to WebAssembly and deploy a lean, fast Rust service.
rust-dev.challenge: Compile a Rust module to WebAssembly and call it from a web page, then containerize your API for deployment.

children.title: Compiling to WebAssembly
children.description: Rust compiles to the `wasm32-unknown-unknown` target, producing a `.wasm` binary that runs in browsers and server-side WASM runtimes like Wasmtime. WASM modules share no memory with the JavaScript host by default — all data must cross the boundary through a linear memory buffer. Not understanding this boundary causes panics when Rust code tries to call browser APIs directly without going through wasm-bindgen.

children.title: wasm-bindgen — JS Interop
children.description: `wasm-bindgen` generates JavaScript glue code that lets Rust functions accept and return JavaScript values like strings, arrays, and objects across the WASM boundary. Not using `wasm-bindgen` forces manual memory layout work; not annotating the correct JS types for function arguments causes silent type coercion errors in the generated JavaScript that are hard to trace back to the Rust source.

children.title: wasm-pack — Build & Publish
children.description: `wasm-pack build` compiles, runs wasm-bindgen, and produces an npm-compatible package with TypeScript type definitions. Not using wasm-pack means manually writing the glue code it generates; not specifying the correct `--target` (web, bundler, nodejs) produces a package that works in one environment and silently fails in another because the module loading mechanism differs.

children.title: WASM Binary Size Optimization
children.description: WebAssembly binaries include the Rust standard library by default, making naive builds 1–5 MB. Using `opt-level = 'z'`, `lto = true`, `wasm-opt` from Binaryen, and stripping debug symbols reduces this to tens of kilobytes for typical utility modules. Shipping unoptimized WASM to the browser inflates page load time and is one of the most common performance mistakes in Rust-to-WASM projects.

children.title: Multi-Stage Docker Builds
children.description: A multi-stage Dockerfile compiles Rust in a builder image with the full toolchain and then copies only the static binary into a minimal runtime image (e.g., `gcr.io/distroless/cc` or `scratch`). Not using multi-stage builds ships a ~2 GB image containing the full Rust toolchain instead of a ~20 MB image with just the binary, which slows registry pushes, Kubernetes pod startup, and increases attack surface.

children.title: Cross-Compilation & Static Binaries
children.description: Rust supports cross-compilation via `--target` and the `cross` tool, which uses Docker to provide the correct linker and sysroot. Compiling with `--target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl` produces a fully static binary with no glibc dependency, enabling deployment to any Linux host and to `scratch` Docker images. Not using musl or static linking causes `not found: /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6` errors when deploying to a container with a different glibc version.

children.title: Health Checks & Metrics with Prometheus
children.description: Exposing a `/health` endpoint and Prometheus-compatible metrics (`/metrics`) via the `metrics` and `metrics-exporter-prometheus` crates lets Kubernetes liveness probes and Grafana dashboards monitor your service in production. Not implementing health checks means a crashed service continues receiving traffic until the load balancer's TCP timeout triggers; not exposing metrics makes performance regressions invisible until users file bug reports.

children.title: Graceful Shutdown with tokio signal
children.description: A gracefully shutting down service stops accepting new connections, drains in-flight requests, commits or rolls back open database transactions, and then exits. Catching `SIGTERM` via `tokio::signal::unix::signal` and propagating a cancellation token through the application ensures no requests are dropped during a rolling deployment. Not implementing graceful shutdown causes in-flight requests to receive connection-reset errors every time a new version is deployed.

java-spring.title: Java Fundamentals
java-spring.description: Start with the Java language and the JVM. Learn syntax, types and the compile-run cycle that underpins Spring.
java-spring.challenge: Build a CLI bank account simulator with deposits, withdrawals and validation.

children.title: Bytecode & the Class Loader
children.description: Java source compiles to platform-neutral bytecode that the JVM interprets or JIT-compiles at runtime. Misunderstanding this layer leads to confusion about ClassNotFoundException vs NoClassDefFoundError, incorrect classpaths, and failed deployments when the wrong JDK version is targeted.

children.title: JDK vs JRE vs JVM
children.description: The JDK ships the compiler (javac) and tools; the JRE provides the runtime; the JVM is the execution engine. Conflating them causes developers to ship dev-only tools into production images or to try to compile with a JRE-only installation, both of which are avoidable mistakes.

resources.label: Baeldung — JDK vs JRE vs JVM

children.title: Stack vs Heap Allocation
children.description: Primitives live on the stack while objects are heap-allocated and accessed by reference. Confusing value semantics with reference semantics leads to subtle bugs when passing objects to methods, incorrect equality checks, and unexpected mutation of shared state.

children.title: Autoboxing & Integer Cache
children.description: The JVM caches Integer instances from -128 to 127, so == comparisons may return true for small values but false for larger ones. Relying on reference equality instead of .equals() for wrapper types is a classic source of intermittent production bugs that are hard to reproduce.

children.title: javac Compilation Pipeline
children.description: javac parses source, performs annotation processing, type-checks, and emits .class files. Understanding this pipeline explains why annotation processors like Lombok run at compile time, how incremental builds work, and why source-level compatibility flags matter when targeting older runtimes.

children.title: Java Versions & LTS Releases
children.description: Java releases every six months; LTS versions (11, 17, 21) receive multi-year support. Spring Boot 3.x requires Java 17+. Using an EOL version means missing security patches and Spring compatibility, while jumping versions blindly can break deprecated API usage across the team.

children.title: String Immutability & String Pool
children.description: Strings are immutable in Java and literals are interned in the string pool. Repeatedly concatenating strings with + inside loops creates many intermediate objects, thrashing the GC; StringBuilder is the correct tool. Misunderstanding immutability also causes bugs when passing strings to methods expecting mutable state.

children.title: Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions
children.description: Checked exceptions must be declared or caught; unchecked (RuntimeException) do not. Spring and modern Java APIs prefer unchecked exceptions to avoid forcing callers to handle errors they cannot recover from. Catching Exception broadly and swallowing errors is one of the most common sources of silent failures in backend services.

java-spring.title: OOP & Core Java
java-spring.description: Model your domain with object-oriented Java. Master classes, inheritance, interfaces and exception handling.
java-spring.challenge: Refactor your bank simulator into a clean OOP model with interfaces and custom exceptions.

children.title: Dynamic Dispatch & Method Resolution
children.description: Java resolves overridden methods at runtime based on the actual object type, not the declared reference type. Misunderstanding dynamic dispatch leads to unexpected behavior when overriding methods in subclasses and is the source of fragile inheritance hierarchies that are hard to extend without breaking callers.

children.title: Interfaces vs Abstract Classes
children.description: Interfaces define contracts and support multiple inheritance of type; abstract classes share implementation across a hierarchy. Choosing the wrong abstraction forces rigid class hierarchies that break the Open/Closed Principle and make Spring bean substitution difficult when you need to swap implementations.

children.title: The equals/hashCode Contract
children.description: Objects used as HashMap keys or HashSet members must override both equals and hashCode consistently. Breaking the contract — for example overriding equals without hashCode — causes objects to be silently lost in collections, a bug that only surfaces under load when entries cannot be found by key.

children.title: Records & Immutable Value Objects
children.description: Java 16+ Records auto-generate equals, hashCode, and toString based on all components, making them ideal for DTOs and value objects. Using plain classes for these without implementing the contract correctly is a recurring source of broken collection lookups and misleading log output in Spring applications.

children.title: Strategy & Template Method
children.description: Strategy encapsulates interchangeable algorithms behind an interface; Template Method fixes the skeleton of an algorithm with customizable steps. Spring uses both patterns extensively — for example TransactionTemplate and sorting/comparison utilities. Skipping these means reinventing wheels rather than plugging into Spring's extension points.

children.title: Builder & Factory Patterns
children.description: Builder avoids telescoping constructors and makes object creation readable; Factory decouples creation from usage. Spring's fluent configuration APIs (SecurityFilterChain, WebClient.Builder) are Builders. Not knowing when to use Factory can lead to scattered new-keyword coupling that breaks testability.

children.title: Custom Exception Hierarchies
children.description: Well-designed exception hierarchies let callers catch at the right level of specificity without knowing implementation details. Spring's DataAccessException is a canonical example: it wraps all persistence errors so your code does not couple to Hibernate-specific exceptions, making it possible to switch JPA providers without changing service code.

children.title: Try-with-Resources & AutoCloseable
children.description: Try-with-resources guarantees that any AutoCloseable (streams, connections, sessions) is closed even when exceptions occur. Forgetting to close JDBC connections or Hibernate sessions is the leading cause of connection pool exhaustion in Spring applications under load.

java-spring.title: Collections, Generics & Streams
java-spring.description: Use Java's powerful standard library. Work with collections, write generic code, and process data with the Stream API.
topics: The Stream API — map, filter, collect
java-spring.challenge: Process a list of transactions with streams to produce per-category totals and summaries.

children.title: HashMap Internals & Buckets
children.description: HashMap distributes entries into buckets using hashCode mod capacity and uses a linked list (or tree for Java 8+) to resolve collisions. A poor hashCode implementation that returns the same value for all objects degrades HashMap from O(1) to O(n) lookups, which can bring a high-traffic Spring service to its knees.

children.title: List Implementations: ArrayList vs LinkedList
children.description: ArrayList offers O(1) random access but O(n) insertions in the middle; LinkedList is O(1) for head/tail insertions but O(n) for index access and has high memory overhead per node. Picking LinkedList for random-access-heavy workloads is a common performance mistake that becomes visible only under load.

children.title: Type Erasure
children.description: Java generics are erased at runtime; List<String> and List<Integer> are both just List at the bytecode level. This is why you cannot do instanceof List<String> and why reflection-based frameworks like Spring sometimes need TypeReference or ParameterizedType tricks to recover generic type information for deserialization.

children.title: Wildcards & Bounded Types
children.description: Producer Extends, Consumer Super (PECS) is the rule for choosing ? extends T vs ? super T. Getting wildcard variance wrong makes APIs unnecessarily restrictive or unsafe, and it is the most common reason developers resort to raw types which defeat the purpose of generics and break compile-time safety.

children.title: Lazy Evaluation in Streams
children.description: Stream operations are divided into intermediate (lazy) and terminal (eager). Intermediate operations do not execute until a terminal operation is called, and only the elements needed are processed. Misunderstanding laziness leads to surprising behavior when using streams with side effects, and to performance losses from building unnecessary data structures.

children.title: Optional & Null Safety
children.description: Optional is a container that forces callers to handle the absent case explicitly, eliminating NullPointerExceptions from unchecked return values. Using Optional.get() without isPresent(), or storing Optional in fields and collections, defeats its purpose and introduces the same NPE risk in a more obscure form.

children.title: Functional Interfaces & Method References
children.description: A functional interface has exactly one abstract method and can be represented by a lambda or method reference. Understanding which functional interface (Function, Predicate, Supplier, Consumer) maps to which signature prevents writing unnecessary anonymous classes and makes stream pipelines readable.

children.title: Collectors & Grouping
children.description: Collectors.groupingBy, partitioningBy, and toMap are the workhorses for aggregating stream results. Not knowing these forces developers to write verbose manual loops that are harder to parallelize and less expressive, and misusing toMap without a merge function throws an exception on duplicate keys.

java-spring.title: Build Tools & Spring Basics
java-spring.description: Enter the Spring ecosystem. Manage builds with Maven and understand dependency injection — Spring's core idea.
java-spring.challenge: Create a Spring Boot project with Maven and wire two beans together via constructor injection.

children.title: Phases, Goals & Plugin Binding
children.description: Maven's build lifecycle (validate → compile → test → package → install → deploy) executes bound plugin goals in sequence. Misunderstanding which phase runs which goal causes confusing failures — for example running mvn package and wondering why integration tests are skipped — and makes it impossible to write effective CI pipelines.

children.title: Dependency Management & BOMs
children.description: A Bill of Materials (BOM) centralizes version management so child projects inherit compatible dependency versions. Spring Boot's parent POM is a BOM; overriding its managed versions without understanding transitive dependency resolution is the most common cause of version conflicts and mysterious ClassNotFoundExceptions at startup.

children.title: Inversion of Control & the ApplicationContext
children.description: IoC inverts object creation: instead of your code constructing collaborators with new, the container creates and wires them. The ApplicationContext is the central registry. Failing to understand IoC leads developers to use new inside Spring-managed beans, bypassing the container and silently creating unmanaged objects without injected dependencies.

children.title: Bean Scopes
children.description: Spring beans default to singleton scope: one shared instance per ApplicationContext. Injecting a prototype-scoped (stateful) bean into a singleton without a proxy or lookup method means all callers share the same prototype instance, causing data corruption between requests in a multi-threaded server.

children.title: Constructor vs Field Injection
children.description: Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit, enables immutable beans, and works without the Spring container in tests. Field injection with @Autowired hides dependencies, makes beans harder to test in isolation, and can lead to circular dependency failures that are only detected at startup rather than compile time.

children.title: Spring Boot Auto-Configuration
children.description: Spring Boot scans META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports and conditionally creates beans via @Conditional annotations. Not understanding auto-configuration causes developers to manually configure things Spring Boot already provides, or to be confused when auto-configured beans override custom ones due to missing @ConditionalOnMissingBean guards.

children.title: Environment-Specific Profiles
children.description: Profiles allow activating different bean configurations (dev, staging, prod) without changing code. Hardcoding environment-specific values instead of using profiles or externalized configuration makes it impossible to promote the same artifact through environments, violating the 12-factor app principle of strict separation of config from code.

children.title: @ConfigurationProperties vs @Value
