Not having grpcurl or an equivalent in your workflow means every debugging session requires writing throwaway client code, slowing diagnosis during incidents.

children.title: Postman & Kreya for gRPC
children.description: GUI tools like Postman (with gRPC support) and Kreya provide a visual interface for constructing calls, managing environments, and saving request collections, which is especially helpful for onboarding new team members to a gRPC API. Teams relying solely on CLI tools miss the discoverability benefits that reduce onboarding time.

grpc-dev.title: Streaming RPCs
grpc-dev.description: Go beyond request/response. Implement server, client and bidirectional streaming over HTTP/2.
grpc-dev.challenge: Build a live metrics service using server-side streaming and a chat using bidirectional streaming.

children.title: Four gRPC Communication Patterns
children.description: gRPC supports unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, and bidirectional streaming; each maps to a different HTTP/2 stream usage pattern and has different framing and half-close semantics. Choosing the wrong pattern (e.g. unary for large file uploads) causes memory exhaustion from buffering entire payloads before sending.

children.title: Half-Close & Stream Termination
children.description: A client signals it has finished sending by half-closing its side of the HTTP/2 stream; the server reads EOF and must drain its response before closing. Forgetting to call SendMsg/CloseSend appropriately leaves streams open indefinitely, leaking connections and eventually hitting per-connection stream limits.

children.title: HTTP/2 Flow Control Windows
children.description: HTTP/2 uses per-stream and per-connection flow control windows to prevent a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver; gRPC streams pause automatically when the receiver's window is full. Ignoring this means a server streaming at maximum speed to a slow browser client will stall all other streams on the same connection.

children.title: Initial Window Size Tuning
children.description: The default HTTP/2 initial flow control window (65535 bytes) is often too small for high-throughput gRPC streams, causing unnecessary WINDOW_UPDATE round trips that limit throughput on high-latency links. Tuning this value for your workload (e.g. large binary payloads, satellite links) can reduce stream latency by an order of magnitude.

children.title: Server-Streaming Use Cases
children.description: Server streaming is ideal for pushing live events (metrics, logs, notifications) to a client that made a single subscription request; the server sends multiple messages over one stream without the overhead of repeated connection setup. Using polling instead of server streaming on high-frequency updates wastes bandwidth and increases latency.

children.title: Client-Streaming for Bulk Ingestion
children.description: Client streaming lets a client send a sequence of messages (e.g. chunked file upload, sensor readings) and receive a single summary response; this avoids materializing the entire payload in memory before transmission. Teams that use a large unary request for bulk ingestion hit message size limits (default 4MB) and cause memory pressure on the server.

children.title: Errors Mid-Stream
children.description: In a streaming RPC, an error terminates the stream immediately via a GRPC-Status trailer; the client must inspect this trailer rather than the HTTP status. Failing to handle mid-stream errors causes the client to silently process partial data as if the stream completed successfully.

children.title: Context Cancellation in Streams
children.description: Cancelling the client context immediately sends an RST_STREAM frame to the server, which must detect context.Done() in its send loop to stop processing and release resources. Ignoring context cancellation in server-side streaming handlers causes goroutine/thread leaks that accumulate over time.

grpc-dev.title: gRPC in Go
grpc-dev.description: Implement high-performance gRPC services in Go, the language gRPC feels most at home in.
grpc-dev.challenge: Implement your order service in Go with unary and streaming methods and a Go client.

children.title: protoc-gen-go & protoc-gen-go-grpc
children.description: Go code generation requires two separate plugins: protoc-gen-go for message types and protoc-gen-go-grpc for service stubs; using an older combined plugin generates deprecated APIs that the modern runtime no longer supports. Version pinning both plugins in a go.mod-adjacent Makefile or buf.gen.yaml is essential for reproducible builds.

children.title: Module Path & go_package Option
children.description: The go_package option in a .proto file controls where generated Go files are placed within the module; an incorrect go_package causes import path mismatches that break compilation even when the generated file is present. Aligning go_package with your module path from the start avoids a painful refactor when the codebase grows.

children.title: RegisterXxxServer & net.Listen
children.description: A Go gRPC server requires registering each service implementation and binding to a net.Listener before calling Serve; missing a registration means the service is silently unreachable rather than failing loudly. Using a unix socket listener (instead of TCP) for same-host services reduces kernel overhead measurably.

children.title: Graceful Stop vs Force Stop
children.description: grpc.Server.GracefulStop() waits for in-flight RPCs to complete before shutting down, while Stop() forcefully terminates them; deploying with Stop() causes client-visible UNAVAILABLE errors during rolling restarts. Coupling GracefulStop with a context timeout ensures the server drains without blocking a deployment indefinitely.

children.title: Connection Reuse & grpc.Dial
children.description: grpc.Dial (and the newer grpc.NewClient) creates a ClientConn that lazily establishes HTTP/2 connections and reuses them; creating a new connection per request incurs TLS handshake overhead and connection setup latency on every call. The connection should be created once at startup and shared across all goroutines.

children.title: Context, Deadlines & Cancellation
children.description: Every gRPC call in Go accepts a context.Context that carries deadlines, cancellation signals, and request-scoped values; not propagating the parent context from an HTTP handler means the gRPC call will not be cancelled when the HTTP client disconnects, wasting downstream capacity. Deadlines set on the client context are automatically transmitted to the server as gRPC deadline headers.

children.title: Goroutines Per Stream
children.description: grpc-go spawns goroutines to handle each incoming stream; under high concurrency the scheduler overhead of thousands of goroutines can become measurable, and unbounded concurrency without a semaphore can exhaust memory. Using a worker pool or a concurrency limiter interceptor keeps resource usage predictable.

children.title: Error Wrapping with status.Error
children.description: In Go, gRPC errors must be created with the status package (status.Error or status.Errorf) rather than fmt.Errorf; a plain Go error is transmitted as UNKNOWN to the client, losing the original status code and any attached details. Consistently wrapping errors with the correct code makes client retry and alerting logic reliable.

grpc-dev.title: gRPC in Node.js
grpc-dev.description: Bring gRPC to the Node ecosystem. Generate types and build services and clients in TypeScript.
grpc-dev.challenge: Build a Node.js gRPC client in TypeScript that talks to your Go order service.

children.title: @grpc/grpc-js vs grpc (legacy)
children.description: @grpc/grpc-js is the pure-JavaScript rewrite of the original native grpc package; the legacy grpc package relies on a compiled C++ addon that breaks on Node.js version upgrades and is no longer maintained. Using the legacy package causes build failures on modern Node.js and misses security patches.

children.title: Dynamic vs Static Code Generation
children.description: grpc-js supports loading .proto files dynamically at runtime (via @grpc/proto-loader) or using statically generated code from ts-proto/protoc; dynamic loading is convenient for prototyping but loses TypeScript type safety and has higher startup cost. Production services should use static codegen to catch type mismatches at compile time.

children.title: ts-proto vs protoc-gen-ts
children.description: ts-proto generates idiomatic TypeScript interfaces and async/await-friendly stubs, while protoc-gen-ts generates class-based code closer to the Java style; ts-proto integrates better with modern TypeScript toolchains and frameworks like NestJS. Mixing generated code from both tools in the same project causes type incompatibilities at service boundaries.

children.title: Handling Long / Int64 in Node.js
children.description: JavaScript's Number type cannot represent all int64 values without precision loss; grpc-js returns int64 fields as Long objects (from the long npm package) by default, which breaks direct arithmetic unless properly converted. Not handling this causes subtle billing or ID truncation bugs that only appear with large values.

children.title: Implementing a Node Server
children.description: A grpc-js server requires binding service implementations as plain objects with method handlers and calling server.bindAsync before start; forgetting bindAsync and using the deprecated server.bind causes the server to silently start on a random port or fail without a clear error. Enabling reflection via grpc-reflection-js aids debugging without shipping .proto files with each client.

children.title: Promisifying Node gRPC Clients
children.description: grpc-js client stubs use callback-based APIs by default; wrapping calls with util.promisify or using ts-proto's generated Promise-based stubs lets engineers use async/await throughout the codebase. Mixing callbacks and promises in the same call chain leads to unhandled rejection bugs that are hard to trace during async error propagation.

children.title: Cross-Language Service Communication
children.description: Because all gRPC services share the same wire format defined by Protobuf, a TypeScript client can call a Go server without any translation layer; however, timestamp, enum default, and oneof representations can differ subtly between language runtimes. Testing cross-language calls in CI with real generated stubs prevents integration surprises at deployment time.

children.title: NestJS gRPC Microservices
children.description: NestJS has first-class gRPC transport support via @nestjs/microservices, allowing decorators like @GrpcMethod to map service definitions to controller methods; however, the NestJS gRPC module uses ts-proto or protoc-gen-ts generated types and its own channel configuration that differs from raw grpc-js. Conflating NestJS gRPC configuration with raw grpc-js docs leads to misconfigured channels and missing interceptors.

grpc-dev.title: Interceptors, Auth & Metadata
grpc-dev.description: Add cross-cutting concerns to gRPC. Use interceptors and metadata for auth, logging and tracing, secured with TLS.
grpc-dev.challenge: Add a JWT-checking interceptor and enable mutual TLS between your services.

children.title: Unary vs Stream Interceptors
children.description: gRPC distinguishes between unary interceptors (which wrap a single request/response) and streaming interceptors (which wrap the stream object); a unary interceptor will not fire for streaming calls, so auth or logging implemented only as a unary interceptor silently skips all streaming RPCs. Both interceptor types must be implemented to achieve uniform policy enforcement.

children.title: Chaining Multiple Interceptors
children.description: Interceptor chaining (via grpc.ChainUnaryInterceptor or go-grpc-middleware) applies multiple interceptors in order; each interceptor must call the next handler or the chain silently terminates, dropping the request without an error. Ordering matters: auth should precede logging so unauthenticated request details are not persisted.

children.title: Sending & Receiving Metadata
children.description: gRPC metadata travels in HTTP/2 headers (initial metadata) and trailers (trailing metadata); clients attach metadata via context and servers extract it via metadata.FromIncomingContext. Treating metadata as request body fields adds unnecessary bytes to every response message rather than keeping it in zero-cost headers.

children.title: Binary vs ASCII Metadata Values
children.description: Metadata keys ending in -bin carry base64-encoded binary values, while other keys carry ASCII strings; sending binary data in an ASCII metadata key causes transport errors or silent truncation depending on the proxy. Using the -bin suffix and letting grpc-js/grpc-go handle encoding ensures binary metadata survives through proxies and load balancers.

children.title: JWT Bearer Tokens via Metadata
children.description: JWT tokens are typically passed as the Authorization metadata key; a server-side unary and stream interceptor validates the token before calling the handler, and must return UNAUTHENTICATED on failure rather than PERMISSION_DENIED. Returning PERMISSION_DENIED for invalid tokens misleads clients into believing they have the wrong role rather than an expired token.

children.title: TLS & Mutual TLS (mTLS)
children.description: Standard TLS authenticates only the server; mTLS requires the client to present a certificate as well, making it ideal for service-to-service authentication without managing JWTs. Skipping mTLS in microservices that trust the network perimeter is an insider threat vector — any process on the network can impersonate a service.

children.title: Structured Logging in Interceptors
children.description: Interceptors are the right place to emit structured logs (method name, status code, duration, request ID) because they execute around every RPC without scattering logging calls across handlers. Logging inside individual handlers duplicates effort and produces inconsistent log schemas that break log-based alerting.

children.title: Distributed Tracing with OpenTelemetry
children.description: OpenTelemetry gRPC interceptors propagate trace context via metadata automatically, linking spans across service boundaries for end-to-end latency analysis. Without trace propagation, distributed traces appear as disconnected spans and make it impossible to identify which service in a call chain is responsible for a latency spike.

grpc-dev.title: Error Handling & Deadlines
grpc-dev.description: Make gRPC robust. Use rich error models, propagate deadlines, and add retries for reliability.
grpc-dev.challenge: Add structured error details, deadlines and a retry policy to your order client.

children.title: Mapping Domain Errors to Status Codes
children.description: The 16 gRPC status codes have precise semantics (INVALID_ARGUMENT for bad input, NOT_FOUND for missing resources, UNAVAILABLE for transient failures); using INTERNAL for all errors prevents clients from applying correct retry logic and causes over-retrying on permanent failures. Client libraries and service meshes use status codes to decide whether to retry, fail fast, or circuit-break.

children.title: Rich Error Model with ErrorDetails
children.description: Beyond a status code, the google.rpc.Status proto allows attaching structured details like BadRequest.FieldViolation, RetryInfo, or QuotaFailure; this lets clients programmatically parse the cause without screen-scraping error messages. Teams that only return a plain status code with a string message force client engineers to build fragile string-parsing logic.

children.title: Deadline Propagation Across Services
children.description: When a client sets a deadline on a gRPC call, the remaining deadline is transmitted to the server in a grpc-timeout header; the server must propagate the same context to any downstream calls so the entire request tree aborts when the original deadline expires. Forgetting to propagate the context causes downstream services to continue processing after the upstream client has already timed out and returned an error.

children.title: Choosing the Right Deadline Value
children.description: Deadlines should be set to the 99th-percentile expected latency of the downstream service, not an arbitrary large value; setting deadlines too high defeats their purpose and leaves threads/goroutines blocked during outages. Setting them too low causes DEADLINE_EXCEEDED cascades under normal load spikes.

children.title: gRPC Retry Policy Configuration
children.description: gRPC supports declarative retry policies in the service config JSON, specifying which status codes are retryable, max attempts, and exponential backoff parameters; retries are transparent to application code. Not using built-in retry policies forces engineers to implement ad-hoc retry loops that ignore in-flight request budgets and can amplify load during outages.

children.title: Hedging for Latency Tail Reduction
children.description: Hedged requests send the same RPC to multiple backends simultaneously and use the first successful response, cutting tail latency at the cost of extra load; this is distinct from retry (which waits for failure). Hedging is appropriate only for read-only RPCs — applying it to write operations causes duplicate mutations.

children.title: gRPC Health Checking Protocol
children.description: The gRPC health checking protocol defines a standard Health.Check RPC that load balancers and Kubernetes readiness probes use to determine service availability; implementing it ensures traffic is not routed to a starting or draining instance. Services that omit health checks receive traffic before they are ready and continue receiving traffic while they are draining.

children.title: Graceful Shutdown Sequence
children.description: A correct shutdown sequence marks the health check as NOT_SERVING, waits for the load balancer to stop routing traffic (typically one health-check interval), then calls GracefulStop; skipping the wait causes in-flight requests from clients that have not yet seen the health check failure to receive connection resets. This sequence is mandatory for zero-downtime deployments in Kubernetes.

grpc-dev.title: gRPC-Web, Gateways & Testing
grpc-dev.description: Bridge gRPC to browsers and REST clients, and test your services thoroughly.
grpc-dev.challenge: Expose your service as REST via grpc-gateway and load-test the gRPC endpoint with ghz.

children.title: gRPC-Web Protocol Differences
children.description: Browsers cannot use HTTP/2 trailers, so gRPC-Web encodes trailers inside the response body using a special trailer frame byte; a regular gRPC client and a gRPC-Web client are not interchangeable even though they call the same server method. Treating gRPC-Web as identical to gRPC leads to missing status codes and broken streaming in browser clients.

children.title: Envoy as a gRPC-Web Proxy
children.description: Envoy translates between gRPC-Web (HTTP/1.1-compatible) from browsers and native gRPC (HTTP/2) for backend services; it also handles transcoding, header manipulation, and connection management. Deploying gRPC-Web without a proxy like Envoy requires each backend service to understand the gRPC-Web framing protocol, duplicating proxy logic everywhere.

children.title: Generating REST APIs from Proto
children.description: grpc-gateway reads google.api.http annotations in .proto files and generates a reverse-proxy that translates incoming JSON REST requests into gRPC calls; this means REST and gRPC share a single implementation with no duplication. Maintaining a separate REST handler alongside gRPC creates drift between the two APIs and doubles the testing surface.

children.title: google.api.http Annotations
children.description: HTTP binding annotations specify URL patterns, HTTP methods, and how proto fields map to path parameters, query parameters, and the request body; incorrect bindings silently map the wrong proto fields to HTTP parameters, producing confusing 400 errors. Using the buf lint rule for http_annotations ensures all RPCs have valid bindings before deployment.

children.title: Unit Testing with In-Process Servers
children.description: Testing gRPC services in-process (using bufconn in Go or in-memory servers in Node) avoids network overhead, port conflicts, and CI environment dependencies while exercising the full RPC serialization path. Tests that mock at the stub level instead of using real generated code miss serialization bugs and proto default-value edge cases.

children.title: ghz for Load Testing
children.description: ghz is a gRPC-native load testing tool that sends configurable RPS to a service, reports latency percentiles, and checks against proto reflection or a .proto file; it produces the same statistics (p50/p95/p99) that SRE teams use for SLO baselines. Using HTTP-level load testers like k6 without gRPC support means tests never exercise the actual Protobuf serialization path.

children.title: Buf Breaking Change Detection in CI
children.description: Buf's breaking change detector compares the current .proto files against a baseline (a git tag or the BSR) and fails the build if any wire-incompatible changes are detected; this acts as a contract test that protects all deployed clients from silent breakage. Without this check, a field renumber or type change silently passes code review and corrupts production data.

children.title: Mocking gRPC Clients in Unit Tests
children.description: Code generated by protoc produces interfaces (in Go) or abstract classes (in Java) that can be swapped with mocks in unit tests; using a real gRPC channel in unit tests introduces network dependencies that make tests flaky and slow in CI. Tools like gomock, mockery (Go), or jest.mock (Node) generate type-safe mocks directly from the generated interfaces.

grpc-dev.title: Performance & Deployment
grpc-dev.description: Ship and scale gRPC in production. Manage schemas, load balance, and deploy on Kubernetes.
grpc-dev.challenge: Manage your protos with Buf, containerize the services, and deploy them load-balanced on Kubernetes.

children.title: Buf Schema Registry (BSR)
children.description: The Buf Schema Registry hosts versioned .proto modules that teams depend on like npm packages, enabling shared types and breaking-change audits across repositories. Organizations that share .proto files via git submodules or copy-paste lose version tracking and cannot enforce compatibility guarantees across service boundaries.

children.title: Versioning gRPC APIs
children.description: gRPC APIs are versioned via the Protobuf package (e.g. myservice.v1, myservice.v2) rather than URL paths; serving both versions simultaneously lets clients migrate at their own pace without a hard cutover. Teams that break the v1 package instead of creating v2 force all clients to redeploy simultaneously, creating a coordinated deployment nightmare.

children.title: Keep-Alive Pings
children.description: gRPC keep-alive sends HTTP/2 PING frames on idle connections to detect broken TCP connections faster than the OS TCP keep-alive timer; without it, a silently dropped connection is only discovered on the next RPC, returning UNAVAILABLE after a long timeout. Misconfiguring keep-alive (e.g. too aggressive on cloud load balancers) causes connections to be killed by the LB, creating the very failures it aims to prevent.

children.title: Connection Pooling Strategies
children.description: A single gRPC channel supports up to 100 concurrent streams by default over one TCP connection; services with very high fan-out concurrency benefit from a pool of channels to distribute streams across multiple TCP connections. Creating too few channels creates a single-connection bottleneck; creating too many wastes file descriptors and TLS session memory.

children.title: L7 vs L4 Load Balancing
children.description: Standard L4 TCP load balancers distribute connections, not requests; because gRPC multiplexes many RPCs over one connection, all RPCs from one client go to the same backend, defeating horizontal scaling. L7-aware proxies (Envoy, nginx, Linkerd) or client-side load balancing are required to distribute individual gRPC calls across a pod pool.

children.title: Client-Side Load Balancing with Resolvers
children.description: grpc-go supports pluggable name resolvers (DNS, xDS, etcd) and balancer policies (round-robin, pick-first) that distribute calls client-side without a proxy hop; this reduces latency but requires the client to receive a fresh address list when replicas scale up or down. Using a stale address list after a scale-down event causes a fraction of RPCs to hit terminated pods until the resolver refreshes.

children.title: Kubernetes Service & HTTP/2
children.description: Kubernetes Services (ClusterIP) perform L4 load balancing, which does not distribute gRPC streams across pods; using a headless Service with client-side load balancing or a service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) is necessary to achieve per-RPC distribution. Deploying gRPC behind a standard ClusterIP Service causes 'hot pod' problems where one pod handles all traffic while others are idle.

children.title: OpenTelemetry & Production Observability
children.description: Instrumenting gRPC services with OpenTelemetry produces traces, metrics (RPC duration, error rate, message size), and logs that are vendor-neutral and compatible with Prometheus, Jaeger, and Grafana; this data is essential for diagnosing latency regressions and setting SLOs. Services without distributed tracing make it impossible to pinpoint which microservice is responsible for a latency spike in a deep call chain.

node-dev.title: JavaScript & TypeScript Foundations
node-dev.description: A Node developer lives in JavaScript and, increasingly, TypeScript. Solidify the language features the backend relies on every day.
node-dev.challenge: Build a typed CLI tool that reads JSON, transforms it, and writes results with zero 'any'.

children.title: The Event Loop & Microtask Queue
children.description: JavaScript is single-threaded, and the event loop is the engine that keeps it alive. Misunderstanding the difference between the microtask queue (Promises, queueMicrotask) and the macrotask queue (setTimeout, setInterval) leads to subtle ordering bugs where async callbacks fire at unexpected times, causing race conditions that are nearly impossible to reproduce.

resources.label: MDN — Concurrency model and Event Loop

children.title: Closures & Lexical Scope
children.description: Closures let inner functions capture variables from their enclosing scope, which is the foundation of callbacks, module patterns, and memoization. Getting this wrong causes classic bugs like loop variables all resolving to the same final value, or memory leaks from closures that unintentionally hold large objects alive.

children.title: Generics & Type Narrowing
children.description: Generics allow you to write reusable, type-safe functions without sacrificing specificity — essential for repository patterns, API handlers, and utility functions. Without proper type narrowing, you lose the safety net TypeScript provides and end up with runtime errors that the type checker should have caught at compile time.

children.title: tsconfig for Node.js
children.description: A misconfigured tsconfig is the silent killer of Node.js TypeScript projects — wrong module resolution (commonjs vs nodenext) causes import failures at runtime even when the compiler emits no errors. Getting target, moduleResolution, and strict mode right from the start prevents painful refactors when you later try to add ES modules or path aliases.

children.title: ES Modules vs CommonJS
children.description: Node.js supports both module systems, but mixing them without understanding the interop rules causes cryptic errors like "ERR_REQUIRE_ESM" or top-level await failures. Knowing when to use .mjs, .cjs, or the package.json 'type' field is critical for writing libraries and apps that work correctly in both module environments.

children.title: Async/Await & Promise Combinators
children.description: Promise.all, Promise.allSettled, and Promise.race are not interchangeable — using Promise.all when one failure should not cancel others causes silent data loss, while using sequential await in a loop instead of Promise.all creates unnecessary latency. Understanding which combinator fits each use case is foundational to writing performant async Node code.

children.title: Utility Types (Partial, Pick, Omit)
children.description: Utility types let you derive new types from existing ones without duplication, keeping your type definitions DRY across DTOs, update payloads, and database models. Ignoring them leads to types that drift out of sync — the source model gets updated but the partial update type does not, allowing invalid data to pass type checking undetected.

children.title: Interfaces vs Type Aliases
children.description: While often interchangeable, interfaces support declaration merging (useful for extending third-party types) and type aliases enable union and intersection types. Choosing the wrong one in a library boundary or plugin system causes unexpected behavior when consumers try to extend your types, which breaks a key TypeScript extensibility pattern.

node-dev.title: Node.js Runtime Internals
node-dev.description: Understand what makes Node tick — the event loop, the module system, and the core APIs you build everything on.
topics: CommonJS vs ES modules in Node
Core modules — fs, path, os, events
node-dev.challenge: Build a small file-watcher using core modules and the EventEmitter pattern.

children.title: libuv & the Thread Pool
children.description: Node.js is not single-threaded for I/O — libuv maintains a thread pool (default size 4) for file system operations, DNS lookups, and crypto. If you block this pool with CPU-heavy crypto or too many parallel fs calls, unrelated network requests stall visibly, a production bug that only manifests under load and is hard to trace without knowing libuv's architecture.

children.title: process.nextTick vs setImmediate
children.description: process.nextTick callbacks run before any I/O events in the current iteration — scheduling too many nextTick calls recursively can starve I/O and make your server appear unresponsive. setImmediate defers to the check phase of the next event loop iteration, making it the safer choice for breaking up long synchronous work without blocking I/O.

children.title: The fs Module & Async I/O
children.description: Node's fs module exposes both synchronous and asynchronous file APIs — using synchronous calls (fs.readFileSync) in request handlers blocks the entire event loop, making your server unresponsive to all other requests during every file read. Always prefer the promise-based fs/promises API in async handlers to keep I/O non-blocking.

children.title: EventEmitter Pattern
children.description: EventEmitter is the backbone of Node.js streams, HTTP servers, and countless libraries — failing to remove listeners after use leads to memory leaks that grow slowly until the process crashes. Adding the same listener multiple times (a common mistake when re-initializing) creates phantom handlers that fire multiple times per event.

children.title: CommonJS Module Resolution
children.description: Node's CommonJS resolver follows a specific algorithm — checking for exact file, then .js/.json/.node extensions, then index files, then node_modules up the directory tree. Misunderstanding this causes silent fallbacks to wrong modules and hard-to-debug 'module not found' errors in monorepos where multiple versions of a package may exist at different tree levels.

children.title: Environment Variables & Config
children.description: Hard-coded config values are a security risk and an ops nightmare — environment variables decouple your code from its deployment context so the same binary runs in dev, staging, and prod without changes. Without a validation layer on startup (like env-schema or Zod), a missing or misspelled env var silently defaults to undefined and causes cryptic failures deep inside request handling.

children.title: V8 JIT Compilation & Hidden Classes
children.description: V8 generates optimized machine code based on 'hidden classes' — objects that share the same shape (same properties added in the same order) get compiled into a single fast class. Dynamically adding properties in different orders across instances causes V8 to deoptimize that code path to slow interpreted mode, degrading throughput in hot loops.

children.title: Garbage Collection & Memory
children.description: V8's generational garbage collector (Scavenger + Mark-Compact) pauses the event loop to collect unreachable objects — long GC pauses cause request latency spikes that are invisible in CPU profiles but obvious in p99 latency metrics. Keeping objects small, avoiding large allocations in hot paths, and releasing references promptly keeps GC pauses short.

resources.label: V8 Blog — Trash talk: the Orinoco garbage collector

node-dev.title: Async Patterns & Streams
node-dev.description: Master Node's asynchronous nature. Work with streams for efficient I/O and avoid the common async pitfalls.
node-dev.challenge: Build a streaming CSV-to-JSON converter that processes a large file without loading it into memory.

children.title: Readable & Writable Streams
children.description: Streams let you process data chunk-by-chunk rather than loading it all into memory — reading a 4 GB file into a Buffer crashes a Node process, but streaming it uses only kilobytes of RAM. Understanding the difference between flowing mode (data events) and paused mode (read() calls) is essential because accidentally switching modes mid-stream drops data silently.

children.title: Backpressure & pipe()
children.description: Backpressure is the mechanism by which a slow writable stream signals a fast readable to pause — ignoring it by not respecting the boolean return value of write() causes unbounded memory growth as unprocessed chunks accumulate. The pipe() method handles backpressure automatically, but manually piping streams without respecting highWaterMark is the most common cause of Node memory leaks in data pipelines.

children.title: Async Iterators & for-await-of
children.description: Async iterators provide a clean, pull-based API for consuming streams without managing 'data' and 'end' events manually — they also integrate naturally with async generators for producing data lazily. Not using them forces you into callback-heavy code that is hard to read and makes error handling in multi-step stream pipelines fragile and verbose.

children.title: Transform Streams
children.description: Transform streams are duplex streams that can modify or transform data as it passes through — they are the building blocks of compression, encryption, parsing, and encoding pipelines. Implementing a Transform incorrectly by forgetting to call the callback or by pushing data after the stream has ended causes the pipeline to hang forever waiting for a callback that never fires.

children.title: Worker Threads for CPU Work
children.description: Node.js is single-threaded for JavaScript execution — a CPU-heavy task like image processing or cryptography on the main thread blocks the event loop and freezes all concurrent requests. Worker threads provide true parallelism for CPU-bound work by running JavaScript in a separate V8 instance while the main thread continues serving I/O.

resources.label: Node.js — Don't block the Event Loop

children.title: Cluster Module & Load Distribution
children.description: The cluster module forks the Node process across multiple CPU cores, multiplying throughput for I/O-bound services, but all workers share the same port via OS-level load balancing which means session state must live in Redis not in-process memory. Running a single-process Node server on an 8-core machine leaves 7 cores idle — a trivial performance improvement that cluster makes straightforward.

resources.label: Node.js — How to use the Cluster module

children.title: Unhandled Promise Rejections
children.description: Before Node 15, unhandled promise rejections were silent warnings — since Node 15 they crash the process, which means missing a single catch on an async call can take down your entire server. Using process.on('unhandledRejection') as a last resort and combining it with structured logging is essential to diagnose these crashes in production before they happen.

children.title: async_hooks & Context Propagation
children.description: AsyncLocalStorage (built on async_hooks) lets you propagate request-scoped context — like a correlation ID — automatically through async call chains without threading it through every function signature. Without it, adding request tracing to an existing codebase requires invasive changes to every service and utility function.

node-dev.title: Building APIs with Fastify
node-dev.description: Build fast, well-structured HTTP APIs with Fastify — a high-performance, schema-first Node framework.
node-dev.challenge: Build a REST API for an inventory service with Fastify, JSON schema validation and plugins.

children.title: Request Lifecycle & Hooks
children.description: Fastify processes every request through a defined lifecycle with distinct hook points (onRequest, preHandler, onSend, onResponse) — attaching logic to the wrong hook causes subtle bugs like auth checks running after route handlers or response serialization breaking because a hook modified headers too late. Understanding the lifecycle is the difference between clean Fastify code and spaghetti middleware.

children.title: Schema-Based Validation & Serialization
children.description: Fastify uses JSON Schema to validate incoming requests and serialize outgoing responses — this is not optional boilerplate but the core of what makes Fastify fast, as serialization via fast-json-stringify is 2–3x faster than JSON.stringify. Skipping schema definitions defeats the performance gains and exposes your API to malformed input that bypasses any manual validation you add later.

children.title: Plugin Encapsulation with fastify-plugin
children.description: Fastify's plugin system creates encapsulated scopes — decorators and routes registered inside a plugin are not visible outside unless you use fastify-plugin to break encapsulation. Misunderstanding this causes confusing bugs where a decorator registered in one plugin is mysteriously missing in another, or shared state leaks across what should be isolated contexts.

children.title: Decorators for Dependency Injection
children.description: Fastify decorators attach services (database clients, config, mailers) to the fastify instance, making them accessible in every route handler without importing them directly. Bypassing this pattern and importing services directly into handlers couples your route logic to concrete implementations, making unit testing impossible without real infrastructure.

children.title: RESTful Resource Modeling
children.description: REST APIs are built around resources, not actions — using verbs in URLs (/getUser, /createOrder) instead of HTTP methods on nouns (/users, /orders) creates inconsistent APIs that are hard to document and consume. Correct resource modeling with proper use of GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE makes your API predictable and allows HTTP caching layers to work correctly.

children.title: HTTP Status Codes & Error Responses
children.description: Returning HTTP 200 for every response, including errors, breaks every HTTP client that relies on status codes for control flow — monitoring tools, load balancers, and client SDKs all use status codes to decide retry, alert, or failure behavior. Using 4xx for client errors, 5xx for server errors, and consistent error response shapes is the minimum for a production-quality API.

children.title: Scalable Fastify Project Layout
children.description: Colocating all routes, plugins, and business logic in a single file works for tutorials but collapses in real projects — a flat structure forces circular imports and makes onboarding impossible. Separating plugins, routes, services, and schemas into dedicated directories and loading them via autoload plugins keeps the codebase navigable as it grows to hundreds of endpoints.

children.title: Graceful Shutdown in Fastify
children.description: When a Kubernetes pod is killed or a deployment is rolled out, Node receives SIGTERM — if you do not handle it by stopping the HTTP server and draining in-flight requests, active connections are cut mid-response and in-progress database writes may be left incomplete. Fastify's fastify.close() drains the connection pool and calls onClose hooks, making it the correct way to shut down cleanly.

node-dev.title: Data with Prisma & PostgreSQL
node-dev.description: Persist data with a typed ORM. Model relations, run migrations, and query safely with Prisma against PostgreSQL.
node-dev.challenge: Back your inventory API with PostgreSQL via Prisma, including relations and a transactional stock update.

children.title: Prisma Schema & Client Generation
children.description: The Prisma schema is the single source of truth for your database structure and generated TypeScript types — running queries without regenerating the client after a schema change causes type errors that only surface at runtime because the compiled JS is still using the old shape. Always pair schema changes with prisma generate and prisma migrate dev to keep the type system and database in sync.

children.title: Connection Pooling with Prisma
children.description: Prisma opens a connection pool to PostgreSQL and reuses those connections across requests — instantiating a new PrismaClient on every request exhausts the database's max_connections limit within seconds under any real load. In serverless or edge environments, connection pooling requires PgBouncer or Prisma Accelerate because each function invocation creates a new process with its own pool.

children.title: Prisma Migrate & Database Migrations
children.description: Prisma Migrate generates SQL migrations from schema diffs and tracks which have been applied — running prisma db push in production bypasses migration history and makes rollbacks impossible, so it is only appropriate for prototyping. Committing migration files to version control ensures every environment goes through the same schema evolution steps in order.

children.title: Relations & the N+1 Problem
children.description: Prisma's include option eager-loads relations in a single query, but naively nesting includes for deeply nested relations generates costly JOINs. The N+1 problem occurs when you fetch a list of records and then query related data for each — Prisma's select combined with nested includes avoids this, but only if you understand which access patterns your queries are executing against the database.

children.title: Indexes & Query Planning
children.description: A missing index on a frequently filtered column causes PostgreSQL to perform a full sequential scan — on a table with millions of rows this turns a sub-millisecond query into a multi-second one that blocks concurrent writes. Using EXPLAIN ANALYZE to read query plans and adding B-tree or partial indexes where needed is not premature optimization, it is basic database hygiene.

children.title: Transactions & ACID Guarantees
children.description: A database transaction groups multiple statements into an atomic unit — if one step fails, the entire transaction rolls back and the database is left unchanged. Without transactions, a partial failure in a multi-step operation (deduct balance, create order, send notification) leaves your data in an inconsistent state that may require manual intervention to fix.

children.title: Database Normalization
children.description: Normalizing your schema (removing repeating groups and enforcing referential integrity via foreign keys) prevents data anomalies where updating one record leaves related records stale or inconsistent. Over-normalization with too many joins hurts read performance, so understanding when to denormalize for read-heavy workloads is equally important for production schemas.

children.title: Soft Deletes & Audit Trails
children.description: Hard-deleting records with DELETE is irreversible and breaks foreign key references — soft deletes (adding a deletedAt column and filtering it in every query) keep historical data intact for auditing and recovery. Prisma middleware can enforce soft-delete filtering globally so individual queries cannot accidentally expose deleted records.

node-dev.title: Authentication & Security
node-dev.description: Secure your Node API. Implement authentication, protect routes, and apply standard hardening practices.
node-dev.challenge: Add JWT auth with refresh tokens, role-based route guards, and rate limiting to your API.

children.title: JWT Structure & Verification
children.description: JWTs are signed tokens — verifying the signature confirms the token was issued by your server and has not been tampered with, but it does not confirm the token has not been revoked. Using the 'none' algorithm or skipping signature verification (a common misconfiguration) means any user can forge a token and gain access as any user in your system.

children.title: Refresh Token Rotation
children.description: Short-lived access tokens (15 minutes) limit the damage of a token theft, but they require refresh tokens to obtain new ones without re-authentication. Without refresh token rotation (invalidating the old refresh token on each use), a stolen refresh token can be used indefinitely — rotation forces the attacker's token to become invalid as soon as the legitimate user refreshes.

children.title: Password Hashing with bcrypt
children.description: bcrypt is specifically designed for password hashing — unlike SHA-256 or MD5 which are fast hash functions, bcrypt's configurable cost factor makes it intentionally slow, rendering brute-force attacks impractical. Using MD5 or SHA-1 for passwords means an attacker who steals your database can crack all passwords in hours on commodity GPU hardware.

children.title: Timing Attacks & Constant-Time Comparison
children.description: Normal string equality returns early as soon as it finds a mismatching character — comparing secret tokens with === leaks timing information that allows an attacker to guess tokens one character at a time. crypto.timingSafeEqual() compares every byte in constant time regardless of where the difference is, preventing this attack.

children.title: Role-Based Access Control
children.description: RBAC assigns permissions to roles rather than individual users — without it, adding a new permission requires updating every user record individually, creating drift between what users should be able to do and what they actually can. Checking roles in the database on each request rather than encoding them in the JWT ensures permission changes take effect immediately without token invalidation.

children.title: CORS & Security Headers
children.description: CORS misconfiguration with a wildcard origin ('*') combined with credentials:true is a browser security error that exposes authenticated API endpoints to cross-origin requests from any website. Helmet.js sets a suite of security headers (Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, HSTS) in a single plugin call — skipping it leaves your API open to clickjacking and MIME sniffing attacks.

children.title: Injection Attacks & Parameterized Queries
children.description: SQL injection is still the most exploited web vulnerability — concatenating user input directly into SQL strings allows attackers to delete tables, exfiltrate data, or bypass authentication entirely. Prisma's query builder uses parameterized queries by default, but using prisma.$queryRawUnsafe with interpolated input reintroduces the vulnerability.

children.title: Rate Limiting & DoS Prevention
children.description: An unauthenticated endpoint without rate limiting can be hammered by a bot to exhaust your database connection pool within seconds, taking down the entire service for legitimate users. Rate limiting at the application layer (fastify-rate-limit using Redis as a shared store) ensures limits are enforced consistently across all Node processes in a multi-instance deployment.

node-dev.title: Validation, Errors & Architecture
node-dev.description: Make your codebase robust and maintainable. Validate inputs, handle errors consistently, and structure layers cleanly.
node-dev.challenge: Refactor your API into route/service/repository layers with Zod validation and a global error handler.

children.title: Zod Schema Validation
children.description: Zod validates and parses untrusted input at the boundary of your application, ensuring that downstream code only ever sees data that matches the expected shape and types. Validating manually with if-statements is error-prone and inconsistent across endpoints — Zod's parse throws typed errors with precise field-level messages, making it trivial to return structured 400 responses.

children.title: Zod + TypeScript Type Inference
children.description: Zod can infer TypeScript types directly from schema definitions (z.infer<typeof schema>), eliminating the need to maintain separate interface definitions that can drift from runtime validation. Defining an interface separately from its Zod schema is redundant and fragile — the schema becomes the single source of truth for both runtime validation and static type checking.

children.title: Centralized Error Handler
children.description: Fastify's setErrorHandler catches all errors thrown or rejected in route handlers, giving you a single place to translate domain errors into HTTP responses. Without it, different routes return error shapes in different formats, API consumers cannot reliably parse errors, and uncaught errors leak internal stack traces to clients — a security vulnerability as well as an inconsistency.

children.title: Custom Error Classes
children.description: Using generic Error objects everywhere makes it impossible to distinguish a 'not found' from an 'unauthorized' error in the global error handler without string-matching error messages. Custom error classes (NotFoundError, UnauthorizedError) extending Error carry their HTTP status code and application error code, letting the handler produce correct responses without fragile message parsing.

children.title: Route → Service → Repository Pattern
children.description: Separating concerns into routes (HTTP), services (business logic), and repositories (data access) makes each layer independently testable — services can be unit tested without HTTP, repositories can be swapped for in-memory fakes in tests. Mixing database queries directly into route handlers couples transport logic to persistence, making the codebase impossible to test without a running database.

children.title: Dependency Injection Patterns
children.description: Hard-coding new DatabaseClient() inside service constructors makes services untestable and non-replaceable — dependency injection (passing dependencies in via constructor or fastify decorators) makes it trivial to swap real implementations for test doubles. Without DI, testing a payment service requires an actual payment gateway connected, slowing test suites from seconds to minutes.

children.title: Validated Config with env-schema
children.description: env-schema validates environment variables against a JSON Schema on startup and throws immediately if required vars are missing or malformed — without this, a misconfigured deployment starts successfully, only to fail on the first request that touches the unset variable. Failing fast at startup is far better than crashing mid-request after serving users.

children.title: Structured Logging with Pino
children.description: Pino outputs structured JSON logs that log aggregators (Datadog, Loki, CloudWatch) can parse, filter, and alert on — using console.log produces unstructured strings that are unsearchable in production. Pino is also the fastest Node.js logger because it delegates JSON serialization to a worker thread, adding negligible overhead even under high throughput.

node-dev.title: Testing
node-dev.description: Test your Node services with confidence. Cover units, integration, and full API flows.
node-dev.challenge: Write unit tests for services and Supertest integration tests covering auth and CRUD flows.

children.title: Vitest Fundamentals & Test Structure
children.description: Vitest is a Vite-native test runner with a Jest-compatible API that supports TypeScript out of the box without additional transpilation setup. Skipping unit tests for service logic means every bug is discovered in integration or in production — unit tests isolate a single function and verify it handles edge cases (empty input, null, over-limit values) that integration tests rarely exercise.

children.title: Mocking with vi.mock & vi.spyOn
children.description: Mocking replaces real dependencies (database clients, email services, external APIs) with controlled fakes during tests — without mocking, a single unit test that calls the real database is slow, fragile, and pollutes test data. vi.spyOn lets you assert that a function was called with specific arguments, which is essential for verifying side effects like email sends without actually sending emails.

children.title: API Testing with Supertest & Fastify inject()
children.description: Fastify's built-in inject() method fires requests against the server without binding to a real port — this makes integration tests fast, parallel-safe, and free of port conflicts in CI. Testing only units without integration tests misses entire classes of bugs: auth middleware not applied, wrong route registered, plugin loading order errors.

children.title: Test Databases & Fixtures
children.description: Running integration tests against a shared development database causes tests to fail non-deterministically based on whatever data happens to exist — each test suite should own an isolated database that is seeded and torn down per run. Using Docker Compose to spin up a PostgreSQL container in CI ensures tests always run against a clean, version-controlled schema.

children.title: Coverage with V8 & Istanbul
children.description: Code coverage measures which lines, branches, and functions are exercised by tests — a 30% coverage score with no branch coverage is nearly meaningless, because untested branches are exactly where edge-case bugs hide. Vitest's built-in V8 coverage provider generates coverage reports with zero additional dependencies, and adding a minimum threshold to CI prevents coverage from silently degrading over time.

children.title: Testing in CI with GitHub Actions
children.description: Automated tests in CI catch regressions before they reach production — a test suite that only runs locally on a developer's machine provides no protection against broken merges. GitHub Actions matrices let you run tests against multiple Node.js versions simultaneously, ensuring you do not accidentally ship code that only works on the version installed on your laptop.

children.title: Arrange-Act-Assert Pattern
children.description: The AAA pattern structures each test into three clear phases: arrange (set up state and mocks), act (call the function under test), assert (verify the outcome). Tests that interleave these phases or have multiple unrelated assertions become hard to read and produce cryptic failure messages that do not identify which behavior broke.

children.title: Contract Testing
children.description: Contract tests verify that the actual API responses match the schema your clients expect — without them, a backend team can change a response field name and not discover the breakage until a frontend deploy or a mobile app update fails in production. Using Zod schemas as both runtime validators and test contracts ensures producer and consumer stay in sync.

resources.label: Fastify — JSON Schema for response validation

node-dev.title: Performance, Caching & Queues
node-dev.description: Scale your Node app. Cache hot data, offload heavy work to background jobs, and tune performance.
node-dev.challenge: Add Redis caching to hot endpoints and move report generation to a BullMQ background queue.

children.title: Cache-Aside Pattern with Redis
children.description: Cache-aside (lazy loading) checks Redis before hitting the database and populates the cache on misses — without a TTL (time-to-live) on cached keys, stale data is served indefinitely after the underlying record is updated. Caching without a cache invalidation strategy is worse than no caching because it causes hard-to-reproduce bugs where users see outdated data that disappears after a cache flush.

children.title: Redis Data Structures
children.description: Redis is not just a key-value store — its Sorted Sets, Hashes, Lists, and HyperLogLog structures enable leaderboards, rate limiters, pub/sub messaging, and cardinality counting at memory-efficient scale. Using plain string keys for every use case means implementing sorted lists in application code with repeated HGETALL calls, where a single ZADD/ZRANGE would be both faster and atomic.

children.title: Job Queues & Workers
children.description: BullMQ separates job producers (API handlers that enqueue work) from consumers (workers that process it), making your API responses instant while heavy work (PDF generation, email sending, data exports) runs asynchronously. Without queues, an API endpoint that triggers a 30-second operation holds the HTTP connection open, exhausting your server's request concurrency under any real load.

children.title: Job Retries & Dead-Letter Queues
children.description: BullMQ retries failed jobs with configurable backoff strategies — without retries, a transient network error to an external API permanently fails jobs that would have succeeded on the next attempt. Jobs that exceed their retry limit should be moved to a dead-letter queue for inspection, not silently discarded, because silent job loss is nearly impossible to detect without explicit monitoring.

children.title: Profiling with --prof & Clinic.js
children.description: Node's built-in --prof flag generates a V8 profiler trace that shows which functions are consuming CPU time — without profiling, performance optimizations are guesswork that often improve the wrong code paths. Clinic.js wraps profiling tools into a simple CLI that produces flame graphs and identifies event loop delays, I/O bottlenecks, and memory growth patterns visually.

children.title: Detecting & Fixing Memory Leaks
children.description: Memory leaks in Node.js accumulate until the process is killed by OOM, but they are invisible in normal operation — the heap grows so slowly that it only becomes critical after days of uptime. Taking periodic heap snapshots with --heap-prof and comparing them with Chrome DevTools reveals which objects are being retained when they should have been garbage collected.

children.title: Database Connection Pool Tuning
children.description: Prisma's connection pool size defaults to num_cpus * 2 + 1, which is often too high for serverless environments and too low for high-throughput services — an oversized pool exhausts PostgreSQL's max_connections while an undersized one creates queue backlog. Monitoring pool metrics (wait time, active connections) and tuning the connection_limit is essential before a production traffic spike.

children.title: Redis Connection Management
children.description: Creating a new Redis client per request creates a new TCP connection for every operation — TCP connection setup (SYN-SYN/ACK-ACK) adds 1–5ms per request, which compounds to significant overhead on high-traffic APIs. A single shared ioredis client with connection pooling configured reuses connections and handles reconnection automatically on network failures.

node-dev.title: Observability & Deployment
node-dev.description: Ship and operate your Node service. Add logging, metrics and tracing, containerize, and automate deployment.
node-dev.challenge: Add structured logging and tracing, containerize the API, and deploy it via a CI pipeline.

children.title: Distributed Tracing with OpenTelemetry
children.description: OpenTelemetry traces a request's journey across services by propagating a trace context (trace ID, span ID) in HTTP headers — without it, debugging a slow request in a microservices architecture means manually correlating logs from three different services by timestamp, which is imprecise and slow. Auto-instrumentation for Fastify, Prisma, and Redis generates spans automatically with zero code changes.

children.title: Metrics & Health Checks
children.description: Kubernetes and load balancers need a /health endpoint to know whether a pod is ready to receive traffic — without it, a pod with a broken database connection continues receiving requests that all fail. Prometheus-format metrics (request rate, error rate, response time percentiles, queue depth) give you the data needed to alert on anomalies before customers notice.

children.title: Dockerizing a Node.js App
children.description: A Docker image packages your application and its runtime into a reproducible unit that runs identically in dev, CI, and production — without it, 'works on my machine' bugs caused by Node version differences, missing native modules, or OS-specific path handling reach production. Using a multi-stage Dockerfile separates build-time dependencies from the final image, reducing image size from gigabytes to hundreds of megabytes.

children.title: Non-Root Users & Docker Security
children.description: Running Node as the root user inside a container means a compromised process has full container privileges — if the container runtime has a vulnerability, a root container can potentially escape to the host. Adding a non-root user in your Dockerfile and switching to it before the CMD instruction is a one-line change that significantly reduces the blast radius of a supply-chain or RCE attack.

children.title: GitHub Actions for Node.js
children.description: A CI pipeline that runs on every push catches broken builds and failing tests before they reach main — without it, developers manually run tests (or forget to) and broken code accumulates on branches until a deployment fails. Using GitHub Actions' cache action for node_modules cuts pipeline install time from 3 minutes to 15 seconds on repeat runs.

resources.label: GitHub Actions — Building and testing Node.js

children.title: Container Registry & Image Tagging
children.description: Tagging Docker images with the git commit SHA (not just 'latest') means every deployed version is traceable back to the exact commit that produced it — deploying 'latest' makes rollbacks impossible because the tag is overwritten on every build. Pushing images to a private registry (GHCR, ECR) rather than Docker Hub avoids rate limiting and keeps proprietary code private.

children.title: Graceful Shutdown & SIGTERM Handling
children.description: When a Kubernetes pod is terminated or a deployment is rolled out, the process receives SIGTERM and has a grace period (typically 30 seconds) to finish in-flight requests before being killed — ignoring SIGTERM means requests are cut off mid-flight and BullMQ jobs are abandoned mid-execution. Fastify's fastify.close() drains connections, closes the database pool, and triggers onClose hooks in the correct order.

children.title: Environment-Specific Configuration
children.description: Production deployments require different configuration from development — different database URLs, stricter CORS policies, production log levels, and disabled debug endpoints. Using a single config file with environment-based branches keeps configuration centralized while ensuring each deployment gets exactly the settings appropriate for its context, preventing development credentials from leaking into production.
